Policy: Security groups that allow access from all external sources(0.0.0.0/0).
Description: Allowing Inbound traffic from any IPv4 address - 0.0.0.0/0 can lead to attacks like DoS, Brute Force, Smurf, and reconnaissance. It is a best practice to restrict inbound traffic from the required source IP address(es).
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose EC2 or VPC Service. In the Navigation pane, choose "Security Groups". Select the security group to edit, click on the "Inbound" tab and click Edit. Replace the rule with Source as 0.0.0.0/0 with specific IP address or delete the rule
CQL: SecurityGroup should not have inboundRules with [sourceIPv4Ranges with [cidr='0.0.0.0/0']]

Policy: Security groups that allow access to all external sources(0.0.0.0/0).
Description: Allowing Outbound traffic to any IPv4 address - 0.0.0.0/0 can lead to internal resources accessing unwanted and untrusted resources. If a system is compromised, an attacker can use it to exfiltrate data or conduct spam or phishing campaigns. It is a best practice to restrict outbound traffic to only required IP address(es).
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose EC2 or VPC Service. In the Navigation pane, choose "Security Groups". Select the security group to edit, click on the "Outbound" tab and click Edit. Replace the rule with Destination as 0.0.0.0/0 with specific IP address or delete the rule
CQL: SecurityGroup should not have outboundRules with [destIPv4Ranges with [cidr='0.0.0.0/0']]

Policy: Security groups that allow access to entire port range(0-65535) from external sources.
Description: Allowing Inbound traffic on ALL ports increases the attack surface of your environment. It is a best practice to follow the principle of least privilege, and grant access on only required ports.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose EC2 or VPC Service. In the Navigation pane, choose "Security Groups". Select the security group to edit, click on the "Inbound" tab and click Edit. Add the new rule with specific ports, and delete the rule granting access to port range 0-65535
CQL: SecurityGroup should not have inboundRules with [fromPort=0 and toPort=65535]

Policy: Security groups that allow access to entire port range(0-65535) of external sources.
Description: Allowing Outbound traffic to ALL ports can lead to internal resources accessing unwanted and untrusted resources. It is a best practice to follow the principle of least privilege, and grant access to only required ports.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose EC2 or VPC Service. In the Navigation pane, choose "Security Groups". Select the security group to edit, click on the "Outbound" tab and click Edit. Add the new rule with specific ports, and delete the rule granting access to port range 0-65535
CQL: SecurityGroup should not have outboundRules with [fromPort=0 and toPort=65535]

Policy: EC2 instances that allow access to port 22 from external sources.
Description: SSH (Secure Shell) port - 22 is used to get CLI access to Linux Instances. Allowing access from all IP addresses on the Internet to SSH port is vulnerable to "banner grabbing" and "brute force" attacks. As a best practice, it is recommended to restrict access from specific IP addresses to port 22.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose EC2 or VPC Service. In the Navigation pane, choose "Security Groups". Select the security group to edit, click on the "Inbound" tab and click Edit. Replace the rule with Source as 0.0.0.0/0 with specific IP address or delete the rule
CQL: EC2Instance where isPublic=true should not have publicInboundRules with [port=22 and protocol in ('tcp', 'udp')]

Policy: EC2 instances that allow access to port 3389 from external sources.
Description: RDP (Remote Desktop Protocol) port - 3389 is used to get remote control access to Windows Instances. Allowing access from all IP addresses on the Internet to RDP port is vulnerable to “remote code execution”, “privilege elevation” and “flooding attacks”. It is a best practice to restrict access from specific  IP addresses to port 3389.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose EC2 or VPC Service. In the Navigation pane, choose "Security Groups". Select the security group to edit, click on the "Inbound" tab and click Edit. Replace the rule with Source as 0.0.0.0/0 with specific IP address or delete the rule
CQL: EC2Instance where isPublic=true should not have publicInboundRules with [port=3389 and protocol in ('tcp', 'udp')]

Policy: EC2 instances that allow access to port 20 from external sources.
Description: FTP (File Transfer Protocol) port - 20 is used to setup FTP connection between the client and the server. Allowing access from all IP addresses on the Internet to FTP port makes it vulnerable to “FTP brute force attacks”, “Packet Sniffing” using  Man-In-The-Middle (MITM) attacks. It is a best practice to restrict access from specific IP addresses to port 20.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose EC2 or VPC Service. In the Navigation pane, choose "Security Groups". Select the security group to edit, click on the "Inbound" tab and click Edit. Replace the rule with Source as 0.0.0.0/0 with specific IP address or delete the rule
CQL: EC2Instance where isPublic=true should not have publicInboundRules with [port=20 and protocol in ('tcp', 'udp')]

Policy: EC2 instances that allow access to port 21 from external sources.
Description: FTP (File Transfer Protocol) port - 21 is used to transfer data between the client and the server. Allowing access from all IP addresses on the Internet to FTP port makes it vulnerable to “FTP brute force attacks”, “Packet Sniffing” using  Man-In-The-Middle (MITM) attacks. It is a best practice to restrict access from specific IP addresses to port 21.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose EC2 or VPC Service. In the Navigation pane, choose "Security Groups". Select the security group to edit, click on the "Inbound" tab and click Edit. Replace the rule with Source as 0.0.0.0/0 with specific IP address or delete the rule
CQL: EC2Instance where isPublic=true should not have publicInboundRules with [port=21 and protocol in ('tcp', 'udp')]

Policy: EC2 instances that allow access to port 23 from external sources.
Description: TELNET port - 23 is used to gain CLI access to the machines. Allowing access from all IP addresses on the Internet to TELNET port is vulnerable to “communication sniffing”, “brute force" & "DoS attacks”. It is a best practice to restrict access from specific IP addresses to port 23.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose EC2 or VPC Service. In the Navigation pane, choose "Security Groups". Select the security group to edit, click on the "Inbound" tab and click Edit. Replace the rule with Source as 0.0.0.0/0 with specific IP address or delete the rule
CQL: EC2Instance where isPublic=true should not have publicInboundRules with [port=23 and protocol in ('tcp', 'udp')]

Policy: EC2 instances that allow access to port 25 from external sources.
Description: SMTP port - 25 is used to send/receive emails. Allowing access from all IP addresses on the Internet to TCP Port 25 can be vulnerable to DoS and reconnaissance attacks. It is a best practice to restrict access from specific IP addresses to port 25.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose EC2 or VPC Service. In the Navigation pane, choose "Security Groups". Select the security group to edit, click on the "Inbound" tab and click Edit. Replace the rule with Source as 0.0.0.0/0 with specific IP address or delete the rule
CQL: EC2Instance where isPublic=true should not have publicInboundRules with [port=25 and protocol in ('tcp', 'udp')]

Policy: EC2 instances that allow access to port 53 from external sources.
Description: DNS port - 53 is used to query Domain Names to IP address. Allowing access from all IP addresses on the Internet to DNS port can be vulnerable to DNS Hijacking, Cache Poisoning, DNS Tunnelling attack. It is a best practice to restrict access from known public DNS server to destination port 53.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose EC2 or VPC Service. In the Navigation pane, choose "Security Groups". Select the security group to edit, click on the "Inbound" tab and click Edit. Replace the rule with Source as 0.0.0.0/0 with specific IP address or delete the rule
CQL: EC2Instance where isPublic=true should not have publicInboundRules with [port=53 and protocol in ('tcp', 'udp')]

Policy: EC2 instances that allow access to port 135 from external sources.
Description: Remote Procedure Call (RPC) protocol provides an inter-process communication mechanism that allows a program running on one computer to seamlessly execute code on another remote system. Using RPC protocol an attacker can take any action on your system, like installing programs, viewing, changing or deleting data, or creating new accounts with full privileges. It is a best practice to block RPC port 135 from the public internet.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose EC2 or VPC Service. In the Navigation pane, choose "Security Groups". Select the security group to edit, click on the "Inbound" tab and click Edit. Delete the rule with Source as 0.0.0.0/0
CQL: EC2Instance where isPublic=true should not have publicInboundRules with [port=135 and protocol in ('tcp', 'udp')]

Policy: EC2 instances that allow access to port 137 from external sources.
Description: NetBIOS port 137 is used for Network file sharing and shared printer in the LAN. Allowing access from all IP addresses on the Internet to NetBIOS ports can be risky as your shared resources are exposed over the public Internet. It is a best practice to block port 139 from the open Internet.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose EC2 or VPC Service. In the Navigation pane, choose "Security Groups". Select the security group to edit, click on the "Inbound" tab and click Edit. Delete the rule with Source as 0.0.0.0/0
CQL: EC2Instance where isPublic=true should not have publicInboundRules with [port=137 and protocol in ('tcp', 'udp')]

Policy: EC2 instances that allow access to port 138 from external sources.
Description: NetBIOS port 138 is used for Network file sharing and shared printer in the LAN. Allowing access from all IP addresses on the Internet to NetBIOS ports can be risky as your shared resources are exposed over the public Internet. It is a best practice to block port 139 from the open Internet.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose EC2 or VPC Service. In the Navigation pane, choose "Security Groups". Select the security group to edit, click on the "Inbound" tab and click Edit. Delete the rule with Source as 0.0.0.0/0
CQL: EC2Instance where isPublic=true should not have publicInboundRules with [port=138 and protocol in ('tcp', 'udp')]

Policy: EC2 instances that allow access to port 139 from external sources.
Description: NetBIOS port 139 is used for Network file sharing and shared printer in the LAN. Allowing access from all IP addresses on the Internet to NetBIOS ports can be risky as your shared resources are exposed over the public Internet. It is a best practice to block port 139 from the open Internet.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose EC2 or VPC Service. In the Navigation pane, choose "Security Groups". Select the security group to edit, click on the "Inbound" tab and click Edit. Delete the rule with Source as 0.0.0.0/0
CQL: EC2Instance where isPublic=true should not have publicInboundRules with [port=139 and protocol in ('tcp', 'udp')]

Policy: EC2 instances that allow access to port 1433 from external sources.
Description: MSSQL server port - 1433 is used by SQL server to listen for requests from SQL server clients. Allowing access from all IP addresses on the Internet to MSSQL port is vulnerable to DoS, Buffer Overflow, SQL Injection attacks. It is a best practice to block public access, and restrict access from specific IP addresses to port 1433.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose EC2 or VPC Service. In the Navigation pane, choose "Security Groups". Select the security group to edit, click on the "Inbound" tab and click Edit. Replace the rule with Source as 0.0.0.0/0 with specific IP address or delete the rule
CQL: EC2Instance where isPublic=true should not have publicInboundRules with [port=1433 and protocol in ('tcp', 'udp')]

Policy: EC2 instances that allow access to port 1434 from external sources.
Description: MSSQL port - 1434 is used by SQL server to listen for requests from SQL server clients. Allowing access from all IP addresses on the Internet to MSSQL port is vulnerable to DoS, Buffer Overflow, SQL Injection attacks. It is a best practice to block public access, and restrict access from specific IP addresses to port 1434.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose EC2 or VPC Service. In the Navigation pane, choose "Security Groups". Select the security group to edit, click on the "Inbound" tab and click Edit. Replace the rule with Source as 0.0.0.0/0 with specific IP address or delete the rule
CQL: EC2Instance where isPublic=true should not have publicInboundRules with [port=1434 and protocol in ('tcp', 'udp')]

Policy: EC2 instances that allow access to port 3306 from external sources.
Description: MySQL port - 3306 is used by MySQL server to listen requests from clients. Allowing access from all IP addresses on the Internet to MySQL port is vulnerable to DoS, Buffer Overflow, SQL Injection attacks. It is a best practice to block public access, and restrict access from specific IP addresses to port 3306.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose EC2 or VPC Service. In the Navigation pane, choose "Security Groups". Select the security group to edit, click on the "Inbound" tab and click Edit. Replace the rule with Source as 0.0.0.0/0 with specific IP address or delete the rule
CQL: EC2Instance where isPublic=true should not have publicInboundRules with [port=3306 and protocol in ('tcp', 'udp')]

Policy: EC2 instances that allow access to port 1521 from external sources.
Description: Oracle TNS(Transparent Network Substrate) port - 1521 is used by Oracle client to connect to the database server over Oracle SQL Net protocol. Allowing access from all IP addresses on the Internet to TNS port will let commands to be sent to the listener, the listener can be shut down, or the databases can be queried. It is a best practice to block public access, and restrict access from specific IP addresses to port 1521.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose EC2 or VPC Service. In the Navigation pane, choose "Security Groups". Select the security group to edit, click on the "Inbound" tab and click Edit. Replace the rule with Source as 0.0.0.0/0 with specific IP address or delete the rule
CQL: EC2Instance where isPublic=true should not have publicInboundRules with [port=1521 and protocol in ('tcp', 'udp')]

Policy: EC2 instances that allow access to port 5432 from external sources.
Description: PostgreSQL - 5432 is used by SQL server to listen for client requests. Allowing access from all IP addresses on the Internet to PostgreSQL port is vulnerable to postgresql exploit. It is a best practice to block public access, and restrict access from specific IP addresses to port 5432.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose EC2 or VPC Service. In the Navigation pane, choose "Security Groups". Select the security group to edit, click on the "Inbound" tab and click Edit. Replace the rule with Source as 0.0.0.0/0 with specific IP address or delete the rule
CQL: EC2Instance where isPublic=true should not have publicInboundRules with [port=5432 and protocol in ('tcp', 'udp')]

Policy: EC2 instances that allow access to port 445 from external sources.
Description: SMB (Server Message Block) Port - 445 is used for file sharing and TCP/IP networking on Windows 2000 and later versions. Allowing access from all IP addresses on the Internet to port 445 can make it vulnerable to WannaCry exploit on Microsoft Endpoints. It is a best practice to block port 445 from the public internet.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose EC2 or VPC Service. In the Navigation pane, choose "Security Groups". Select the security group to edit, click on the "Inbound" tab and click Edit. Delete the rule with Source as 0.0.0.0/0
CQL: EC2Instance where isPublic=true and platform='Windows' should not have publicInboundRules with [port=445 and protocol in ('tcp','udp')]

Policy: Default security groups associated with atleast one or more ingress or egress rules.
Description: In AWS, the default security group comes with pre-defined settings that denies all inbound traffic, allows all outbound traffic and allows traffic between instances associated to the default security group. It is a best practice to remove all the rules in the default security group, thereby enforcing and encouraging creation of  least privilege security groups as the need arises.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose EC2 or VPC Service. In the Navigation pane, choose "Security Groups". Select each default security group (across different regions) and delete the inbound and outbound rules
CQL: SecurityGroup where groupName='default' should have inboundRules isEmpty() and outboundRules isEmpty()

Policy: EC2 instances associated with default security group.
Description: In AWS, when an EC2 Instance is launched without configuring a security group, it will automatically associate with the default security group. The default security group allows all inbound traffic from other instances associated with the same. This can enable access to other EC2 Instances which are not in your control. It is a best practice to create custom security groups using the principle of least privilege and associate with the EC2 Instances.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose EC2. In the Navigation pane, choose "Instances". Select the EC2 instance associated with default security group and associate to a different security group. Remove the association with default security group
CQL: EC2Instance should not have defaultSecurityGroupAssociated=true

Policy: EC2 instances launched in default VPC.
Description: Every AWS account comes with default Virtual Private Cloud in each region. A default VPC is suitable for getting started quickly, and for launching public instances for simple websites. But, if you need to host a complex multi-tier application or add more layers of security to your infrastructure it is a best practice to create non-default VPC with public, private subnets & demilitarized (DMZ) zones. This segregates the network based on their functionality, services, and security.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose EC2. Select the EC2 instance reported under violation. Stop the Instance, Create an AMI of the instance. Launch new EC2 instance using the AMI created and place it in the non-default VPC
CQL: EC2Instance should not have defaultVpcAssociated=true

Policy: S3 buckets allowing un-authenticated public READ access through ACL.
Description: Bucket ACLs (Access Control List) are resource based policies that grant access to other aws accounts, users and log group. This policy checks whether S3 bucket is configured to grant READ access to aws predefined group http://acs.amazonaws.com/groups/global/AllUsers. This empowers any user on the internet with the list access on s3 bucket objects. It is a best practice to grant limited access to specific authenticated users.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose S3. Select the S3 bucket reported under violation. Click on "Permissions" tab, select "Access Control List". Under Public Access click "Everyone", uncheck "List Objects" and save the configurations. Or use AWS CLI to remove "READ" access to predefined group "http://acs.amazonaws.com/groups/global/AllUser"
CQL: S3Bucket should not have grants with [uri='http://acs.amazonaws.com/groups/global/AllUsers' and permission='READ']

Policy: S3 buckets allowing access to all users through bucket policy.
Description: Bucket Policy governs the access permissions on AWS S3 resources. This policy checks whether S3 bucket is configured with bucket policy with "Effect":"Allow", "Principal":"*" and with any combination of allowed actions. This enables bucket to be accessed by anyone, and if proper conditions are not added it could be exposed to the internet. It is a best practice to grant limited access to specific authenticated users.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose S3. Select the S3 bucket reported under violation. Click on "Permissions" tab and select "Bucket Policy". Replace "*" under "Principal" with a specific user, role, or service
CQL: S3Bucket should not have policyStatement with [(principal='*' or principalAWS contain-any ('*')) and effect='Allow']

Policy: S3 buckets allowing authenticated public READ access through ACL.
Description: Bucket ACLs (Access Control List) are resource based policies that grant access to other aws accounts, users and log group. This policy checks whether S3 bucket is configured to grant READ access to aws predefined group http://acs.amazonaws.com/groups/global/AuthenticatedUsers. This empowers any aws authenticated user with the list access on s3 bucket objects. It is a best practice to grant limited access to specific authenticated users.
Recommendation: This can be fixed only through CLI or AWS SDK APIs. Use AWS CLI to remove "READ" access to predefined group "http://acs.amazonaws.com/groups/global/AuthenticatedUsers"
CQL: S3Bucket should not have grants with [uri='http://acs.amazonaws.com/groups/global/AuthenticatedUsers' and permission='READ']

Policy: S3 buckets allowing access to all users to perform all actions(*) through bucket policy.
Description: Bucket Policy governs the access permissions on AWS S3 resources. This policy checks whether S3 bucket is configured with bucket policy with "Effect":"Allow", "Principal":"*" and "Action":"*". This enables bucket to be accessed by anyone, and if proper conditions are not added it could be exposed to the internet. It is a best practice to grant limited access to specific authenticated users
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose S3. Select the S3 bucket reported under violation. Click on "Permissions" tab and select "Bucket Policy". Replace "*" under "Principal" with a specific user, role, or service and replace "Action":"*" with specific action
CQL: S3Bucket should not have policyStatement with [(principal='*' or principalAWS contain-any ('*')) and effect='Allow' and action contain-any ('*','s3:*')]

Policy: S3 buckets that do not have encryption enabled.
Description: AWS provides an option to set default encryption on S3 buckets. The default encryption on bucket ensures that all the objects stored in the bucket are encrypted. The objects are encrypted using server-side encryption with either Amazon S3-managed keys (SSE-S3) or AWS KMS-managed keys (SSE-KMS). It is a best practice to ensure encryption is set on S3 buckets.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose S3. Select the S3 bucket reported under violation. Click on "Properties" tab, select either AES-256 or AWS-KMS (select the keys from the list) and Save
CQL: S3Bucket should have encryption isNotEmpty()

Policy: S3 buckets that do not have access logging enabled.
Description: AWS provides an option to enable server side access logging on S3 buckets. Server side access logs provide detailed records of the requests that are made to S3 bucket. These logs are useful in security & access audits. It is a best practice to enable server side logging on S3 buckets.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose S3. Select the S3 bucket reported under violation. Click on "Properties" tab, select "Server access logging", select "Enable logging" and configure the required parameters
CQL: S3Bucket should have loggingEnabled isNotEmpty()

Policy: S3 buckets allowing full control access to authenticated users through ACL.
Description: Bucket ACLs (Access Control List) are resource based policies that grant access to other aws accounts, users and log group. This policy checks whether S3 bucket is configured to grant FULL_CONTROL access to aws predefined group http://acs.amazonaws.com/groups/global/AuthenticatedUsers. This empowers any aws authenticated user with the full access on s3 bucket objects and permissions. It is a best practice to grant limited access to specific authenticated users.
Recommendation: This can be fixed only through CLI or AWS SDK APIs. Use AWS CLI to remove access to predefined group "http://acs.amazonaws.com/groups/global/AuthenticatedUsers", and grant required access to only specific authenticated users
CQL: S3Bucket should not have grants with [uri='http://acs.amazonaws.com/groups/global/AuthenticatedUsers' and permission='FULL_CONTROL']

Policy: AWS accounts that do not have public access block settings to block new public ACLs and uploading public objects.
Description: S3 buckets can be used to store sensitive and confidential information in an organization. So it is always advised to protect them from public access to avoid any data breaches. As a best practice enable S3 bucket public access setting  "Block new public ACLs and uploading public objects". This prevents any accidental exposure of S3 buckets to the public.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose S3. Select "Public access settings for this account". Click on "Edit" button, enable a setting option "Block new public ACLs and uploading public objects" and save the configurations
CQL: Account should have pabBlockPublicAcls=true

Policy: S3 buckets allowing full control access to un-authenticated users through ACL.
Description: Bucket ACLs (Access Control List) are resource based policies that grant access to other aws accounts, users and log group. This policy checks whether S3 bucket is configured to grant FULL_CONTROL access to aws predefined group http://acs.amazonaws.com/groups/global/AllUsers. This empowers any user on the internet with the full access on s3 bucket objects and permissions. It is a best practice to grant limited access to specific authenticated users.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose S3. Select the S3 bucket reported under violation. Click on "Permissions" tab, select "Access Control List". Under Public Access click "Everyone", uncheck "List Objects", "Write Objects", "Read bucket permissions" & "Write bucket permissions" and save the configurations
CQL: S3Bucket should not have grants with [uri='http://acs.amazonaws.com/groups/global/AllUsers' and permission='FULL_CONTROL']

Policy: AWS accounts that do not have public access block settings to ignore public access granted through public ACLs.
Description: S3 buckets can be used to store sensitive and confidential information in an organization. So it is always advised to protect them from public access to avoid any data breaches. As a best practice enable S3 bucket public access setting  "Remove public access granted through public ACLs". This prevents any accidental exposure of S3 buckets to the public.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose S3. Select "Public access settings for this account". Click on "Edit" button, enable a setting option "Remove public access granted through public ACLs" and save the configurations
CQL: Account should have pabIgnorePublicAcls=true

Policy: AWS accounts that have root users with multi-factor authentication disabled.
Description: The root account is the most privileged user in an AWS account. MFA adds an extra layer of protection on top of a username and password. With MFA enabled, when a root user signs into AWS, they will be prompted for their user name and password as well as an authentication code from their AWS MFA device.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/ using root credentials. Go to "My Security Credentials" from drop-down menu next to account name on top right corner. If a pop-up opens select "Continue to Security Credentials". On "Your Security Credentials" page select "Multi-factor authentication (MFA)". Click on "Activate MFA" and Follow the instructions on "Manage MFA Device" wizard to setup "Other hardware MFA" device
CQL: Account should have rootAccountMFAEnabled=true

Policy: AWS accounts that have root users with access keys configured(including inactive ones).
Description: AWS provides access keys (access key ID and secret access key) to programmatically access resources in an account. The root user has unrestricted access to all resources in an account. It is a best practice to delete the access keys associated with the root user.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/ using root credentials. Go to "My Security Credentials" from drop-down menu next to account name on top right corner. If a pop-up opens select "Continue to Security Credentials". On "Your Security Credentials" page select "Access keys (access key ID and secret access key)". Click on "Make Inactive" or Delete keys
CQL: Account should have rootAccountAccessKeyCount=0

Policy: AWS accounts without password policy configured.
Description: Password policies are, in part, used to enforce password complexity requirements. IAM password policies can be used to ensure the password is comprised of different character sets. It is recommended that the password policy is enabled for AWS account.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose IAM service. Click on "Account settings" on the left navigation pane. Make sure all the available options are selected, and password policy is applied
CQL: Account should have passwordPolicy isNotEmpty()

Policy: AWS accounts without password policy restriction of atleast one lowercase character configured.
Description: Password policies are, in part, used to enforce password complexity requirements. IAM password policies can be used to ensure the password is comprised of different character sets. It is recommended that the password policy require at least one lower case letter.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose IAM service. Click on "Account settings" on the left navigation pane. Select "Require at least one lowercase letter". Click on "Apply password policy"
CQL: Account should have passwordsWithLowercaseLetter=true

Policy: AWS accounts without passsword policy policy restriction of minimum password length configured.
Description: Password policies are, in part, used to enforce password complexity requirements. IAM password policies can be used to ensure password are at least a given length. It is recommended that the password policy require a minimum password length 14.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose IAM service. Click on "Account settings" on the left navigation pane. Make sure "Minimum password length" is set to 14 characters. Click on "Apply password policy"
CQL: Account should have passwordsWithMinimumLength>=14

Policy: AWS accounts without password policy restriction of password expiry configured.
Description: IAM password policies can require passwords to be rotated or expired after a given number of days. It is recommended to set password expiry after 90 days or less.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose IAM service. Click on "Account settings" on the left navigation pane. Select "Enable password expiration" and set "Password expiration period (in days)" is set to 90 days. Click on "Apply password policy"
CQL: Account should have maximumPasswordAge<=90

Policy: AWS accounts without password policy of allowing users to change their passwords.
Description: Password policies are, in part, used to enforce password complexity requirements. IAM password policies can be used to ensure users have complete control on changing their passwords. It is recommended that the password policy allow users to change their passwords.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose IAM service. Click on "Account settings" on the left navigation pane. Select "Allow users to change their own password". Click on "Apply password policy"
CQL: Account should have allowUsersToChangePassword=true

Policy: IAM users without any policies associated directly(inline or attached) or indirectly through groups.
Description: IAM users are created to perform any Console, CLI or API based operations on AWS cloud accounts. They are associated with policies that grant them permissions to perform required operations. An IAM user without any permission is a security risk, it is recommended to either add required permissions or delete them to adhere to compliance standards.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose IAM service. Click "Users" on left navigation, and select the user reported as non-compliant. Select "Groups" tab, click "Add user to groups" button. Select appropriate group with the policies already associated, and click "Add to Groups"
CQL: IAMUser should not have attachedPolicies isEmpty() and inlinePolicies isEmpty() and hasAttachedGroupPolicy=false

Policy: AWS accounts without password policy restriction not using previous passwords.
Description: IAM password policies can prevent the reuse of passwords by the same user. It is recommended that the password policy prevent the reuse of the last 14 passwords.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose IAM service. Click on "Account settings" on the left navigation pane. Select "Prevent password reuse" and set "Number of passwords to remember" to 14. Click on "Apply password policy"
CQL: Account should have countPreventPreviousPasswordReuse>=14

Policy: AWS accounts without password policy restriction of atleast one numeric character configured.
Description: Password policies are, in part, used to enforce password complexity requirements. IAM password policies can be used to ensure the password is comprised of different character sets. It is recommended that the password policy require at least one number.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose IAM service. Click on "Account settings" on the left navigation pane. Select "Require at least one number". Click on "Apply password policy"
CQL: Account should have passwordsWithNumber=true

Policy: AWS accounts without password policy restriction of atleast one special character configured.
Description: Password policies are, in part, used to enforce password complexity requirements. IAM password policies can be used to ensure the password is comprised of different character sets. It is recommended that the password policy require at least one symbol.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose IAM service. Click on "Account settings" on the left navigation pane. Select "Require at least one non-alphanumeric character". Click on "Apply password policy"
CQL: Account should have passwordsWithSpecialCharacter=true

Policy: AWS accounts without password policy restriction of atleast one uppercase character configured.
Description: Password policies are, in part, used to enforce password complexity requirements. IAM password policies can be used to ensure the password is comprised of different character sets. It is recommended that the password policy require at least one uppercase letter.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose IAM service. Click on "Account settings" on the left navigation pane. Select "Require at least one uppercase letter". Click on "Apply password policy"
CQL: Account should have passwordsWithUppercaseLetter=true

Policy: IAM users that have access keys not rotated for more than 90 days
Description: Access keys consist of an access key ID and secret access key, which are used to sign programmatic requests that you make to AWS. Access keys should be rotated to ensure that data cannot be accessed with an old key which might have been lost, cracked, or stolen.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose IAM service. Click on "Users" and select the user reported as non-compliant. Click "Security credentials" tab. Click on "Make inactive" or Delete keys. Click on Create Access Key. Update programmatic call with new Access Key credentials

Policy: IAM users with console access that do not have MFA configured
Description: Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) adds an extra layer of protection on top of a username and password. Enabling MFA provides increased security for console access as it requires the authenticating principal to possess a device that emits a time-sensitive key and have knowledge of a credential
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose IAM service. Click on "Users" and select the user reported as non-compliant. Click "Security credentials" tab. Click on "Manage" next to "Assigned MFA device" heading. Follow the instructions in Manage MFA Device wizard to setup MFA of your choice
CQL: IAMUser should not have passwordEnabled=true and mfaActive=false

Policy: IAM users with unused credentials enabled for more than 90 days
Description: AWS IAM users use credentials like passwords and access keys to connect to AWS resources. It is a best practice disable any credentials unused for more than 90days. Disabling or removing unnecessary  credentials will reduce the window of opportunity for credentials associated with a compromised or abandoned account to be used.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose IAM service. Click on "Users" and select the user reported as non-compliant. Click "Security credentials" tab. Click on "Make inactive" or Delete keys

Policy: IAM users with console access that have default access keys configured
Description: By default, Creating an IAM user through AWS management console creates the access keys for a user. This results in many access keys being generated unnecessarily. It is a best practice to generate access keys only if required to avoid any additional management work in auditing and rotating these keys.Requiring that additional steps be taken by the user after their profile has been created will give a stronger indication of an intent that access keys are [a] necessary for their work and [b] once the access key is established on an account that the keys may be in use somewhere in the organization
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose IAM service. Click on "Users" and select the user reported as non-compliant. Click "Security credentials" tab. Click on "Make inactive" or Delete keys
CQL: IAMUser where passwordEnabled=true should not have accessKeyConfiguredAtCreation=true

Policy: AWS accounts that have root users with Hardware MFA not configured
Description: The root account is the most privileged user in an AWS account. Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) adds an extra layer of protection on top of a username and password.  A hardware MFA has a smaller attack surface than a virtual MFA. For example, a hardware MFA does not suffer the attack surface introduced by the mobile smartphone on which a virtual MFA resides. It is a best practice to at least have hardware MFA for a privileged user like root account.
Recommendation: Log in to the AWS Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/ using root credentials. Go to "My Security Credentials" from drop-down menu next to account name on top right corner. If a pop-up opens select "Continue to Security Credentials". On "Your Security Credentials" page select "Multi-factor authentication (MFA)". Click on "Activate MFA" and Follow the instructions on "Manage MFA Device" wizard to setup "Other hardware MFA" device
CQL: Account should have rootAccountMFAEnabled=true and rootUserVirtualMFADeviceConfigured=true

Policy: EC2 instances that allow access to port 27017 from external sources.
Description: The port 27017 is the default port for mongod and mongose instances. It is a security risk to expose DB port to the public internet even though they are on a secure socket layer. Anyone on the internet can run port scanning tools, determine the open ports and launch specific attacks. It is a best practice to block public access, and restrict access from specific IP addresses to port 27017 and make the connection secure.
Recommendation: Log in to the AWS Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose EC2 or VPC Service. In the Navigation page, choose Security Groups. Select the security group to edit, click on the "Inbound" tab and click Edit. Replace the rule with Source as 0.0.0.0/0 with specific IP address or delete the rule
CQL: EC2Instance where isPublic=true should not have publicInboundRules with [port=27017 and protocol in ('tcp', 'udp')]

Policy: EC2 instances that allow access to port 27018 from external sources.
Description: The port 27018 is the default port for mongod when running with--shardsvr command-line option or the shardsvr value for the clusterRole setting in a configuration file. It is a security risk to expose DB port to the public internet even though they are on a secure socket layer. Anyone on the internet can run port scanning tools, determine the open ports and launch specific attacks. It is a best practice to block public access, and restrict access from specific IP addresses to port 27018 and make the connection secure.
Recommendation: Log in to the AWS Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose EC2 or VPC Service. In the Navigation page, choose Security Groups. Select the security group to edit, click on the "Inbound" tab and click Edit. Replace the rule with Source as 0.0.0.0/0 with specific IP address or delete the rule
CQL: EC2Instance where isPublic=true should not have publicInboundRules with [port=27018 and protocol in ('tcp', 'udp')]

Policy: EC2 instances that allow access to port 27019 from external sources.
Description: The port 27019 is the default port for mongod when running with --configsvr command-line option or the configsvr value for the clusterRole setting in a configuration file. It is a security risk to expose DB port to the public internet even though they are on a secure socket layer. Anyone on the internet can run port scanning tools, determine the open ports and launch specific attacks. It is a best practice to block public access, and restrict access from specific IP addresses to port 27019 and make the connection secure.
Recommendation: Log in to AWS Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose EC2 or VPC Service. In the Navigation page, choose Security Groups. Select the security group to edit, click on the "Inbound" tab and click Edit. Replace the rule with Source as 0.0.0.0/0 with specific IP address or delete the rule
CQL: EC2Instance where isPublic=true should not have publicInboundRules with [port=27019 and protocol in ('tcp', 'udp')]

Policy: AWS accounts that have root users being used for accessing AWS account.
Description: The root account is the most privileged user in an AWS account. Minimizing the use of this account and adopting the principle of least privilege for access management will reduce the risk of accidental changes and unintended disclosure of highly privileged credentials. It is a best practice to create IAM users with required priviliges and use them for various configurations.
Recommendation: Log in to the AWS console and go IAM Service and click on Users . Create individual users for anyone who needs access to your AWS account and grant permissions on the least access basis. Also, create an IAM user with administrative permissions, and use that user for your work

Policy: IAM users directly attached to policies(inline or managed).
Description: An IAM user gets the required permissions to access AWS resources through IAM policies. The IAM policy can be associated with users, groups, or roles. It is recommended that IAM policies be applied directly to groups and roles but not to users. Assigning privileges at the group or role level reduces the complexity of access management as the number of users grow.
Recommendation: Log in to the AWS Console and go to the IAM service and click on Users. For each user, select the user and click on the Permissions tab. Make sure policies are associated only under category - "Attached from group". Remove policies under category - "Attached directly"
CQL: IAMUser should have attachedPolicies isEmpty() or inlinePolicies isEmpty()

Policy: AWS accounts that are not configured with atleast one trail that is enabled in all regions.
Description: CloudTrail records all API calls on AWS account and delivers the log files to S3 bucket. The recorded information includes the identity of the API caller, the time of the API call, the source IP address of the API caller, the request parameters, and the response elements returned by the AWS service. It is a best practice to enable cloud trail across all regions so that you can detect if there are any unexpected activity happening in unused regions
Recommendation: Log in to the AWS Console and go to Cloud Trail https://console.aws.amazon.com/cloudtrail . Click on Trails on the left navigation pane. If no Trail exists, create a new trail by setting "Apply trail to all regions" to Yes and "Read/Write events" to All. If some trails already exist, select the target trail to enable for global logging and set "Apply trail to all regions" to Yes and "Read/Write events" to All
CQL: Account should not have multiRegionCloudTrailCount=0

Policy: Cloud Trails with log file validation disabled.
Description: CloudTrail log file validation creates a digitally signed digest file containing a hash of each log that CloudTrail writes to S3. These digest files can be used to determine whether a log file was changed, deleted, or unchanged after CloudTrail delivered the log. It is recommended that file validation is enabled on all CloudTrails.
Recommendation: Log in to the AWS Management Console and go to CloudTrail https://console.aws.amazon.com/cloudtrail. Click on Trails on the left navigation pane. Click on target trail, and within S3 section make sure "Enable log file validation" is set to Yes
CQL: CloudTrail should have logFileValidationEnabled=true

Policy: Cloud Trails that either have no integration with Cloud Watch or do not have successful log delivery to CloudWatch in last 24 hours.
Description: CloudTrail records all API calls on AWS account and delivers the log files to S3 bucket. The recorded information includes the identity of the API caller, the time of the API call, the source IP address of the API caller, the request parameters, and the response elements returned by the AWS service. For real-time analysis of these logs, it is advised to configure CloudTrail to send logs to CloudWatch Logs. Sending CloudTrail logs to CloudWatch Logs will facilitate historic activity logging based on user, API, resource, and IP address, and provides an opportunity to establish alarms and notifications for anomalous or sensitivity account activity
Recommendation: Log in to the AWS Management Console and go to CloudTrail  https://console.aws.amazon.com/cloudtrail. Click Trails in the left menu. Click on each trail where no CloudWatch Logs log group is empty. Go to the CloudWatch Logs section and click on Configure to integrate CloudWatch log group

Policy: EC2 instances that allow access to port 9042 from external sources.
Description: Databases are the placeholders for most sensitive and confidential information in an organization. Allowing access from all IP addresses on the Internet to the database ports can lead to attacks like DoS, Brute Force, Smurf, and reconnaissance. It is a best practice to block public access, and restrict the Inbound traffic from specific addresses.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose EC2 or VPC Service. In the Navigation pane, choose "Security Groups". Select the security group to edit, click on the "Inbound" tab and click Edit. Replace the rule with Source as 0.0.0.0/0 with specific IP address or delete the rule
CQL: EC2Instance where isPublic=true should not have publicInboundRules with [port=9042 and protocol in ('tcp', 'udp')]

Description: Databases are the placeholders for most sensitive and confidential information in an organization. CQL: EC2Instance where isPublic=true should not have publicInboundRules with [port=9300 and protocol in ('tcp', 'udp')]

Policy: EC2 instances that allow access to port 389 from external sources.
Description: LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) is used to connect to directory services for user authentication and retrieval. Allowing access from all IP addresses on the Internet to LDAP ports can lead to attacks like DoS, Brute Force, Smurf and reconnaissance. It is a best practice to restrict the Inbound traffic from specific addresses.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose EC2 or VPC Service. In the Navigation pane, choose "Security Groups". Select the security group to edit, click on the "Inbound" tab and click Edit. Replace the rule with Source as 0.0.0.0/0 with specific IP address or delete the rule
CQL: EC2Instance where isPublic=true should not have publicInboundRules with [port=389 and protocol in ('tcp', 'udp')]

Policy: EC2 instances that allow access to port 11211 from external sources.
Description: Allowing access from all IP addresses on the Internet to Memcached ports can lead to attacks like DoS, Brute Force, Smurf and reconnaissance. It is a best practice to restrict the Inbound traffic from specific addresses.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose EC2 or VPC Service. In the Navigation pane, choose "Security Groups". Select the security group to edit, click on the "Inbound" tab and click Edit. Replace the rule with Source as 0.0.0.0/0 with specific IP address or delete the rule
CQL: EC2Instance where isPublic=true should not have publicInboundRules with [port=11211 and protocol in ('tcp', 'udp')]

Policy: IAM policies that allow full administrative privileges.
Description: Creating policies with *.* ("Effect": "Allow" and "Action": "*" over "Resource": "*") grants full administrative privileges. It is a best practice to start with a minimum set of permissions and grant additional permissions as required, rather than starting with permissions that are too lenient and then trying to tighten them later.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose IAM service. In the Navigation pane, go to "Policies". Select policy reported as violation. Under "Policy actions", select "Detach". Select all users, roles and groups that have this policy attached and click "Detach Policy"
CQL: IAMPolicy should not have arn!='arn:aws:iam::aws:policy' and policyVersionList with [isDefaultVersion=true and policyStatement with [effect='Allow' and action contain-any ('*') and resource contain-any ('*')]]

Policy: EC2 instances that allow access to port 2483 from external sources.
Description: Databases are the placeholders for most sensitive and confidential information in an organization. Allowing access from all IP addresses on the Internet to the database ports can lead to attacks like DoS, Brute Force, Smurf, and reconnaissance. It is a best practice to block public access, and restrict the Inbound traffic from specific addresses and make the connection secure.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose EC2 or VPC Service. In the Navigation pane, choose "Security Groups". Select the security group to edit, click on the "Inbound" tab and click Edit. Replace the rule with Source as 0.0.0.0/0 with specific IP address or delete the rule
CQL: EC2Instance where isPublic=true should not have publicInboundRules with [port=2483 and protocol in ('tcp', 'udp')]

Policy: EC2 instances that allow access to port 6379 from external sources.
Description: Redis is a caching service to store application related informations which are sensitive and confidential. Allowing access from all IP addresses on the Internet to the redis ports can lead to attacks like DoS, Brute Force, Smurf, and reconnaissance. It is a best practice to block public access, and restrict the Inbound traffic from specific addresses and make the connection secure.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose EC2 or VPC Service. In the Navigation pane, choose "Security Groups". Select the security group to edit, click on the "Inbound" tab and click Edit. Replace the rule with Source as 0.0.0.0/0 with specific IP address or delete the rule
CQL: EC2Instance where isPublic=true should not have publicInboundRules with [port=6379 and protocol in ('tcp', 'udp')]

Policy: IAM users configured with more than one active access key.
Description: IAM user access keys are mainly used for programmatic access through CLI or API calls. Each IAM user is restricted to have maximum only 2 access keys at any point of time. This is mainly to support the rotation of keys so that applications already using old access keys can gracefully update the new keys without any downtime. It is a best practice to always use one access key and create new access key only at the time of rotation.
CQL: IAMUser should not have firstAccessKeyActive=true and secondAccessKeyActive=true

Policy: AWS accounts that are not configured to monitor and notify for authorized API calls.
Description: Real-time monitoring of API calls can be achieved by directing CloudTrail Logs to CloudWatch Logs and establishing corresponding metric filters and alarms. It is recommended that a metric filter and alarm be established for unauthorized API calls
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, choose "cloudtrail". Click on "Trails" on left navigation pane and create new, or select  already existing trail which is enabled across all regions ("Region" column with value "All" and with Read/Write events "All") and integrated with cloudwatch. Copy the log group name under "CloudWatch Logs Log group" column . Go to "cloudwatch" service, click on "Logs" on left navigation pane . Select the log group copied in step 3 and click on "Create Metric Filter" . Apply the Filter Pattern "{ ($.errorCode = *UnauthorizedOperation) || ($.errorCode = AccessDenied*) }" and click on "Assign Metric" . Provide proper Filter name, Metric Namespace and Metric Name and click on "Create Filter". On the confirmation page, click on "Create Alarm" link next to the Metric listed or Go to "Alarm" in the left navigation pane and click on "Create Alarm". On the Create new alarm page, make sure the metric created is selected. Under "Alarm Details", provide a Name for alarm, and select an appropriate condition under "Whenever" section. eg: "is" >= 1, for 1 out of 1 datapoints. Under "Actions", select appropriate alarm condition and SNS topic, and click on "Create Alarm". Make sure at least one user is subscribed to SNS topic selected in step 11
CQL: Account should have logMetricAlarmWithSNSOnCloudTrail with [filterPattern='{ ($.errorCode = *UnauthorizedOperation) || ($.errorCode = AccessDenied*) }']

Policy: AWS accounts that do not have public access block settings to block new public bucket policies.
Description: S3 buckets can be used to store sensitive and confidential information in an organization. So it is always advised to protect them from public access to avoid any data breaches. As a best practice enable S3 bucket public access setting  "Block new public bucket policies". This prevents any accidental exposure of S3 buckets to the public
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose S3. Select "Public access settings for this account". Click on "Edit" button, enable a setting option "Block new public bucket policies" and save the configurations
CQL: Account should have pabBlockPublicPolicy=true

Policy: AWS accounts that do not have public access block settings to restrict public and cross-account access to buckets that have public policies.
Description: S3 buckets can be used to store sensitive and confidential information in an organization. So it is always advised to protect them from public access to avoid any data breaches. As a best practice enable S3 bucket public access setting  "Block public and cross-account access to buckets that have public policies". This prevents any accidental exposure of S3 buckets to the public and also blocks cross-account access to the bucket
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose S3. Select "Public access settings for this account". Click on "Edit" button, enable a setting option "Block public and cross-account access to buckets that have public policies" and save the configurations
CQL: Account should have pabRestrictPublicBuckets=true

Policy: ELBs that allow access to port 3389 from external sources.
Description: RDP (Remote Desktop Protocol) port - 3389 is used to get remote control access to Windows Instances. Allowing access from all IP addresses on the Internet to RDP port is vulnerable to “remote code execution”, “privilege elevation” and “flooding attack”. It is a best practice to restrict access from specific  IP addresses to port 3389.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose EC2 or VPC Service. In the Navigation pane, choose "Security Groups". Select the security group associated to ELB. Click on the "Inbound" tab and click Edit. Replace the rule with Source as 0.0.0.0/0 with specific IP address or delete the rule
CQL: ELB where isPublic=true should not have publicInboundRules with [port=3389 and protocol in ('tcp', 'udp')]

Policy: ELBs that allow access to port 22 from external sources.
Description: SSH (Secure Shell) port - 22 is used to get CLI access to Linux Instances. Allowing Inbound traffic from all external IP addresses to SSH port is vulnerable to “banner grabbing” and “brute force attack”. It is a best practice to restrict access from specific IP addresses to port 22.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose EC2 or VPC Service. In the Navigation pane, choose "Security Groups". Select the security group associated to ELB. Click on the "Inbound" tab and click Edit. Replace the rule with Source as 0.0.0.0/0 with specific IP address or delete the rule
CQL: ELB where isPublic=true should not have publicInboundRules with [port=22 and protocol in ('tcp', 'udp')]

Allowing Inbound traffic from external IPv4 addresses to the database ports can lead to attacks like DoS, Brute Force, Smurf, and reconnaissance. It is a best practice to block public access, and restrict the Inbound traffic from specific addresses and make the connection secure.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose EC2 or VPC Service. In the Navigation pane, choose "Security Groups". Select the security group associated to ELB. Click on the "Inbound" tab and click Edit. Replace the rule with Source as 0.0.0.0/0 with specific IP address or delete the rule
CQL: ELB where isPublic=true should not have publicInboundRules with [port=2483 and protocol in ('tcp', 'udp')]

Policy: ELBs that have no security group associated.
Description: It is a security best practice to always have application load balancers associated with security groups to avoid any data loss or unauthorized access.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose EC2. In the Navigation pane, choose "Load Balancers". Select the load balancer reported as violation, and associate appropriate Security groups
CQL: ELB where type='application' should not have securityGroups isEmpty()

Policy: AWS accounts that are not configured to monitor and notify Management Console sign-in without MFA.
Description: Real-time monitoring of API calls can be achieved by directing CloudTrail Logs to CloudWatch Logs and establishing corresponding metric filters and alarms. It is recommended that a metric filter and alarm be established for console logins that are not protected by multi-factor authentication (MFA).
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, choose "cloudtrail". Click on "Trails" on left navigation pane and create new, or select already existing trail which is enabled across all regions ("Region" column with value "All" and with Read/Write events "All") and integrated with cloudwatch. Copy the log group name under "CloudWatch Logs Log group" column . Go to "cloudwatch" service, click on "Logs" on left navigation pane . Select the log group copied in step 3 and click on "Create Metric Filter" . Apply the Filter Pattern "{ ($.eventName = ConsoleLogin) && ($.additionalEventData.MFAUsed != Yes) }" and click on "Assign Metric" . Provide proper Filter name, Metric Namespace and Metric Name and click on "Create Filter". On the confirmation page, click on "Create Alarm" link next to the Metric listed or Go to "Alarm" in the left navigation pane and click on "Create Alarm". On the Create new alarm page, make sure the metric created is selected. Under "Alarm Details", provide a Name for alarm, and select an appropriate condition under "Whenever" section. eg: "is" >= 1, for 1 out of 1 datapoints. Under "Actions", select appropriate alarm condition and SNS topic, and click on "Create Alarm". Make sure at least one user is subscribed to SNS topic selected in step 11
CQL: Account should have logMetricAlarmWithSNSOnCloudTrail with [filterPattern='{ ($.eventName = ConsoleLogin) && ($.additionalEventData.MFAUsed != Yes) }']

Policy: AWS accounts that are not configured to monitor and notify usage of root account.
Description: Real-time monitoring of API calls can be achieved by directing CloudTrail Logs to CloudWatch Logs and establishing corresponding metric filters and alarms. It is recommended that a metric filter and alarm be established for usage of "root" account
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, choose "cloudtrail". Copy the log group name under "CloudWatch Logs Log group" column . Go to "cloudwatch" service, click on "Logs", select the log group copied and click on "Create Metric Filter". Apply the Filter Pattern "{ ($.userIdentity.type = Root) && ($.userIdentity.invokedBy NOT EXISTS) && ($.eventType != AwsServiceEvent) }" and create metric filter with proper filter name, metric namespace and metric name . Go to "Alarm", create an alarm for the metric created in step 5 with an appropriate alarm condition and SNS topic. Make sure at least one user is subscribed to SNS topic selected in step 6
CQL: Account should have logMetricAlarmWithSNSOnCloudTrail with [filterPattern='{ ($.userIdentity.type = Root) && ($.userIdentity.invokedBy NOT EXISTS) && ($.eventType != AwsServiceEvent) }']
