Allowing Inbound traffic from external IPv4 addresses to the database ports can lead to attacks like DoS, Brute Force, Smurf, and reconnaissance. It is a best practice to block public access, and restrict the Inbound traffic from specific addresses and make the connection secure.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose EC2 or VPC Service. In the Navigation pane, choose "Security Groups". Select the security group associated to ELB. Click on the "Inbound" tab and click Edit. Replace the rule with Source as 0.0.0.0/0 with specific IP address or delete the rule
CQL: ELB where isPublic=true should not have publicInboundRules with [port=9300 and protocol in ('tcp', 'udp')]

Policy: ELBs that allow access to port 389 from external sources.
Description: LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) is used to connect to directory services for user authentication and retrieval. Allowing Inbound traffic from external IPv4 addresses to LDAP ports can lead to attacks like DoS, Brute Force, Smurf and reconnaissance. It is a best practice to restrict the Inbound traffic from specific addresses.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose EC2 or VPC Service. In the Navigation pane, choose "Security Groups". Select the security group associated to ELB. Click on the "Inbound" tab and click Edit. Replace the rule with Source as 0.0.0.0/0 with specific IP address or delete the rule
CQL: ELB where isPublic=true should not have publicInboundRules with [port=389 and protocol in ('tcp', 'udp')]

Policy: ELBs that allow access to port 636 from external sources.
Description: LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) is used to connect to directory services for user authentication and retrieval. Allowing Inbound traffic from external IPv4 addresses to LDAP ports can lead to attacks like DoS, Brute Force, Smurf and reconnaissance. It is a best practice to restrict the Inbound traffic from specific addresses.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose EC2 or VPC Service. In the Navigation pane, choose "Security Groups". Select the security group associated to ELB. Click on the "Inbound" tab and click Edit. Replace the rule with Source as 0.0.0.0/0 with specific IP address or delete the rule
CQL: ELB where isPublic=true should not have publicInboundRules with [port=636 and protocol in ('tcp', 'udp')]

Policy: ELBs that allow access to port 11211 from external sources.
Description: Allowing Inbound traffic from external IPv4 addresses to Memcached ports can lead to attacks like DoS, Brute Force, Smurf and reconnaissance. It is a best practice to restrict the Inbound traffic from specific addresses.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose EC2 or VPC Service. In the Navigation pane, choose "Security Groups". Select the security group associated to ELB. Click on the "Inbound" tab and click Edit. Replace the rule with Source as 0.0.0.0/0 with specific IP address or delete the rule
CQL: ELB where isPublic=true should not have publicInboundRules with [port=11211 and protocol in ('tcp', 'udp')]

Policy: ELBs that allow access to port 6379 from external sources.
Description: Redis is a caching service to store application related informations which are sensitive and confidential. Allowing Inbound traffic from external IPv4 addresses to the redis ports can lead to attacks like DoS, Brute Force, Smurf, and reconnaissance. It is a best practice to block public access, and restrict the Inbound traffic from specific addresses and make the connection secure.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose EC2 or VPC Service. In the Navigation pane, choose "Security Groups". Select the security group associated to ELB. Click on the "Inbound" tab and click Edit. Replace the rule with Source as 0.0.0.0/0 with specific IP address or delete the rule
CQL: ELB where isPublic=true should not have publicInboundRules with [port=6379 and protocol in ('tcp', 'udp')]

Policy: EC2 instances that allow access to port 636 from external sources.
Description: LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) is used to connect to directory services for user authentication and retrieval. Allowing access from all IP addresses on the Internet to the LDAP ports can lead to attacks like DoS, Brute Force, Smurf and reconnaissance. It is a best practice to restrict the Inbound traffic from specific addresses.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose EC2 or VPC Service. In the Navigation pane, choose "Security Groups". Select the security group to edit, click on the "Inbound" tab and click Edit. Replace the rule with Source as 0.0.0.0/0 with specific IP address or delete the rule
CQL: EC2Instance where isPublic=true should not have publicInboundRules with [port=636 and protocol in ('tcp', 'udp')]

Policy: RDS instance allowing traffic from 0.0.0.0/0
Description: Databases are the placeholders for most sensitive and confidential information in an organization. Allowing Inbound traffic from external IPv4 addresses to the database ports can lead to attacks like DoS, Brute Force, Smurf, and reconnaissance. It is a best practice to block public access, and restrict the Inbound traffic from specific addresses.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, choose RDS. In the navigation pane, choose Databases. Select the database reported as a violation, and click Modify. Under "Network & Security" set the "Public accessibility" to "No" and click Continue. Under "Scheduling of modifications" select "Apply Immediately" and click "Modify DB Instance. Also, ensure the VPC security group associated with the database instance do not allow traffic from 0.0.0.0/0
CQL: RDSInstance should not have isPublic = true

Policy: RDS instance that is not encrypted
Description: Encrypted DB instances provide an additional layer of data protection by securing data from unauthorized access to the underlying storage. It is a best practice to encrypt databases to increase protection of data at rest, and also to fulfil compliance requirements.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, choose RDS. In the navigation pane, choose Databases. Select the database reported as a violation, and click Actions and select "Take snapshot". On "Take DB Snapshot" page, provide an appropriate snapshot name and click "Take Snapshot". Once the snapshot is created, select the newly created snapshot from the Snapshots page. Click Actions, and select "Copy Snapshot". On "Make Copy of DB Snapshot", provide a new name for the snapshot under "New DB Snapshot Identifier" field, check "Copy Tags", and select "Enable encryption", choose an appropriate "Master key" for encryption, and click "Copy Snapshot". Once the snapshot is created, select the newly created snapshot from the Snapshots page. Click Actions, and select "Restore Snapshot". On "Restore DB Instance" page, provide a new name for DB instance under "DB Instance Identifier" field, verify the configurations and click "Restore DB Instance". Once the new DB instance status is "available", update your application to refer to the new instance. And also delete the old unencrypted DB instance
CQL: RDSInstance should have encrypted = true

Policy: RDS instance without deletion protection
Description: Deletion protection avoids any accidental deletion of the database by any user through Console, CLI or API. It is a best practice to enable deletion protection to keep your database safe.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, choose RDS. In the navigation pane, choose Databases. Select the database reported as a violation, and click Modify. Under "Deletion protection", check "Enable deletion protection" and click Continue. Under "Scheduling of modifications" select your desired option, and click "Modify DB Instance"
CQL: RDSInstance should have deletionProtection = true

Policy: RDS instance without automated backups
Description: Amazon RDS supports automated backups of DB instance according to the backup retention period that you specify. It is a best practice to configure automated backups of your database instance to avoid any accidental loss of information. It facilitates you to recover your database to any point in time during the backup retention period.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, choose RDS. In the navigation pane, choose Databases. Select the database reported as a violation, and click Modify. Under Backup, ensure "Backup retention period" is set to more than 0 days with appropriate backup window and click Continue. Under "Scheduling of modifications" select your desired option, and click "Modify DB Instance"
CQL: RDSInstance should not have backupRetentionPeriod = '0'

Policy: RDS snapshot that is public
Description: If an RDS snapshot is exposed to the public, any AWS account can copy the snapshot and create a new database instance from it. It is a best practice to ensure RDS snapshots are not exposed to the public to avoid any accidental leak of sensitive information.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, choose RDS. In the navigation pane, choose Snapshots. Select the snapshot reported as a violation, and click Actions, and select "Share Snapshot". On "Snapshot permissions" page, select "Private" under "DB snapshot visibility", and add only desired AWS accounts to share the snapshot, and click Save
CQL: RDSSnapshot should not have dbSnapshotAttributeValues contain ('all')

Policy: RDS snapshot that is not encrypted
Description: Encrypted DB snapshot provides an additional layer of data protection by securing data from any unauthorized access. RDS snapshot created from an encrypted source DB instance or cluster is encrypted by default. But if the source is not encrypted, the RDS snapshot is also not encrypted. However, it is possible to encrypt an unencrypted DB snapshot to add an extra layer of protection for data at rest.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, choose RDS. In the navigation pane, choose Snapshots. Select the snapshot reported as a violation, and click Actions, and select "Copy Snapshot". On "Make Copy of DB Snapshot", provide a new name for the snapshot under "New DB Snapshot Identifier" field, check "Copy Tags", and select "Enable encryption", choose an appropriate "Master key" for encryption, and click "Copy Snapshot"
CQL: RDSSnapshot should have encrypted = true

Policy: Lambda functions should have active tracing enabled
Description: Enable active tracing for Lambda functions to gain insights into your function processes and to identify any issues. It records the function invocations and errors in the Amazon X-Ray service, which helps to troubleshoot and diagnose any problems.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/. Navigate to AWS Lambda. Click on the specific function. Under "AWS X-Ray" section, select the checkbox named "Active tracing" and save the configuration
CQL: Lambda should have tracingConfigMode = 'Active'

Policy: Lambda functions should have tags
Description: Tags help you to group resources together that are related to or associated with each other. It is a best practice to tag cloud resources to better organize and gain visibility into their usage.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/. Navigate to AWS Lambda. Click the specific function. Under "Tags" section, add appropriate tags and save the configuration
CQL: Lambda should not have tags isEmpty()

Policy: Lambda functions should access only VPC resources
Description: Ensure the lambda functions are configured to access your cloud resources within a VPC (Virtual Private Cloud). It enables Lambda functions to connect securely to VPC resources like databases, cache instances, or internal services.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/. Navigate to AWS Lambda. Click the specific function. Under "Network" section, select the appropriate VPC, Subnets, and Security groups and save the configuration
CQL: Lambda should not have vpcId isEmpty()

Policy: Lambda functions should not have Admin roles
Description: An execution role grants required permission to Lambda function to access AWS services and resources. Associating a role with wild card permissions ("Effect": "Allow" and "Action": "*" and "Resource": "*") for grants full administrative privileges to the Lambda function. It is recommended to have limited permissions for each Lambda function to follow the principle of least privilege access.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/. Navigate to AWS Lambda. For each lambda function, ensure the IAM role does not grant wild card permissions ("Effect": "Allow" and "Action": "*" and "Resource": "*")
CQL: Lambda should not have executionRolePolicy with [statement with [effect = 'Allow' and action contain-any ('*') and resource contain-any ('*')]]

Policy: Lambda functions should not share the same execution roles
Description: An execution role grants required permission to Lambda function to access AWS services and resources. It is recommended to associate the unique IAM role for each Lambda function to follow the principle of least privilege access.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/. Navigate to AWS Lambda. For each lambda function, ensure different IAM role is associated under Execution role section
CQL: Lambda should not have isExecutionRoleShared = true

Policy: Route 53 domains should have auto renewal enabled
Description: It is recommended to enable auto-renewal for registered route 53 domains to avoid the loss of domain name due to expiration.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/. Navigate to AWS Route53. In the left navigation panel, under "Domains," click on "Registered domains." . Click on the desired domain name, and enable "Auto renew."
CQL: Route53Domain should have autoRenew=true

Policy: Route 53 domains should have transfer lock enabled
Description: It is recommended to enable the transfer lock on your domains. It avoids the transfer of your domain to any other registrar without your permission.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/. Navigate to AWS Route53. In the left navigation panel, under "Domains," click on "Registered domains.". Click on the desired domain name, and enable "Transfer lock."
CQL: Route53Domain should have transferLock=true

Policy: Route 53 domains should be restored or removed
Description: This policy lists all the domain names that are already exipred. Once the domain names are expired they are available for others to register. If you need to retain the domain names, restore them by paying appropriate fee, or otherwise delete them.
Recommendation: If you want to restore the domain name, follow the instructions documented in the link - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/Route53/latest/DeveloperGuide/domain-restore-expired.html. If you want to delete the domain name, navigate to the registered domain name in AWS console and click "Delete domain."
CQL: Route53Domain should not have expiryDays < 0

Policy: Route 53 domains that are due for renewal
Description: This policy lists all the domain names that are due for renewal in next 30 days. Please configure the auto-renewal to renew the domains before their expiration.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/. Navigate to AWS Route53. In the left navigation panel, under "Domains," click on "Registered domains.". Click on the desired domain name, and enable "Auto renew."
CQL: Route53Domain should not have expiryDays < 30

Policy: Route 53 domains should have privacy protection enabled
Description: A domain without privacy protection allows anyone to send WHOIS ("Who is") query on your domain and get all the contact information you have provided at the time of domain registration or domain transfer. It is recommended to enable privacy protection to avoid leaking of your PII details like name, address, phone number, and email address.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/. Navigate to AWS Route53. In the left navigation panel, under "Domains," click on "Registered domains.". Click on the desired domain name, and click "Edit contact.". Ensure "Privacy Protection" is set to "Enable" for Registrant, Admin and Tech Contact
CQL: Route53Domain should have registrantPrivacy=true

Policy: ECS Cluster CloudWatch Container Insights feature should be enabled
Description: CloudWatch Container Insights is a monitoring and troubleshooting solution for containerized applications and microservices. It collects, aggregates, and summarizes compute utilization such as CPU, memory, disk, and network, and diagnostic information such as container restart failures to help you isolate issues with your clusters and resolve them quickly.
Recommendation: Run following AWS CLI to enable "Container Insights" on already existing clusters. Ensure to replace <cluster_name_or_arn> with appropriate cluster arn and <region_name> with appropriate AWS region name (e.g: us-east-1). aws ecs update-cluster-settings --cluster <cluster_name_or_arn> --settings name=containerInsights,value=enabled --region <region_name>
CQL: ECSCluster should have settings with [settingName='containerInsights' and settingValue='enabled']

Policy: ECS Cluster should have active services
Description: Amazon ECS service allows you to run and maintain a specified number of instances of a task definition simultaneously in an Amazon ECS cluster. It is recommended to have clusters with the active services to avoid any container attack surface.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/. Navigate to AWS ECS. Select the cluster that does not have any service. Under "Services" tab click the button "create" and create a service with appropriate parameters
CQL: ECSCluster should not have activeServicesCount=0

Policy: ECS clusters should atleast one service with running tasks
Description: A task is the instantiation of a task definition within a cluster. Amazon ECS service instantiates and maintains the specified number of tasks simultaneously in a cluster. As a best practice, ensure you always have running tasks in a cluster.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/. Navigate to AWS ECS. Select the cluster that does not have any active tasks. Under "Tasks" tab click the button "Run new Task" and create a task based on the appropriate Task Definition
CQL: ECSCluster should not have runningTasksCount=0

Policy: ECR repositories should not be exposed to Public
Description: Amazon ECR is a managed AWS Docker registry service that supports private Docker repositories to store and manage your Docker images. The access to Docker images can be controlled by the resource-based IAM permissions that define the users or EC2 instances that can access the repository. As a best practice, avoid granting anonymous or public access to your repositories, instead, allow limited access to authenticated users or EC2 instances.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/. Navigate to AWS ECR. Select the Repositories reported as a violation, and click Permissions, and click "Edit". On "Edit permissions" page, under Principal section, uncheck "Everyone(*)," and add only desired Service principal or AWS accounts or IAM entities to access ECR. Under Actions section, select only the desired actions and save the configuration
CQL: ECRRepository should not have policyStatement with [(principal='*' or principalAWS contain-any ('*')) and effect='Allow']

Policy: EFS file systems should be encrypted
Description: Amazon EFS file system provides simple and scalable file storage for EC2 Instances. As a security best practice, it is recommended to encrypt the EFS file system at rest and adhere to compliance standards.
Recommendation: It is not possible to encrypt the already created EFS file system. The only option is to create a new Amazon EFS file system with encryption enabled, and then copy all the data from the existing unencrypted file system onto the new one. Follow the below instructions to create an encrypted EFS file system. Login into Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/. Navigate to AWS EFS. Click on the "Create File System" button. Under Step 1: Configure file system access, choose your VPC, create your mount targets, and then choose Next Step. Under Step 2: Configure optional settings, ensure to check the box "Enable encryption of data at rest," and select the KMS master key, and then choose Next Step. Under Step 3: Review and create, review your settings, and choose Create File System
CQL: EFSFileSystem should have encrypted=true

Policy: EFS file systems should have tags
Description: Tags help you to group resources together that are related to or associated with each other. It is a best practice to tag cloud resources to better organize and gain visibility into their usage.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/. Navigate to AWS EFS. Select the specific EFS file system. And choose "Actions" and select "Manage tags". Under Manage tags screen, add appropriate tags and save the configuration
CQL: EFSFileSystem should not have tags isEmpty()

Policy: EKS Clusters that do not have logging enabled
Description: Amazon EKS control plane logging provides audit and diagnostic logs to monitor the cluster. These logs are sent directly to the CloudWatch Logs in the same account. As a security best practice, ensure you enable the desired log types and stream them to CloudWatch logs.
Recommendation: Login into Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/. Navigate to AWS EKS. Select the EKS Cluster reported under violation. Select the specific EKS Cluster, navigate to the "Logging" section, click the "Update" button. Enable the desired log types, and save the configuration
CQL: EKSCluster should have clusterLogging with [enabled=true]

Policy: Elasticsearch domains should have encryption of data at rest enabled
Description: To meet security compliance standards and to protect the sensitive data from malicious attacks, Elasticsearch domains must enable encryption of data at rest. It encrypts domain aspects like indices, elastic search logs, swap files, data in the application directory, and automated snapshots. If a domain is already created without encryption, you can't enable the encryption on the same domain. You need to create a new domain by enabling encryption and migrate data from the old domain to the new domain.
Recommendation: Login into Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/. Navigate to AWS Elasticsearch Service. Select the ES domain reported as a violation, and copy the configuration details of the same. Create a new ES domain by setting "Enable encryption of data at rest", and selecting an appropriate key from the "KMS master key" dropdown. And also, ensuring the copied configurations from the old domain are set on the new domain. Once the new ES domain is created, follow the link to migrate data from the old domain to the new domain. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/elasticsearch-service/latest/developerguide/es-version-migration.html#snapshot-based-migration. Once data is migrated to a new domain, delete the old domain
CQL: ESDomain should have isEncryptionAtRestEnabled=true

Policy: Elasticsearch cluster should have node-to-node encryption enabled
Description: The node-to-node encryption enables TLS 1.2 encryption for all communications between Elasticsearch nodes in a cluster. As an additional layer of security for ES domains, ensure to configure node-to-node encryption. By default, the node-to-node encryption is not enabled on ES domains, and it is not possible to enable node-to-node encryption on an already created domain. To enable the feature, you must create another domain and migrate your data. And node-to-node encryption works only on Elasticsearch versions 6.0 or later.
Recommendation: Login into Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/. Navigate to AWS Elasticsearch Service. Select the ES domain reported as a violation, and copy the configuration details of the same. Create a new ES domain by enabling "Node-to-node encryption". https://docs.aws.amazon.com/elasticsearch-service/latest/developerguide/es-createupdatedomains.html#es-createdomains And ensure the copied configurations are set on the new domain. Once the new ES domain is created, follow the link to migrate data from the old domain to the new domain. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/elasticsearch-service/latest/developerguide/es-version-migration.html#snapshot-based-migration. Once data is migrated to a new domain, delete the old domain
CQL: ESDomain should have isNodeToNodeEncryptionEnabled=true

Policy: Elasticsearch domains should have HTTPS enabled
Description: Ensure the Elasticsearch domains enable HTTPS connection to avoid any eavesdropping or man-in-the-middle attacks.
Recommendation: Login into Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/. Navigate to AWS Elasticsearch Service. Select the ES domain reported as a violation, and click "Edit domain". Under "Encryption", check "Require HTTPS for all traffic to the domain" and submit the configuration changes
CQL: ESDomain should have isHTTPSEnforcedOnDomainEndpoint=true

Policy: Elasticsearch domains should have VPC configured
Description: The VPC access enables you to connect to Elasticsearch domains from within an Amazon VPC without the need for NAT or Internet Gateways. It ensures that the traffic between your other application services and Elasticsearch stays within the AWS private network, isolated from the public internet.
Recommendation: Login into Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/. Navigate to AWS Elasticsearch Service. Select the ES domain reported as a violation, and copy the configuration details of the same. Create a new ES domain by setting "VPC access" under Network configuration and selecting corresponding VPC, subnet, and security groups.https://docs.aws.amazon.com/elasticsearch-service/latest/developerguide/es-createupdatedomains.html#es-createdomains And ensure the copied configurations are set on the new domain. Once the new ES domain is created, follow the link to migrate data from the old domain to the new domain. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/elasticsearch-service/latest/developerguide/es-version-migration.html#snapshot-based-migration. Once data is migrated to a new domain, delete the old domain
CQL: ESDomain should not have vpcId isEmpty()

Policy: Elasticsearch zone awareness should be enabled
Description: To improve the fault-tolerance for your ES domain, ensure you enable zone awareness. It distributes the Elasticsearch nodes across multiple availability zones in the same AWS region and assures the cluster is highly available.
Recommendation: Login into Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/. Navigate to AWS Elasticsearch Service. Select the ES domain reported as a violation, and click "Edit domain". Under "Data nodes", ensure the "Availability zones" are set as "2-AZ" or "3-AZ" and the "Number of nodes" is configured to be more than one
CQL: ESDomain should have isESClusterZoneAwarenessEnabled=true

Policy: Elasticsearch with anonymous access policy
Description: A resource-based policy enables you to specify which AWS account and which AWS users or roles can access your Amazon ES endpoint. It also provides an option to define a condition for restricting access to specific source IP addresses. Allowing access to any principal (* or AWS: *) on all actions(* or es:*) is against compliance requirements, and if appropriate conditions are not added, it might expose Elasticsearch to the public. As a best practice, ensure you allow access to the specific principal on a particular set of actions on the Elasticsearch.
Recommendation: Login into Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/. Navigate to AWS Elasticsearch Service. Select the ES domain reported as a violation, and click "Modify access policy". Ensure access policy does not contain Effect as "Allow" and Principal as "*" or "AWS: *" and Action as "*" or "es:*"
CQL: ESDomain should not have accessPolicyStatement with [(principalAWS contain-any ('*') or principal='*') and effect='allow' and action contain-any ('*', 'es:*')]

Policy: Elasticsearch missing tags
Description: Tags help you to group resources together that are related to or associated with each other. It is a best practice to tag cloud resources to better organize and gain visibility into their usage.
Recommendation: Login into Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/. Navigate to AWS Elasticsearch Service. Select the ES domain reported as a violation, and click "Manage tags". Under Manage tags screen, add appropriate tags, and submit the configuration
CQL: ESDomain should not have tags isEmpty()

Policy: EC2 instance exposed to public
Description: Allowing access from all IP addresses on the Internet to EC2 instance is risky as it can lead to Brute Force or DoS attacks. To determine public access of an instance, Cloud Control checks for all the following conditions. (i) instance associated with Public IP address. (ii) instance associated with security group allowing traffic on any port from 0.0.0.0/0. (iii) instance placed in a subnet that is connected to the Internet gateway, and the subnet is associated with NACL allowing traffic from 0.0.0.0/0 on any port. As a security best practice, ensure EC2 instances are not open to the World.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose EC2 or VPC Service. In the Navigation pane, choose "Security Groups". Select the security group to edit, click on the "Inbound" tab and click Edit. Replace the rule with Source as 0.0.0.0/0 with specific IP address or delete the rule
CQL: EC2Instance should not have isPublic=true

Policy: EC2 instance associated with security group allowing traffic from 0.0.0.0/0
Description: Associating an EC2 instance with a security group that allows traffic from 0.0.0.0/0 can lead to potential threats like Brute Force or DoS attacks. As a security best practice, ensure EC2 instances are associated with the security group(s) that are allowing traffic from specific IP addresses.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose EC2 or VPC Service. In the Navigation pane, choose "Security Groups". Select the security group to edit, click on the "Inbound" tab and click Edit. Replace the rule with Source as 0.0.0.0/0 with specific IP address or delete the rule
CQL: EC2Instance should not have sgInboundRules with [sourceIPv4Ranges with [cidr='0.0.0.0/0']]

Policy: EC2 instance should have tags
Description: Tags help you to group resources together that are related to or associated with each other. It is a best practice to tag cloud resources to better organize and gain visibility into their usage.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose EC2. In the navigation pane, choose Instances and then choose the instance reported as a violation. Under "Tags" section, add appropriate tags and save the configuration
CQL: EC2Instance should not have tags isEmpty()

Policy: S3 buckets should have tags
Description: Tags help you to group resources together that are related to or associated with each other. It is a best practice to tag cloud resources to better organize and gain visibility into their usage.
Recommendation: Login into Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/. Choose S3. Select the Bucket name reported as a violation, and click "Properties". Choose Tags and then choose Add Tag. Add appropriate tags and save the configuration
CQL: S3Bucket should not have tags isEmpty()

Policy: IAM users should have tags
Description: Tags help you to group resources together that are related to or associated with each other. It is a best practice to tag cloud resources to better organize and gain visibility into their usage.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose "IAM". In the navigation pane, choose Users and then choose the user reported as a violation. Under "Tags" section, add appropriate tags and save the configuration
CQL: IAMUser should not have tags isEmpty()

Policy: IAM roles should have tags
Description: Tags help you to group resources together that are related to or associated with each other. It is a best practice to tag cloud resources to better organize and gain visibility into their usage.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose "IAM". In the navigation pane, choose Roles and then choose the role reported as a violation. Under "Tags" section, add appropriate tags and save the configuration
CQL: IAMRole should not have tags isEmpty()

Policy: Security groups should have tags
Description: Tags help you to group resources together that are related to or associated with each other. It is a best practice to tag cloud resources to better organize and gain visibility into their usage.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose EC2 or VPC Service. In the Navigation pane, choose "Security Groups". Choose the name of the security group that has reported as a violation. Under "Tags" section, add appropriate tags and save the configuration
CQL: SecurityGroup should not have tags isEmpty()

Policy: RDS instance should have tags
Description: Tags help you to group resources together that are related to or associated with each other. It is a best practice to tag cloud resources to better organize and gain visibility into their usage.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, choose RDS. In the navigation pane, choose Databases. Choose the name of the DB instance that has reported as a violation. Under "Tags" section, add appropriate tags and save the configuration
CQL: RDSInstance should not have tags isEmpty()

Policy: VPC should have tags
Description: Tags help you to group resources together that are related to or associated with each other. It is a best practice to tag cloud resources to better organize and gain visibility into their usage.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose VPC Service. In the Navigation pane, choose "Your VPCs". Select the VPC reported under violation. Under "Tags" section, add appropriate tags and save the configuration
CQL: VPC should not have tags isEmpty()

Policy: EKS clusters should have tags
Description: Tags help you to group resources together that are related to or associated with each other. It is a best practice to tag cloud resources to better organize and gain visibility into their usage.
Recommendation: Login into Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/. Navigate to AWS EKS. Select the EKS Cluster reported under violation, and click "Manage tags". Under Manage tags screen, add appropriate tags, and submit the configuration
CQL: EKSCluster should not have tags isEmpty()

Policy: ECS clusters should have tags
Description: Tags help you to group resources together that are related to or associated with each other. It is a best practice to tag cloud resources to better organize and gain visibility into their usage.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/. Navigate to AWS ECS. Select the ECS Cluster reported under violation. Under "Tags" section, add appropriate tags and save the configuration
CQL: ECSCluster should not have tags isEmpty()

Policy: EBS snapshot publicly accessible
Description: Snapshots are the backups taken on the Amazon EBS Volumes. They store sensitive and confidential information about your applications stored on the EBS volumes. As a best practice, avoid granting public access to the snapshots.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose "EC2". In the Navigation pane, select "Snapshots" under Elastic Block Store section. Select the snapshot reported as a violation and choose Actions > Modify Permissions. In the Modify Permissions modal, select the option "Private" and specify the account number you want to grant access and Save the configuration
CQL: EBSSnapshot should not have createVolumePermissionGroup='all'

Policy: Ensure SQS queues not exposed to Everyone
Description: A resource-based policy enables you to specify which AWS account and which AWS users or roles can access your SQS Queue. Allowing access to any principal (* or AWS: *) is against compliance requirements, and if appropriate conditions are not added, it might expose the SQS queue to the public. As a best practice, ensure SQS queues enable access to only desired cloud accounts or IAM members.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose SQS service. Select the desired SQS queue and go to the "Permissions" tab in the bottom panel. Click Edit icon for the permission that has "Effect" as "Allow" and "Principals" as "Everybody (*)". Under "Principal" uncheck the option "Everybody (*)" and provide an appropriate AWS cloud account number or an IAM User, and click "Save changes"
CQL: SQS should not have policyStatement with [(principal='*' or principalAWS contain-any ('*')) and effect='Allow']

Policy: Ensure EC2 Instances enable detailed monitoring
Description: By default, all EC2 instances have CloudWatch Basic monitoring. In Basic monitoring, EC2 instance sends the metric data to CloudWatch for every 5 minutes,and you can use that data to access the historical information and gain better insights into the applications hosted on the platform. As a best practice, for certain critical EC2 instances, it is recommended to enable detail monitoring. In detail monitoring, the EC2 instance pushes the metric datafor every 1 minute and also allows you to aggregate data across groups of similar EC2 instances.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose EC2. In the Navigation pane, choose "Instances". Select the instance and choose "Actions", "CloudWatch Monitoring", "Enable Detailed Monitoring". In the "Enable Detailed Monitoring" dialog box choose "Yes, Enable"
CQL: EC2Instance should have monitoringState = 'enabled'

Policy: SNS topics that allow everyone to publish
Description: A resource-based policy enables you to specify which AWS account and which AWS users or roles can access your SNS Topic. Allowing "Everyone" to publish messages to SNS topic is a security risk and can lead to DoS attacks. As a best practice, ensure SNS topics do not allow "Everyone" to Publish messages.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose SNS service. In the left navigation panel, select Topics. Select the desired SNS topic and click the Edit button. Under "Access policy" section, edit the policy statement, ensure no statement exist with a combination "Effect": "Allow", "Principal": { "AWS":"*" }, "Action": "SNS:Publish" and click "Save changes"
CQL: SNS should not have policyStatement with [effect = 'Allow' and action contain-any ('SNS:Publish') and (principal='*' or  principalAWS contain-any ('*'))]

Policy: SNS topics that allow everyone to subscribe
Description: A resource-based policy enables you to specify which AWS account and which AWS users or roles can access your SNS Topic. Allowing "Everyone" to subscribe to the SNS topic is a security risk and can lead to data leaks. As a best practice, ensure SNS topics do not allow "Everyone" to Subscribe to topics.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose SNS service. In the left navigation panel, select Topics. Select the desired SNS topic and click the Edit button. Under "Access policy" section, edit the policy statement, ensure no statement exist with a combination "Effect": "Allow", "Principal": { "AWS":"*" }, "Action": ["SNS:Subscribe", "SNS:Receive"] and click "Save changes"
CQL: SNS should not have policyStatement with [effect = 'Allow' and action contain-any ('SNS:Subscribe', 'SNS:Receive') and (principal='*' or  principalAWS contain-any ('*'))]

Policy: SNS topics that grant wildcard (*) access
Description: A resource-based policy enables you to specify which AWS account and which AWS users or roles can access your SNS Topic. Allowing access to any principal (* or AWS: *) is against compliance requirements, and if appropriate conditions are not added, it might expose SNS topics to the public. As a best practice, ensure SNS topics enable access to only desired cloud accounts or IAM members.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose SNS service. In the left navigation panel, select Topics. Select the desired SNS topic and click the Edit button. Under "Access policy" section, edit the policy statement, ensure no statement exist with a combination "Effect": "Allow", "Principal": { "AWS":"*" } and click "Save changes"
CQL: SNS should not have policyStatement with [effect = 'Allow' and (principal='*' or  principalAWS contain-any ('*'))]

Policy: SNS topics that are not encrypted
Description: Ensure SNS topics are encrypted to protect sensitive messages and avoid any data leaks.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose SNS service. In the left navigation panel, select Topics. Select the desired SNS topic and click the Edit button. Under Encryption section, select "Enable encryption" and click "Save changes"
CQL: SNS should not have kmsMasterKeyId isEmpty()

Policy: SNS topics that are not encrypted using CMKs
Description: Ensure SNS topics are encrypted using Customer Master Keys instead of default AWS managed keys reserved for SNS service to protect highly sensitive messages and have more control over the encryption and decryption process.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose SNS service. In the left navigation panel, select Topics. Select the desired SNS topic and click the Edit button. Under Encryption section, select "Enable encryption", choose one of the listed CMK (not the (Default) alias/aws/sns) or provide the ARN of the desired key. Click "Save changes"
CQL: SNS should not have kmsMasterKeyId isEmpty() or kmsMasterKeyId='alias/aws/sns'

Policy: Ensure EBS Volumes attached to EC2 Instance are Encrypted
Description: It is a security best practice to attach encrypted EBS volumes to EC2 instances to ensure data at rest is always protected.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose EC2. In the Navigation pane, choose "Volumes". Select the unencrypted EBS volume that is attached to an EC2 instance, and create a snapshot of the volume. Go to Snapshots, select the newly created snapshot, and create a copy of the same by choosing the "Encryption" option. Select the newly created encrypted snapshot and create an EBS volume from the snapshot in the same availability zone as the EC2 Instance. Go to Instances, Stop the EC2 Instance. Go to Volumes, detach the unencrypted EBS volume. Select the newly created encrypted volume and attach the same to an EC2 Instance. Note: Ensure to specify the appropriate "Device" name while attaching a volume. e.g., for Linux root volumes specify the "Device" as "/dev/xvda". Go to Instances, Start the EC2 Instance
CQL: EC2Instance should not have attachedEBSVolumes with [encrypted = false ]

Policy: Ensure SQS queues enable a dead letter queue
Description: Dead-letter Queue helps you in troubleshooting the message failures. It isolates the problematic messages to help you determine the reasons for failures. As a best practice, ensure the desired SQS queues enable the dead letter queue.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose SQS service. Select the desired queue, click "Queue Actions" and select "Configure Queue". Under "Dead Letter Queue Settings," check the option "Use Redrive Policy," input the "Dead Letter Queue" name, and set "Maximum Receives" between 1 to 1000. Click on "Save Changes"
CQL: SQS should not have deadLetterQueueArn isEmpty()

Policy: Ensure Amazon SQS queues enable Server-Side Encryption (SSE)
Description: Server-Side Encryption (SSE) protects the messages in SQS queues using AES 256 algorithm. As a security best practice, ensure you enable SSE for SQS queues that store sensitive data and protect the data at rest.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose SQS service. Select the desired queue, click "Queue Actions" and select "Configure Queue". Under "Server-Side Encryption(SSE) Settings," check the option "Use SSE," select the appropriate key under "AWS KMS Customer Master Key (CMK)", and set the "Data Key Reuse Period". Click on "Save Changes"
CQL: SQS should not have kmsMasterKeyId isEmpty()

Policy: Ensure Redshift clusters are encrypted
Description: Encrypted DB instances provide an additional layer of data protection by securing data from unauthorized access to the underlying storage. It is a best practice to encrypt databases to increase the protection of data at rest, and also to fulfill compliance requirements.
Recommendation: Log in to AWS Management Console and open the Amazon Redshift console https://console.aws.amazon.com/redshift/. On the navigation menu, choose "CLUSTERS"; select the desired cluster and click "Actions", "Modify". Under the "Database configurations" section, select the appropriate Encryption option (AWS Key Management Service or Hardware Security Module) and click "Modify cluster"
CQL: RedshiftCluster should have encrypted=true

Policy: Ensure Redshift clusters are not publicly accessible
Description: Granting public access allows unrestricted access to the database from any user or machine on the internet, and can result in malicious attacks. As a security best practice, and to meet the compliance guidelines, ensure the Redshift clusters are not publicly accessible.
Recommendation: Log in to AWS Management Console and open the Amazon Redshift console https://console.aws.amazon.com/redshift/. On the navigation menu, choose "CLUSTERS"; select the desired cluster and click "Actions", "Modify publicly accessible setting". In the Publicly accessible pop-up, select the option "No" and click "Confirm"
CQL: RedshiftCluster should have publiclyAccessible=false

Policy: Ensure Redshift clusters enable audit logging
Description: Database audit logs record the information about the connections and user activities in the database. It helps you to monitor the security of your database and also to troubleshoot any issues.
Recommendation: Log in to AWS Management Console and open the Amazon Redshift console https://console.aws.amazon.com/redshift/. On the navigation menu, choose "CLUSTERS"; click on the name of the desired cluster. In the cluster details view, choose the tab "Maintenance and monitoring", and under the "Audit Logging" section, click "Edit". In the Configure audit logging pop-up, select the option "Yes" to Enable audit logging, and also select the appropriate S3 bucket to store the audit logs, and click "Confirm"
CQL: RedshiftCluster should have loggingEnabled=true

Policy: Ensure Redshift clusters enable SSL for all connections
Description: As a security best practice, enable SSL on Redshift clusters to secure the database connections. Note: If a Redshift cluster is associated with the default parameter group, it is not possible to modify the parameter values. So, first, change them to the non-default parameter group and then set the parameter "require_ssl" to true.
Recommendation: Log in to AWS Management Console and open the Amazon Redshift console https://console.aws.amazon.com/redshift/. On the navigation menu, choose "CLUSTERS"; click on the name of the desired cluster. In the cluster details view, select the tab "Properties", and under the "Database configurations" section, click on the non-default Parameter group. In the Workload management page, for the selected parameter group, select the tab "Parameters" and click "Edit Parameters". Set the "require_ssl" parameter to true and save the configuration
CQL: RedshiftCluster should have parameters with [parameterName='require_ssl' and parameterValue='true']

Policy: Ensure unattached EBS volumes are deleted.
Description: To prevent data leaks and save costs, ensure the unattached EBS volumes are deleted.
Recommendation: Log in to AWS Management Console and go to Amazon EC2 console https://console.aws.amazon.com/ec2/. In the navigation pane, choose "Elastic Block Store", "Volumes". Select the volume with the state as "available" and choose "Actions", "Delete Volume". In the confirmation dialog box, click "Yes, Delete"
CQL: EBSVolume should have state = 'in-use'

Policy: Ensure SQS queues are Encrypted using CMKs
Description: Ensure SQS queues are encrypted using Customer Master Keys instead of default AWS managed keys reserved for SQS service to protect highly sensitive messages and have more control over the encryption and decryption process.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose SQS service. Select the desired queue, click "Queue Actions" and select "Configure Queue". Under "Server-Side Encryption(SSE) Settings," check the option "Use SSE," for "AWS KMS Customer Master Key (CMK)" select one of the listed CMK (not the (Default) aws/sqs) or provide the ARN of the desired key, and set the "Data Key Reuse Period". Click on "Save Changes"
CQL: SQS should not have kmsMasterKeyId isEmpty() or kmsMasterKeyId='alias/aws/sqs'

Policy: Ensure CMKs (Customer Master Keys) are not exposed to everyone
Description: AWS Key Management Service helps you to create and manage CMKs (Customer Master Keys). The CMK is used to encrypt the data across various services like storage, compute, etc. So, allowing anonymous access to your AWS CMKs is a security risk and can lead to data loss.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose "Key Management Service". In the Navigation pane, choose "Customer Managed Keys". Click on "Key ID" of each key reported as violation. Under "Key policy" tab, click on "Edit" and ensure key policy does not contain Effect as "Allow" and Principal as "*" or "AWS: *"
CQL: CMK should not have policyStatement with [(principal='*' or principalAWS contain-any ('*')) and effect='Allow']

Policy: Ensure AMIs are encrypted.
Description: Amazon Machine Image contains information to launch an EC2 instance. The AMIs that are encrypted always create an EC2 instance with encrypted root volumes. It is recommended to encrypt AMIs to protect sensitive information from unauthorized access.
Recommendation: Log in to Log in to AWS Management Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose EC2 service. In the navigation pane, choose AMIs. Select the AMI, go to "Actions", "Copy AMI". In the Copy AMI dialog box, check the "Encryption" option and select the appropriate Master Key and click "Copy AMI". Once the new encrypted image is created successfully, deregister the old un-encrypted image
CQL: AMI should have blockDeviceMappings with [ebsEncrypted = true]

Policy: Ensure AMIs are not Publicly Accessible.
Description: AMIs with public access can be accessed by any AWS account to create EC2 instances. And thus can expose any sensitive details stored as part of an image. So always ensure the AMIs are not configured with Public access.
Recommendation: Log in to Amazon Console https://console.aws.amazon.com/, Choose EC2 service. In the navigation pane, choose AMIs. Select the AMI, go to "Actions", "Modify Image Permissions". Select the option "Private" and click Save
CQL: AMI should not have public = true

Policy: Storage accounts without SSL access
Description: The "secure transfer" option enforces the SSL communication between the clients (users, applications) and storage accounts. It denies any unencrypted HTTP requests and protects data from eavesdropping and man-in-the-middle (MITM) attacks. It is best practice to enable SSL access on storage accounts hosting sensitive and private data.
Recommendation: Login in to Azure Portal https://portal.azure.com. Navigate to "Storage accounts" and select the storage account. Click on the "configuration.". Set "Secure transfer required" to Enabled
CQL: StorageAccount should have enableHttpsTrafficOnly=true

Policy: Storage accounts without default access rule set as deny
Description: It is recommended to restrict access to storage accounts from specific virtual networks. It helps in building a secure network boundary for your applications.
Recommendation: Login in to Azure Portal https://portal.azure.com. Navigate to "Storage accounts" and select the storage account. Click on the "Firewalls and virtual networks.". Ensure "Allow access from" is set for "Selected networks."
CQL: StorageAccount should not have networkRuleDefaultAction='Allow'

Policy: Storage accounts without access to trusted microsoft services
Description: Some Microsoft services that interact with storage accounts operate from networks that can not be granted access through network rules. So, to take benefits of all applicable Microsoft services like Azure Backup, Azure Data Box, Azure DevTest Labs, Azure Monitor etc...on storage accounts, it is recommended to add an exception to allow the set of trusted Microsoft services to bypass the network rules.
Recommendation: Login in to Azure Portal https://portal.azure.com. Navigate to "Storage accounts" and select the storage account. Click on the "Firewalls and virtual networks.". Click on "Selected networks.". Ensure "Allow trusted Microsoft services to access this storage account" is checked under "Exceptions."
CQL: StorageAccount should have networkRuleByPass='AzureServices'

Policy: SQLServers without Auditing
Description: Enabling Auditing on SQL server ensures that all existing and newly created databases on the SQL server instance are audited. It is a security best practice to enable auditing at SQL server to track all database events.
Recommendation: Login into Azure Portal https://portal.azure.com. Navigate to "SQL servers.". For each SQL server, click on "Auditing.". Ensure "Auditing" is set to ON
CQL: SQLServer should have auditState='Enabled'

Policy: SQLServers without appropriate AuditActionGroups
Description: The SQL Server Audit feature enables you to audit server-level and database-level groups of events and individual events. It is recommended to have AuditActionGroup with at least 3 groups -  SUCCESSFUL_DATABASE_AUTHENTICATION_GROUP, FAILED_DATABASE_AUTHENTICATION_GROUP & BATCH_COMPLETED_GROUP.
Recommendation: There is NO support to configure the AuditActionGroup from Azure Console. It should ideally be set by default to include SUCCESSFUL_DATABASE_AUTHENTICATION_GROUP, FAILED_DATABASE_AUTHENTICATION_GROUP, BATCH_COMPLETED_GROUP
CQL: SQLServer should have auditActionAndGroups contain ('SUCCESSFUL_DATABASE_AUTHENTICATION_GROUP', 'FAILED_DATABASE_AUTHENTICATION_GROUP', 'BATCH_COMPLETED_GROUP')

Policy: SQLServers without audit retention greated than 90 days
Description: Audit logs provide detailed information regarding threats, vulnerabilities, and anomalies in data access patterns. It is best practice to set "Audit Retention" greater than 90 days.
Recommendation: Login into Azure Portal https://portal.azure.com. Navigate to "SQL servers.". For each SQL server, click on "Auditing. Select "Storage details.". Ensure "Retention (Days)" is greater than 90 days
CQL: SQLServer should have auditRetentionDays>90

Policy: SQLServers without Advanced Data Security
Description: Advanced data security (ADS) provides a set of advanced SQL security capabilities. It is recommended to set SQL ADS to ON to take benefits of vulnerability assessment, and superior threat protection.
Recommendation: Login into Azure Portal https://portal.azure.com. Navigate to "SQL servers. For each SQL server, click on "Advanced Data Security.". Ensure "ADVANCED DATA SECURITY" is set to ON
CQL: SQLServer should have securityAlertPolicies with [state='Enabled']

Policy: SQLServers without ALL types of Threat Detections
Description: Advanced Threat Protection enables customers to detect and respond to potential threats as they occur by providing security alerts on anomalous activities. It is a best practice to configure advanced threat protection for ALL types to receive an alert upon suspicious database activities, potential vulnerabilities, SQL injection attacks, anomalous database access, and queries patterns.
Recommendation: Login into Azure Portal https://portal.azure.com. Navigate to "SQL servers.". For each SQL server, click on "Advanced Data Security.". Ensure "ADVANCED DATA SECURITY" is set to ON. Under "ADVANCED THREAT PROTECTION SETTINGS", ensure "Advanced Threat Protection types" is set to ALL
CQL: SQLServer should have securityAlertPolicies with [state='Enabled' AND disabledAlerts isEmpty()]

Policy: SQLServers configured without email configurations to receive alerts on Advanced Data security
Description: It is recommended to configure email addresses to receive alerts on anomalous activities on SQL servers. It will ensure the detection of unusual events as soon as they occur and helps in taking necessary mitigation steps to reduce the risk.
Recommendation: Login into Azure Portal https://portal.azure.com. Navigate to "SQL servers.". For each SQL server, click on "Advanced Data Security.". Ensure "ADVANCED DATA SECURITY" is set to ON. Under "ADVANCED THREAT PROTECTION SETTINGS", ensure that "Send alerts to" is configured with appropriate email addresses
CQL: SQLServer should have securityAlertPolicies with [state='Enabled' AND emailAddresses isNotEmpty()]

Policy: SQLServers without Email notification to admins and Subscription owners under Advanced Data Security
Description: It is recommended to enable email notifications to admins and subscription owners to receive alerts on anomalous activities on SQL servers. It will ensure the detection of unusual events as soon as they occur and helps in taking necessary mitigation steps to reduce the risk.
Recommendation: Login into Azure Portal https://portal.azure.com. Navigate to "SQL servers.". For each SQL server, click on "Advanced Data Security.". Ensure "ADVANCED DATA SECURITY" is set to ON. Under "ADVANCED THREAT PROTECTION SETTINGS", ensure that email notification to admins and subscription owners is enabled
CQL: SQLServer should have securityAlertPolicies with [state='Enabled' AND emailAccountAdmins=true]

Policy: SQLServers without Azure Active Directory Admin
Description: Azure Active Directory authentication is a mechanism of connecting to Azure SQL Database and SQL Data Warehouse using identities in Azure Active Directory (Azure AD). It is recommended to set Azure Active Directory Admin to centrally manage the identities of database users and other Microsoft services in one central location.
Recommendation: Login into Azure Portal https://portal.azure.com. Navigate to "SQL servers.". For each SQL Server, click on "Active Directory Admin.". Click on "Set admin.". Select the admin and Save
CQL: SQLServer should have activeDirectoryAdmin isNotEmpty()

Policy: SQLServers exposed to Internet
Description: It is a best practice to accept connections at SQL databases only from specific trusted IP addresses and block access to the external world.
Recommendation: Login into Azure Portal https://portal.azure.com. Navigate to "SQL servers.". For each SQL Server, click on "Firewalls and virtual networks.". Ensure that "Allow access to Azure services" is set to OFF. Ensure that no firewall rule exists with "Start IP" as 0.0.0.0. And also other combinations that allow wider public IP ranges
CQL: SQLServer should not have firewallRules with [startIpAddress='0.0.0.0']

Policy: Virtual Machines that allow access to port 22 from external sources.
Description: SSH (Secure Shell) port - 22 is used to get CLI access to Linux Instances. Allowing Inbound traffic from all the external IP addresses to SSH port is vulnerable to “banner grabbing” and “brute force attack.” It is a best practice to restrict access from specific IP addresses to port 22.
Recommendation: Login into Azure Portal https://portal.azure.com. Navigate to "Virtual machines". For each virtual machine, Click on "Networking". Ensure there are no Inbound port rules from 0.0.0.0/0 to port 22
CQL: VirtualMachine where isPublic = true should not have publicInboundRules with [protocol in ('tcp', 'udp') and port = 22]

Policy: Virtual Machines that allow access to port 3389 from external sources.
Description: RDP (Remote Desktop Protocol) port - 3389 is used to get remote control access to Windows Instances. Allowing Inbound traffic from any external IP address to RDP port is vulnerable to “remote code execution,” “privilege elevation,” and “flooding attack.” It is a best practice to restrict access from specific IP addresses to port 3389.
Recommendation: Login into Azure Portal https://portal.azure.com. Navigate to "Virtual machines". For each virtual machine, Click on "Networking". Ensure there are no Inbound port rules from 0.0.0.0/0 to port 3389
CQL: VirtualMachine where isPublic = true and osDiskType = 'Windows' should not have publicInboundRules with [protocol in ('tcp', 'udp') and port = 3389]

Policy: Virtual Machines that allow access to port 445 from external sources.
Description: SMB (Server Message Block) Port - 445 is used for file sharing and TCP/IP networking on Windows 2000 and later versions. Allowing Inbound traffic from any IP address on TCP port 445 can make it vulnerable to WannaCry exploit on Microsoft Endpoints. It is a best practice to block port 445 from the public internet.
Recommendation: Login into Azure Portal https://portal.azure.com. Navigate to "Virtual machines". For each virtual machine, Click on "Networking". Ensure there are no Inbound port rules from 0.0.0.0/0 to port 445
CQL: VirtualMachine where isPublic = true and osDiskType = 'Windows' should not have publicInboundRules with [protocol in ('tcp', 'udp') and port = 445]

Policy: Virtual Machines that allow access to port 8080 from external sources.
Description: Internal web port  8080 is used for web applications and proxy services. Allowing Inbound traffic from any IP address to TCP port 8080 is vulnerable to exploits like backdoor trojan attacks. It is a best practice to block port 8080 from the public internet.
Recommendation: Login into Azure Portal https://portal.azure.com. Navigate to "Virtual machines". For each virtual machine, Click on "Networking". Ensure there are no Inbound port rules from 0.0.0.0/0 to port 8080
CQL: VirtualMachine where isPublic = true should not have publicInboundRules with [protocol in ('tcp', 'udp') and port = 8080]

Policy: Virtual Machines that allow access to port 636 from external sources.
Description: LDAP SSL port - 636 is used for Secure LDAP authentication. Allowing Inbound traffic from any IP address to TCP port 636 is vulnerable to DoS attacks. It is a best practice to block port 636 from the public internet.
Recommendation: Login into Azure Portal https://portal.azure.com. Navigate to "Virtual machines". For each virtual machine, Click on "Networking". Ensure there are no Inbound port rules from 0.0.0.0/0 to port 636
CQL: VirtualMachine where isPublic = true should not have publicInboundRules with [protocol in ('tcp', 'udp') and port = 636]

Policy: Virtual Machines that allow access to port 1434 from external sources.
Description: MSSQL port - 1434 is used by the SQL Server to listen to requests from SQL server clients. Allowing Inbound traffic from any external IP address on MSSQL port is vulnerable to DoS, Buffer Overflow, SQL Injection attacks. It is a best practice to restrict access from specific IP addresses to port 1434.
Recommendation: Login into Azure Portal https://portal.azure.com. Navigate to "Virtual machines". For each virtual machine, Click on "Networking". Ensure there are no Inbound port rules from 0.0.0.0/0 to port 1434
CQL: VirtualMachine where isPublic = true should not have publicInboundRules with [protocol in ('tcp', 'udp') and port = 1434]

Policy: Virtual Machines that allow access to port 135 from external sources.
Description: Remote Procedure Call (RPC) protocol provides an inter-process communication mechanism that allows a program running on one computer to execute code on another remote system seamlessly. Using the RPC protocol, an attacker can take any action on your machine, like installing programs, viewing, changing or deleting data, or creating new accounts with full privileges. It is a best practice to block RPC port 135 from the public internet.
Recommendation: Login into Azure Portal https://portal.azure.com. Navigate to "Virtual machines". For each virtual machine, Click on "Networking". Ensure there are no Inbound port rules from 0.0.0.0/0 to port 135
CQL: VirtualMachine where isPublic = true and osDiskType = 'Windows' should not have publicInboundRules with [protocol in ('tcp', 'udp') and port = 135]

Policy: Virtual Machines that allow access to port 1433 from external sources.
Description: MSSQL server port - 1433 is used by the SQL Server to listen to requests from SQL server clients. Allowing Inbound traffic from any external IP address on MSSQL port is vulnerable to exploits like DoS, Buffer Overflow, SQL Injection attacks. It is a best practice to restrict access from specific IP addresses to port 1433.
Recommendation: Login into Azure Portal https://portal.azure.com. Navigate to "Virtual machines". For each virtual machine, Click on "Networking". Ensure there are no Inbound port rules from 0.0.0.0/0 to port 1433
CQL: VirtualMachine where isPublic = true should not have publicInboundRules with [protocol in ('tcp', 'udp') and port = 1433]

Policy: Virtual Machines that allow access to port 11211 from external sources.
Description: Memcached port - 11211 is used for the caching system. It is used to reduce response times and the load on components. Allowing Inbound traffic from any external IP address on the Memcached port is vulnerable to DoS attacks. It is a best practice to restrict access from specific IP addresses to port 11211.
Recommendation: Login into Azure Portal https://portal.azure.com. Navigate to "Virtual machines". For each virtual machine, Click on "Networking". Ensure there are no Inbound port rules from 0.0.0.0/0 to port 11211
CQL: VirtualMachine where isPublic = true should not have publicInboundRules with [protocol in ('tcp', 'udp') and port = 11211]

Policy: Virtual Machines that allow access to port 27018 from external sources.
Description: The port 27018 is the default port for MongoDB when running with-shardsvr command-line option or the shardsvr value for the clusterRole setting in a configuration file. It is a security risk to expose DB port to the public internet even though they are on a secure socket layer. Anyone on the internet can run port scanning tools, determine the open ports, and launch specific attacks. It is a best practice to block public access, and restrict access from specific IP addresses to port 27018 and make the connection secure.
Recommendation: Login into Azure Portal https://portal.azure.com. Navigate to "Virtual machines". For each virtual machine, Click on "Networking". Ensure there are no Inbound port rules from 0.0.0.0/0 to port 27018
CQL: VirtualMachine where isPublic = true should not have publicInboundRules with [protocol in ('tcp', 'udp') and port = 27018]

Policy: Virtual Machines that allow access to port 3306 from external sources.

It is a best practice to block public access, and restrict access from specific IP addresses to port 27017 and make the connection secure.

Policy: Network Security Groups allowing ingress traffic from 0.0.0.0/0
Description: Allowing Inbound traffic from any IP address can lead to attacks like DoS, Brute Force, Smurf, and reconnaissance. It is a best practice to restrict inbound traffic from the required source IP address(es).
Recommendation: Login into Azure Portal https://portal.azure.com. Navigate to "Network Security Groups" and select the network security group. For each network security group, Click on "Inbound security rules". Ensure there are no Inbound rule with source as "Any"
CQL: NetworkSecurityGroup should not have securityRules with [direction = 'Inbound' and access = 'Allow' and sourceAddressPrefix = '*']

Policy: Network Security Groups allowing egress traffic to 0.0.0.0/0
Description: Allowing Outbound traffic to any IP address can lead to internal resources accessing unwanted and untrusted resources. If a system is compromised, an attacker can use it to exfiltrate data or conduct spam or phishing campaigns. It is a best practice to restrict outbound traffic to only required IP address(es).
Recommendation: Login into Azure Portal https://portal.azure.com. Navigate to "Network Security Groups" and select the network security group. For each network security group, Click on "Outbound security rules". Ensure there are no outbound rule with source as "Any"
CQL: NetworkSecurityGroup should not have securityRules with [direction = 'Outbound' and access = 'Allow' and destinationAddressPrefix = '*']

Policy: Network Security Groups allowing ingress traffic to ALL ports
Description: Allowing Inbound traffic to ALL ports increases the attack surface of your environment. It is a best practice to follow the principle of least privilege, and allow access to only required ports.
Recommendation: Login into Azure Portal https://portal.azure.com. Navigate to "Network Security Groups" and select the network security group. For each network security group, Click on "Inbound security rules". Ensure there is no rule granting access to port range 0-65535
CQL: NetworkSecurityGroup should not have securityRules with [direction = 'Inbound' and access = 'Allow' and sourcePortRange in ('*', '0-65535')]

Policy: Network Security Groups allowing egress traffic to ALL ports
Description: Allowing Outbound traffic to ALL ports can lead to internal resources accessing unwanted and untrusted resources. It is a best practice to follow the principle of least privilege, and allow access to only required ports.
Recommendation: Login into Azure Portal https://portal.azure.com. Navigate to "Network Security Groups" and select the network security group. For each network security group, Click on "Outbound security rules". Ensure there is no rule granting access to port range 0-65535
CQL: NetworkSecurityGroup should not have securityRules with [direction = 'Outbound' and access = 'Allow' and destinationPortRange in ('*', '0-65535')]

Policy: Virtual Machines missing tags
Description: Tags help you to group resources together that are related to or associated with each other. It is a best practice to tag cloud resources to better organize and gain visibility into their usage.
Recommendation: Login into Azure Portal https://portal.azure.com. Navigate to "Virtual machines". Select the violated Virtual machine, Click on "Tags". Add tags and Save the configuration
CQL: VirtualMachine should not have tags isEmpty()

Policy: Storage accounts missing tags
Description: Tags help you to group resources together that are related to or associated with each other. It is a best practice to tag cloud resources to better organize and gain visibility into their usage.
Recommendation: Login into Azure Portal https://portal.azure.com. Navigate to "Storage accounts". Select the violated Storage account, Click on "Tags". Add tags and Save the configuration
CQL: StorageAccount should not have tags isEmpty()

CQL: SQLServer should not have tags isEmpty()

Policy: Virtual Machines without OSDisk Encryption
Description: It is a security best practice to encrypt Virtual machines OS disk (boot volume) to ensure data at rest is always protected.
Recommendation: Disk encryption at Azure requires the KeyVault to be set up as a prerequisite. Follow the link to set up key vault if not done already https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/security/azure-security-disk-encryption-prerequisites#prerequisite-workflow-for-key-vault. Follow the instructions below to encrypt Linux IaaS VMs https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/security/azure-security-disk-encryption-linux. Follow the instructions below to encrypt Windows IaaS VMs https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/security/azure-security-disk-encryption-windows
CQL: VirtualMachine should have osDiskEncryptionEnabled = true

Policy: Virtual Machines without Data Disks Encryption
Description: Sensitive information stored on systems shall be encrypted at rest. It is a security best practice to encrypt Virtual machines Data Disks to ensure data at rest is always protected.
Recommendation: Disk encryption at Azure requires the KeyVault to be set up as a prerequisite. Follow the link to set up key vault if not done already https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/security/azure-security-disk-encryption-prerequisites#prerequisite-workflow-for-key-vault. Follow the instructions below to encrypt Linux IaaS VMs https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/security/azure-security-disk-encryption-linux. Follow the instructions below to encrypt Windows IaaS VMs https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/security/azure-security-disk-encryption-windows
CQL: VirtualMachine should have allDataDiskEncryptionEnabled = true

Policy: Unattached Disks without Encryption
Description: In order to avoid any sensitive information leaks, it is recommended to have the unattached disks encrypted, or deleted.
Recommendation: Please go through the links below to either encrypt or delete the unattached disks. https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/rest/api/compute/disks/delete. https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/cli/azure/disk?view=azure-cli-latest#az-disk-delete. https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/rest/api/compute/disks/update#encryptionsettings. https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/cli/azure/disk?view=azure-cli-latest#az-disk-update
CQL: Disk where diskState != 'Attached' should have encryptionEnabled = true

Policy: Disks missing tags
Description: Tags help you to group resources together that are related to or associated with each other. It is a best practice to tag cloud resources to better organize and gain visibility into their usage.
Recommendation: Login into Azure Portal https://portal.azure.com. Navigate to "Disks". Select the violated Disk, Click on "Tags". Add tags and Save the configuration
CQL: Disk should not have tags isEmpty()

Policy: Subscription missing Log Profile
Description: The Azure Activity Log provides insight into subscription-level events that have occurred in Azure. They can be used to determine any write operations taken on the resources in your subscription. However, they are stored only for 90 days. It is a best practice to archive the Activity Log for a more extended period using Log Profiles for audit, static analysis, and backup.
Recommendation: Login into Azure Portal https://portal.azure.com. Navigate to "Activity Log". Click on "Export to Event Hub". Select all regions, and configure export to the storage account. Set Retention(days) as 365, or 0 and click Save
CQL: Subscription should have isLogProfilePresent = true

Policy: Activity log retention should be greater than 365 days
Description: Log profile provides a mechanism to export the activity log and store the same for a more extended period. To identify any security incidents, it is a best practice to set a retention period as 365 days or more for log profiles.
Recommendation: Login into Azure Portal https://portal.azure.com. Navigate to "Activity Log". Click on "Export to Event Hub". Select all regions, and configure export to the storage account. Set Retention(days) as 365, or 0 and click Save
CQL: LogProfile should have retentionDays >= 365 or retentionDays = 0

Policy: Log profile to capture all activities
Description: Log profile provides a mechanism to export the activity log and store the same for a more extended period. It is a best practice to configure log profile to include records for the operations "write," "delete," and "action" to ensure all control and management activities at subscription are exported.
Recommendation: It is possible to update log profile only through CLI. Refer the below documentation and use CLI to update log profile to capture operations "write", "delete" and "action". https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/cli/azure/monitor/log-profiles?view=azure-cli-latest#az-monitor-log-profiles-update
CQL: LogProfile should have categories contain ('Write', 'Delete', 'Action')

Policy: Log profile should capture activity logs across all regions
Description: Log profile provides a mechanism to export the activity log and store the same for a more extended period. It is a best practice to configure log profile to include records for all regions/locations. It will ensure the logs from even unused regions are stored and available for any incident response and investigations.
Recommendation: Login into Azure Portal https://portal.azure.com. Navigate to "Activity Log". Click on "Export to Event Hub". Select all regions, and configure export to the storage account
CQL: LogProfile should have locations contain-any ('global')

Policy: Activity Log Alert on Policy Assignments
Description: Activity log alerts are the alerts that get activated when a new activity log event occurs that matches the conditions specified in the alert. It is recommended to monitor the create policy assignment events to detect any undesirable policy assignments.
Recommendation: Login into Azure Portal https://portal.azure.com. Navigate to "Monitor > Alerts". Click "+ New alert rule". Under RESOURCE, select the Subscription. Under  CONDITION, select "Create policy assignment (Microsoft.Authorization/policyAssignments)", and Alert logic as Event Level: "All", Status: "All", Event initiated by: " or empty. Under ACTIONS, select an appropriate action group. Under ALERT DETAILS provide rule name, description, resource group and enable the rule upon creation
CQL: Subscription should have activityLogAlerts with [operationName = 'Microsoft.Authorization/policyAssignments/write' and actionsConfigured = true]
