Policy: MySQL Server missing SSL enforcement
Description: It is a security best practice to enable SSL connection at the MySQL server to enforce secure connections, and avoid any man-in-the-middle attacks.
Recommendation: Login into Azure Portal https://portal.azure.com. Navigate to "Azure Database for MySQL servers". Select each database and click on "Connection security". Under SSL Settings, ensure Enable SSL connection is set to "Enabled"
CQL: MySQLServer should have sslEnforcement = 'Enabled'

Policy: PostgreSQL Server missing SSL enforcement
Description: It is a security best practice to enable SSL connection at the PostgreSQL Database server to enforce secure connections, and avoid any man-in-the-middle attacks.
Recommendation: Login into Azure Portal https://portal.azure.com. Navigate to "Azure Database for PostgreSQL servers". Select each database and click on "Connection security". Under SSL Settings, ensure Enable SSL connection is set to "Enabled"
CQL: PostgreSQLServer should have sslEnforcement = 'Enabled'

Policy: KeyVault missing logging
Description: It is a security best practice to enable AuditEvent logging for Key Vault instances to ensure all interactions with key vaults are logged and available.
Recommendation: Login into Azure Portal https://portal.azure.com. Navigate to "Key vaults". Select each Key vault and click on "Diagnostic settings". Click "Add diagnostic setting" or Click "Edit setting" if diagnostic setting already created. Enable  "Archive to a storage account". Set "AuditEvent" with Retention as 180 days or as appropriate
CQL: KeyVault should have diagnosticSettings with [logs isNotEmpty()]

Policy: App service web apps should enable HTTPS
Description: Allowing only HTTPS traffic ensures the connection between clients and web apps is secure, and avoid any man-in-the-middle attacks.
Recommendation: Login into Azure Portal https://portal.azure.com. Navigate to "App Services". Select each App, and click on "TLS/SSL settings". Under Protocol Settings, Set "HTTPS Only" to "On"
CQL: WebApp should have httpsOnly = true

Policy: App service web apps should use latest TLS version
Description: It is recommended to set the latest TLS version 1.2 for web apps to secure connections.
Recommendation: Login into Azure Portal https://portal.azure.com. Navigate to "App Services". Select each App, and click on "TLS/SSL settings". Under Protocol Settings, Set "Minimum TLS Version" to "1.2"
CQL: WebApp should have configurations with [minTlsVersion = '1.2']

Policy: App service web apps should enable client certificates
Description: It is recommended to enable client certificates to ensure only an authenticated client with valid certificates can access the web app.
Recommendation: Login into Azure Portal https://portal.azure.com. Navigate to "App Services". Select each App, and click on "TLS/SSL settings". Under Protocol Settings, Set "Incoming client certificates" to "On"
CQL: WebApp should have clientCertEnabled = true

Policy: App service web apps should register with AD authentication
Description: The managed identities in Azure provides a mechanism for web apps to authenticate to any other service that supports Azure AD authentication. It helps web apps to securely connect to Azure services like Key Vault or SQL Servers.
Recommendation: Login into Azure Portal https://portal.azure.com. Navigate to "App Services". Select each App, and click on "Identity". Set "Status" to "On" and Save the configuration
CQL: WebApp should have identityPrincipalId isNotEmpty()

Policy: App service web apps should have latest HTTP version
Description: It is recommended to set the HTTP version to 2.0 for web apps to take advantage of security enhancements and additional functionalities.
Recommendation: Login into Azure Portal https://portal.azure.com. Navigate to "App Services". Select each App, and click on "Configuration". Select tab "General Settings", and set "HTTP version" to "2.0"
CQL: WebApp should have configurations with [http20Enabled = true]

Policy: SQL server’s TDE protector should be encrypted with BYOK
Description: Transparent data encryption (TDE) protects Azure SQL Database, Azure SQL Managed Instance, and Azure Data Warehouse by encrypting data at rest using a symmetric key called database encryption key (DEK). And DEK is protected by the transparent data encryption protector. For critical data, it is recommended to protect DEK using a user-owned asymmetric key stored in KeyVault.
Recommendation: Login into Azure Portal https://portal.azure.com. Navigate to "SQL servers". For the desired SQL server instance, click on "Transparent data encryption". Ensure Use your own key is set to "Yes", and select the desired key from Key vault. Ensure the checkbox "Make the selected key the default TDE protector" is checked
CQL: SQLServer should have encryptionProtectors with [kind='azurekeyvault' and serverKeyType='AzureKeyVault' and uri isNotEmpty()]

Policy: PostgreSQL server should enable log_checkpoints
Description: Enable "log_checkpoints" to log each checkpoint and restartpoints in the server log. It helps in identifying, troubleshooting, and repairing configuration errors.
Recommendation: Login into Azure Portal https://portal.azure.com. Navigate to "Azure Database for PostgreSQL servers". Select each database and click on "Server parameters". Ensure parameter with name "log_checkpoints" is turned "ON"
CQL: PostgreSQLServer should have configurations with [configName='log_checkpoints' and configValue='on']

Policy: PostgreSQL server should enable log_connections
Description: Enable "log_connections" to log each attempted connection to the server, and also successful completion of client authentication.
Recommendation: Login into Azure Portal https://portal.azure.com. Navigate to "Azure Database for PostgreSQL servers". Select each database and click on "Server parameters". Ensure parameter with name "log_connections" is turned "ON"
CQL: PostgreSQLServer should have configurations with [configName='log_connections' and configValue='on']

Policy: PostgreSQL server should enable log_disconnections
Description: Enable "log_disconnections" to log session terminations and the duration of the session.
Recommendation: Login into Azure Portal https://portal.azure.com. Navigate to "Azure Database for PostgreSQL servers". Select each database and click on "Server parameters". Ensure parameter with name "log_disconnections" is turned "ON"
CQL: PostgreSQLServer should have configurations with [configName='log_disconnections' and configValue='on']

Policy: PostgreSQL server should enable log_duration
Description: Enable "log_duration" to log the duration of every completed statement.
Recommendation: Login into Azure Portal https://portal.azure.com. Navigate to "Azure Database for PostgreSQL servers". Select each database and click on "Server parameters". Ensure parameter with name "log_duration" is turned "ON"
CQL: PostgreSQLServer should have configurations with [configName='log_duration' and configValue='on']

Policy: PostgreSQL server should enable connection_throttling
Description: It is recommended to configure "connection_throttling" to enable the throttling of connections per IP for too many invalid password login failures.
Recommendation: Login into Azure Portal https://portal.azure.com. Navigate to "Azure Database for PostgreSQL servers". Select each database and click on "Server parameters". Ensure parameter with name "connection_throttling" is turned "ON"
CQL: PostgreSQLServer should have configurations with [configName='connection_throttling' and configValue='on']

Policy: PostgreSQL server should enable log_retention_days
Description: Enable "log_retention_days" to set the number of days a log file is retained.
Recommendation: Login into Azure Portal https://portal.azure.com. Navigate to "Azure Database for PostgreSQL servers". Select each database and click on "Server parameters". Ensure parameter with name "log_retention_days" is set to 3 days
CQL: PostgreSQLServer should have configurations with [configName='log_retention_days' and configValue!='0']

Policy: Keyvault is recoverable using a soft delete option
Description: The Key vault contains objects like keys, secrets, and certificates. These objects are used to encrypt StorageAccounts, SQL databases, and other dependent services. Any accidental deletion of Key vault can lead to immediate data loss as the protected resources are not accessible. So, It is recommended to enable soft-delete on Key vault to recover deleted Key vault or its objects within 90 days from deletion.
Recommendation: Azure portal does not provide option to enable soft-delete on Key vault. Use Azure CLI and run the following command by replacing subscriptionID, resourceGroupName and keyVaultName. az resource update --id /subscriptions/<subscriptionID>/resourceGroups/<resourceGroupName>/providers/Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults/<keyVaultName> --set properties.enableSoftDelete=true
CQL: KeyVault should have enableSoftDelete=true

Policy: Keyvault is recoverable using a purge protection option
Description: The Key vault contains objects like keys, secrets, and certificates. These objects are used to encrypt StorageAccounts, SQL databases, and other dependent services. It is possible to purge key vault objects after they are marked for deletion. So, It is recommended to enable purge-protection to avoid permanent removal of Key vault objects. And with purge-protection on, a vault or an object in the deleted state cannot be purged until the retention period of 90 days has passed.
Recommendation: Azure portal does not provide option to enable soft delete on Key vault. Use Azure CLI and run the following command by replacing subscriptionID, resourceGroupName and keyVaultName. az resource update --id /subscriptions/<subscriptionID>/resourceGroups/<resourceGroupName>/providers/Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults/<keyVaultName> --set properties.enablePurgeProtection=true
CQL: KeyVault should have enablePurgeProtection=true

Policy: Enable RBAC within Azure Kubernetes services
Description: Role-based access control (RBAC) helps in defining granular level access to  Kubernetes instance and the resources managed within the Kubernetes.
Recommendation: It is not possible to enable RBAC once the Kubernetes service is deployed. Recreate the cluster by enabling RBAC
CQL: KubernetesService should have enableRBAC=true

Policy: Kubernetes services should have tags
Description: Tags help you to group resources together that are related to or associated with each other. It is a best practice to tag cloud resources to better organize and gain visibility into their usage.
Recommendation: Login into Azure Portal https://portal.azure.com. Navigate to "Kubernetes services". Select the violated Kubernetes service, Click on "Tags". Add tags and Save the configuration
CQL: KubernetesService should not have tags isEmpty()

Policy: MySQLServes should have tags
Description: Tags help you to group resources together that are related to or associated with each other. It is a best practice to tag cloud resources to better organize and gain visibility into their usage.

Policy: Virtual Machines exposed to public
Description: Allowing access from all IP addresses on the Internet to Virtual Machines is risky as it can lead to Brute Force or DoS attacks. It is a best practice to follow the principle of least privilege, and allow access to only required ports.
Recommendation: Login into Azure Portal https://portal.azure.com. Navigate to "Virtual machines". For each virtual machine, Click on "Networking". Ensure there are no Inbound port rules from 0.0.0.0/0 to port 0-65535
CQL: VirtualMachine should not have isPublic=true

Policy: Ensure CosmosDB has tags
Description: Tags help you to group resources together that are related to or associated with each other. It is a best practice to tag cloud resources to better organize and gain visibility into their usage.
Recommendation: Login into Azure Portal https://portal.azure.com. Navigate to Azure Cosmos DB. Select the violated Cosmos DB, Click on "Tags". Add tags and save the configuration
CQL: CosmosDB should not have tags isEmpty()

Policy: Ensure Cosmos DB account is not exposed to the Internet
Description: Database stores sensitive and confidential information about your applications. Allowing Inbound traffic from external addresses to the database ports can lead to attacks like DoS, Brute Force, Smurf, Reconnaissance, and can result in data loss. It is a best practice to block public access and restrict the Inbound traffic from specific addresses.
Recommendation: Login into Azure Portal https://portal.azure.com. Navigate to Azure Cosmos DB. Select the desired Cosmos DB, click on "Firewall and virtual networks". Under "Allow access from" ensure "Selected networks" option is selected, and add the specific IP address or CIDR ranges for access, and Save the configuration
CQL: CosmosDB should not have ipRangeFilter =''

Policy: EC2Instance should not have tags with key like name and value like demo
CQL: EC2Instance should not have tags with [key = 'name' and value like '&demo&']

Policy: VPC should not be empty for EC2 Instances
CQL: EC2Instance should have vpc isNotEmpty()

Policy: list S3 bucket without tags
CQL: S3Bucket should not have tags isEmpty()

Policy: Compute Instances that have not blocked project-wide SSH keys
Description: Project-wide SSH keys feature eases SSH Key management by allowing users to login to all the instances within that project without creating a separate key for each VM instance. However, If there is any breach of SSH keys, all the VM Instances are compromised. It is a best practice to create Instance specific SSH keys for accessing the Instances.
Recommendation: Login into GCP console - https://console.cloud.google.com. Navigate to "Compute Engine" and choose "VM Instances". Select the VM instance reported under violation and click "EDIT". Select "Block project-wide SSH keys" and Save the configuration
CQL: VMInstance should have metadata with [key='block-project-ssh-keys' and value='true']

Policy: Compute Instances that have enabled connection to serial ports
Description: Interacting with a serial port is often referred to as the serial console, which is similar to using a terminal window. The interactive serial console does not support IP-based access restrictions such as IP whitelists. If you enable the interactive serial console on an instance, clients can attempt to connect to that instance from any IP address. This allows anybody to connect to that instance if they know the correct SSH key, username, project ID, zone, and instance name.
Recommendation: Login into GCP console - https://console.cloud.google.com. Navigate to "Compute Engine" and choose "VM Instances". Click on the Specific VM instance reported as a violation. Ensure "Enable connecting to serial ports" below "Remote access" block is unselected
CQL: VMInstance should not have metadata with [key='serial-port-enable' and value='true']

Policy: Compute Instances that have not enabled OS login
Description: Enabling osLogin ensures that SSH keys used to connect to instances are mapped with IAM users. Revoking access to IAM user will revoke all the SSH keys associated with that particular user. It facilitates centralized and automated SSH key pair management which is useful in handling cases like response to compromised SSH key pairs and/or revocation of external/third-party/Vendor users.
Recommendation: Login into GCP console - https://console.cloud.google.com. Navigate to "Compute Engine" and choose "Metadata". Click "Edit". Add a metadata entry with key as "enable-oslogin" and the value as TRUE, and save the configuration
CQL: VMInstance should have metadata with [key='enable-oslogin' and value='true']

Policy: Compute Instances that allow access to port 22 from external sources.
Description: SSH (Secure Shell) port - 22 is used to get CLI access to Linux Instances. Allowing Inbound traffic from any external IP address on SSH port is vulnerable to “banner grabbing” and “brute force attack”. It is a best practice to restrict access from specific IP addresses to port 22.
Recommendation: Login into GCP console - https://console.cloud.google.com. Navigate to "Compute Engine" and choose "VM Instances". Select the VM instance reported under violation and click "View details" under Network interfaces. Identify the Firewall rule allowing traffic from 0.0.0.0/0 and replace the rule with specific source IP address or delete the rule
CQL: VMInstance where isPublic=true should not have publicInboundRules with [port=22 and protocol in ('tcp', 'udp')]

Policy: Compute Instances that allow access to port 3389 from external sources.
Description: RDP (Remote Desktop Protocol) port - 3389 is used to get remote control access to Windows Instances. Allowing Inbound traffic from any external IP address to RDP port is vulnerable to “remote code execution”, “privilege elevation” and “flooding attack”. It is a best practice to restrict access from specific IP addresses to port 3389.

Identify the Firewall rule allowing traffic from 0.0.0.0/0 and replace the rule with specific source IP address or delete the rule
CQL: VMInstance where isPublic=true and platform='Windows' should not have publicInboundRules with [port=3389 and protocol in ('tcp', 'udp')]

Allowing Inbound traffic from any IP address on TCP port 445 can make it vulnerable to WannaCry exploit on Microsoft Endpoints. It is a best practice to block port 445 from the public internet.

Select the VM instance reported under violation and click "View details" under Network interfaces. CQL: VMInstance where isPublic=true and platform='Windows' should not have publicInboundRules with [port=445 and protocol in ('tcp', 'udp')]

Identify the Firewall rule allowing traffic from 0.0.0.0/0 and replace the rule with specific source IP address or delete the rule
CQL: VMInstance where isPublic=true should not have publicInboundRules with [port=5432 and protocol in ('tcp', 'udp')]

Policy: Compute Instances that allow access to port 161 from external sources.
Description: SNMP UDP 161 used by various devices and applications for logging events, monitoring and management. Allowing Inbound traffic from any external IP address on port 161 is vulnerable to  DoS attack. It is a best practice to block port 161 completely unless explicitly required.
Recommendation: Login into GCP console - https://console.cloud.google.com. Navigate to "Compute Engine" and choose "VM Instances". Select the VM instance reported under violation and click "View details" under Network interfaces. Identify the Firewall rule allowing traffic from 0.0.0.0/0 and replace the rule with specific source IP address or delete the rule
CQL: VMInstance where isPublic=true should not have publicInboundRules with [port=161 and protocol in ('tcp', 'udp')]

Policy: Compute Instances that allow access to port 389 from external sources.

Recommendation: Login into GCP console - https://console.cloud.google.com. Navigate to "Compute Engine" and choose "VM Instances". CQL: VMInstance where isPublic=true should not have publicInboundRules with [port=389 and protocol in ('tcp', 'udp')]

Description: FTP (File Transfer Protocol) port - 20 is used to setup FTP connection between the client and the server. Allowing Inbound traffic from any external IP address on FTP port makes it vulnerable to “FTP brute force attack”, “Packet Sniffing” using Man-In-The-Middle (MITM) attack. It is a best practice to restrict access from specific IP addresses to port 20.

CQL: VMInstance where isPublic=true should not have publicInboundRules with [port=20 and protocol in ('tcp', 'udp')]

Allowing Inbound traffic from any external IP address on FTP port makes it vulnerable to “FTP brute force attack”, “Packet Sniffing” using Man-In-The-Middle (MITM) attack. CQL: VMInstance where isPublic=true should not have publicInboundRules with [port=23 and protocol in ('tcp', 'udp')]

Policy: Compute Instances that allow access to port 25 from external sources.

CQL: VMInstance where isPublic=true should not have publicInboundRules with [port=53 and protocol in ('tcp', 'udp')]

Policy: Compute Instances that allow access to port 1521 from external sources.

Allowing inbound traffic from any external IP address on TNS port will let commands to be sent to the listener, the listener can be shut down, or the databases can be queried. It is a best practice to restrict access from specific IP addresses to port 1521.

CQL: VMInstance where isPublic=true should not have publicInboundRules with [port=1521 and protocol in ('tcp', 'udp')]

Policy: Compute Instances that allow access to port 27017 from external sources.
Description: The port 27017 is the default port for mongod and mongose instances. It is a security risk to expose DB port to the public internet even though they are on a secure socket layer. Anyone on the internet can run port scanning tools, determine the open ports and launch specific attacks. It is a best practice to block public access, and restrict access from specific IP addresses to port 27017 and make the connection secure
Recommendation: Login into GCP console - https://console.cloud.google.com. Navigate to "Compute Engine" and choose "VM Instances". Select the VM instance reported under violation and click "View details" under Network interfaces. Identify the Firewall rule allowing traffic from 0.0.0.0/0 and replace the rule with specific source IP address or delete the rule
CQL: VMInstance where isPublic=true should not have publicInboundRules with [port=27017 and protocol in ('tcp', 'udp')]

Policy: IAM users with non-corporate mail accounts.
Description: Gmail accounts are personally created and managed accounts. Organizations don't have any control over them. It is a best practice to use corporate email accounts for better visibility, auditing and control over access to GCP resources.
Recommendation: Setup corporate login accounts and use them for onboarding users to GCP platform
CQL: IAMUser should not have name like '%gmail.com'

Policy: Service accounts with admin privileges.
Description: Service Account acts as identity for the applications to authenticate to Google cloud platform. Enrolling ServiceAccount with Admin rights gives full access to the assigned resource. It is a best practice to grant only required specific access to the service account and not grant Admin access.
Recommendation: Login into GCP console - https://console.cloud.google.com. Navigate to "IAM & admin" and choose "IAM". For each service account reported as vulnerable, click edit icon and delete the corresponding *Admin roles
CQL: ServiceAccount where type='user_managed' should not have roles contain-any ('roles/%Admin')

Policy: Service accounts with primitive owner privileges.
Description: Service Account acts as identity for the applications to authenticate to Google cloud platform. Enrolling ServiceAccount with primitive Owner rights gives full access to the assigned resource. It is a best practice to grant only required specific access to the service account and not grant primitive Owner access.
Recommendation: Login into GCP console - https://console.cloud.google.com. Navigate to "IAM & admin" and choose "IAM". For each service account reported as vulnerable, click edit icon and delete the corresponding Owner roles
CQL: ServiceAccount where type='user_managed' should not have roles contain-any ('roles/owner')

Policy: Service accounts with primitive editor privileges.
Description: Service Account acts as identity for the applications to authenticate to Google cloud platform. Enrolling ServiceAccount with primitive Editor rights gives full access to the assigned resource. It is a best practice to grant only required specific access to the service account and not grant primitive Editor access.
Recommendation: Login into GCP console - https://console.cloud.google.com. Navigate to "IAM & admin" and choose "IAM". For each service account reported as vulnerable, click edit icon and delete the corresponding Editor roles
CQL: ServiceAccount where type='user_managed' should not have roles contain-any ('roles/editor')

Policy: IAM Users with service account user role at project level.
Description: It is advised that users should not be assigned 'Service Account User' role at the project level. It grants access to all service accounts in the project, including service accounts that may be created in the future. This can result in an elevation of privileges by using service accounts.
Recommendation: Login into GCP console - https://console.cloud.google.com. Navigate to "IAM & admin" and choose "IAM". Click on filter table text bar, Type Role: Service Account User. Ensure no user is listed as a result of the filter
CQL: IAMUser should not have roles contain-any ('roles/iam.serviceAccountUser')

Policy: IAM Groups with service account user role at project level.
Description: It is advised that groups should not be assigned 'Service Account User' role at the project level. It grants access to all service accounts in the project, including service accounts that may be created in the future. This can result in an elevation of privileges by using service accounts.
Recommendation: Login into GCP console - https://console.cloud.google.com. Navigate to "IAM & admin" and choose "IAM". Click on filter table text bar, Type Role: Service Account User. Ensure no group is listed as a result of the filter
CQL: IAMGroup should not have roles contain-any ('roles/iam.serviceAccountUser')

Policy: Service accounts with keys not rotated within 90 days or less.
Description: Service Account keys consist of a key ID (Private_key_Id) and Private key, which are used to sign programmatic requests that you make to Google cloud services accessible to that particular Service account. It is recommended that all Service Account keys are regularly rotated.
Recommendation: Login into GCP console - https://console.cloud.google.com. Navigate to "IAM & admin" and choose "Service accounts". Select desired service account reported as a violation, and click edit. Create a new key using "CREATE KEY" button. Replace the old key at every application with the new key. Delete the old Key
CQL: ServiceAccount where type = 'user_managed' should not have keyAge<=90

Policy: IAM users without proper separation of duties for assigned service account roles.
Description: Service Account acts as identity for the applications to authenticate to Google cloud platform. It is recommended that any user(s) should not have both 'Service Account Admin' and 'Service Account User' roles assigned at the same time.
Recommendation: Login into GCP console - https://console.cloud.google.com. Navigate to "IAM & admin" and choose "IAM". Ensure no member has roles "Service Account Admin" and "Service account User" assigned
CQL: IAMUser should not have roles contain ('roles/iam.serviceAccountAdmin', 'roles/iam.serviceAccountUser')

Policy: IAM groups without proper separation of duties for assigned service account roles.
Description: Service Account acts as identity for the applications to authenticate to Google cloud platform. It is recommended that any group(s) should not have both 'Service Account Admin' and 'Service Account User' roles assigned at the same time.
Recommendation: Login into GCP console - https://console.cloud.google.com. Navigate to "IAM & admin" and choose "IAM". Ensure no member has roles "Service Account Admin" and "Service account User" assigned
CQL: IAMGroup should not have roles contain ('roles/iam.serviceAccountAdmin', 'roles/iam.serviceAccountUser')

Policy: IAM users without proper separation of duties for assigned KMS roles.
Description: Cloud KMS provide facility to encrypt Cloud services either using Symmetric keys or Asymmetric keys providing confidentiality. It is a best practice to restrict user access to the KMS keys based on their roles.
Recommendation: Login into GCP console - https://console.cloud.google.com. Navigate to "IAM & admin" and choose "IAM". Identify Users created with roles Cloud KMS Admin and any of the following roles Cloud KMS CryptoKey Encrypter/Decrypter, Cloud KMS CryptoKey Encrypter, Cloud KMS CryptoKey Decrypter. Click edit icon and click delete icon to remove either role Cloud KMS Admin or any of the following roles Cloud KMS CryptoKey Encrypter/Decrypter, Cloud KMS CryptoKey Encrypter, Cloud KMS CryptoKey Decrypter
CQL: IAMUser where roles contain-any ('roles/cloudkms.admin') should not have roles contain-any ('roles/cloudkms.cryptoKeyEncrypter', 'roles/cloudkms.cryptoKeyDecrypter', 'roles/cloudkms.cryptoKeyEncrypterDecrypter')

Policy: IAM groups without proper separation of duties for assigned KMS roles.
Description: Cloud KMS provide facility to encrypt Cloud services either using Symmetric keys or Asymmetric keys providing confidentiality. It is a best practice to restrict groups access to the KMS keys based on their roles.
Recommendation: Login into GCP console - https://console.cloud.google.com. Navigate to "IAM & admin" and choose "IAM". Identify groups created with roles Cloud KMS Admin and any of the following roles Cloud KMS CryptoKey Encrypter/Decrypter, Cloud KMS CryptoKey Encrypter, Cloud KMS CryptoKey Decrypter. Click edit icon and click delete icon to remove either role Cloud KMS Admin or any of the following roles Cloud KMS CryptoKey Encrypter/Decrypter, Cloud KMS CryptoKey Encrypter, Cloud KMS CryptoKey Decrypter
CQL: IAMGroup where roles contain-any ('roles/cloudkms.admin') should not have roles contain-any ('roles/cloudkms.cryptoKeyEncrypter', 'roles/cloudkms.cryptoKeyDecrypter', 'roles/cloudkms.cryptoKeyEncrypterDecrypter')

Policy: Storage Buckets that allow anonymous/public access from external sources.
Description: Bucket IAM policy Policy governs the access permissions to bucket content. This policy checks whether the bucket is configured to grant anonymous and/or public access to anyone. Granting anonymous or public access is risky if you are storing any sensitive objects. It is recommended to grant limited access to storage buckets for specific authenticated users.
Recommendation: Login into : https://console.cloud.google.com. Navigate to "Storage" and select "Browser". Click on the bucket reported as a violation. Go to the "Permissions" tab and remove the access granted to IAM members "allUsers" and/or "allAuthenticatedUsers" from the table below
CQL: StorageBucket should not have policyBindings with [members contain-any ('allUsers', 'allAuthenticatedUsers')]

Policy: Storage Buckets that are not having logging enabled.
Description: GCP provides an option to enable access logging on storage buckets. These logs provide detailed records of the requests that are made to storage bucket. These logs will be useful for various security audit purposes and also to keep a tab on storage bucket for any unwanted access. It is a best practice to enable access logging on storage buckets.
Recommendation: Access logging can be enabled either using gsutil libraries or API calls. Follow the reference documentation and enable access logging for storage buckets
CQL: StorageBucket should not have logging isEmpty()

Policy: Storage Buckets that allow bucket READER access to public.
Description: Bucket IAM policy governs the access permissions to bucket content. This policy checks whether the bucket is configured to grant "Storage Legacy Bucket Reader" role to "allUsers". This empowers any user on the internet to list a bucket contents and read bucket and its objects metadata. It is recommended to grant limited access to the specific authenticated users.
Recommendation: Login into : https://console.cloud.google.com. Navigate to "Storage" and select "Browser". Click on the bucket reported as a violation. Go to the "Permissions" tab and remove the "Storage Legacy Bucket Reader" access granted to IAM member "allUsers"
CQL: StorageBucket should not have policyBindings with [role='roles/storage.legacyBucketReader' and members contain-any ('allUsers')]

Policy: Storage Buckets that allow bucket READER access to all authenticated users.
Description: Bucket IAM policy governs the access permissions to bucket content. This policy checks whether the bucket is configured to grant "Storage Legacy Bucket Reader" role to "allAuthenticatedUsers". This empowers any user authenticated with a Google account to list a bucket contents and read bucket and its objects metadata. It is recommended to grant limited access to the specific authenticated users.
Recommendation: Login into : https://console.cloud.google.com. Navigate to "Storage" and select "Browser". Click on the bucket reported as a violation. Go to the "Permissions" tab and remove the "Storage Legacy Bucket Reader" access granted to IAM member "allAuthenticatedUsers"
CQL: StorageBucket should not have policyBindings with [role='roles/storage.legacyBucketReader' and members contain-any ('allAuthenticatedUsers')]

Policy: Storage Buckets that allow bucket WRITER access to public.
Description: Bucket IAM policy governs the access permissions to bucket content. This policy checks whether the bucket is configured to grant "Storage Legacy Bucket Writer" role to "allUsers". This empowers any user on the internet to list, create, overwrite, and delete objects in a bucket, and also allows to read bucket and objects metadata. It is recommended to grant limited access to the specific authenticated users.
Recommendation: Login into : https://console.cloud.google.com. Navigate to "Storage" and select "Browser". Click on the bucket reported as a violation. Go to the "Permissions" tab and remove the "Storage Legacy Bucket Writer" access granted to IAM member "allusers"
CQL: StorageBucket should not have policyBindings with [role='roles/storage.legacyBucketWriter' and members contain-any ('allUsers')]

Policy: Storage Buckets that allow bucket WRITER access to all authenticated users.
Description: Bucket IAM policy governs the access permissions to bucket content. This policy checks whether the bucket is configured to grant "Storage Legacy Bucket Writer" role to "allAuthenticatedUsers". This empowers any user authenticated with a Google account to list, create, overwrite, and delete objects in a bucket, and also allows to read bucket and objects metadata. It is recommended to grant limited access to the specific authenticated users.
Recommendation: Login into : https://console.cloud.google.com. Navigate to "Storage" and select "Browser". Click on the bucket reported as a violation. Go to the "Permissions" tab and remove the "Storage Legacy Bucket Writer" access granted to IAM member "allAuthenticatedUsers"
CQL: StorageBucket should not have policyBindings with [role='roles/storage.legacyBucketWriter' and members contain-any ('allAuthenticatedUsers')]

Policy: Storage Buckets that allow bucket OWNER access to public.
Description: Bucket IAM policy governs the access permissions to bucket content. This policy checks whether the bucket is configured to grant "Storage Legacy Bucket Owner" role to "allUsers". This empowers any user on the internet to list, create, overwrite, and delete objects in a bucket, and also allows them to modify the bucket IAM policies. It is recommended to grant limited access to the specific authenticated users.
Recommendation: Login into : https://console.cloud.google.com. Navigate to "Storage" and select "Browser". Click on the bucket reported as a violation. Go to the "Permissions" tab and remove the "Storage Legacy Bucket Owner" access granted to IAM member "allusers"
CQL: StorageBucket should not have policyBindings with [role='roles/storage.legacyBucketOwner' and members contain-any ('allUsers')]

Policy: Storage Buckets that allow bucket OWNER access to all authenticated users.
Description: Bucket IAM policy governs the access permissions to bucket content. This policy checks whether the bucket is configured to grant "Storage Legacy Bucket Owner" role to "allAuthenticatedUsers". This empowers any user authenticated with a Google account to list, create, overwrite, and delete objects in a bucket, and also allows them to modify the bucket IAM policies. It is recommended to grant limited access to the specific authenticated users.
Recommendation: Login into : https://console.cloud.google.com. Navigate to "Storage" and select "Browser". Click on the bucket reported as a violation. Go to the "Permissions" tab and remove the "Storage Legacy Bucket Owner" access granted to IAM member "allAuthenticatedUsers"
CQL: StorageBucket should not have policyBindings with [role='roles/storage.legacyBucketOwner' and members contain-any ('allAuthenticatedUsers')]

Policy: Storage Buckets that allow object READER access to public.
Description: Bucket IAM policy governs the access permissions to bucket content. This policy checks whether the bucket is configured to grant "Storage Legacy Object Reader" role to "allUsers". This empowers any user on the internet to view objects and their metadata. It is recommended to grant limited access to the specific authenticated users.
Recommendation: Login into : https://console.cloud.google.com. Navigate to "Storage" and select "Browser". Click on the bucket reported as a violation. Go to the "Permissions" tab and remove the "Storage Legacy Object Reader" access granted to IAM member "allusers"
CQL: StorageBucket should not have policyBindings with [role='roles/storage.legacyObjectReader' and members contain-any ('allUsers')]

Policy: Storage Buckets that allow object READER access to all authenticated users.
Description: Bucket IAM policy governs the access permissions to bucket content. This policy checks whether the bucket is configured to grant "Storage Legacy Object Reader" role to "allAuthenticatedUsers". This empowers any user authenticated with a Google account to view objects and their metadata. It is recommended to grant limited access to the specific authenticated users.
Recommendation: Login into : https://console.cloud.google.com. Navigate to "Storage" and select "Browser". Click on the bucket reported as a violation. Go to the "Permissions" tab and remove the "Storage Legacy Object Reader" access granted to IAM member "allAuthenticatedUsers"
CQL: StorageBucket should not have policyBindings with [role='roles/storage.legacyObjectReader' and members contain-any ('allAuthenticatedUsers')]

Policy: Storage Buckets that allow object OWNER access to public.
Description: Bucket IAM policy governs the access permissions to bucket content. This policy checks whether the bucket is configured to grant "Storage Legacy Object Owner" role to "allUsers". This empowers any user on the internet to view and edit objects and their metadata, including ACLs. It is recommended to grant limited access to the specific authenticated users.
Recommendation: Login into : https://console.cloud.google.com. Navigate to "Storage" and select "Browser". Click on the bucket reported as a violation. Go to the "Permissions" tab and remove the "Storage Legacy Object Owner" access granted to IAM member "allusers"
CQL: StorageBucket should not have policyBindings with [role='roles/storage.legacyObjectOwner' and members contain-any ('allUsers')]

Policy: Storage Buckets that allow object OWNER access to all authenticated users.
Description: Bucket IAM policy governs the access permissions to bucket content. This policy checks whether the bucket is configured to grant "Storage Legacy Object Owner" role to "allAuthenticatedUsers". This empowers any user authenticated with a Google account to view, edit objects and their metadata, including ACLs. It is recommended to grant limited access to the specific authenticated users.
Recommendation: Login into : https://console.cloud.google.com. Navigate to "Storage" and select "Browser". Click on the bucket reported as a violation. Go to the "Permissions" tab and remove the "Storage Legacy Object Owner" access granted to IAM member "allAuthenticatedUsers"
CQL: StorageBucket should not have policyBindings with [role='roles/storage.legacyObjectOwner' and members contain-any ('allAuthenticatedUsers')]

Policy: Storage Buckets that allow object CREATOR access to public.
Description: Bucket IAM policy governs the access permissions to bucket content. This policy checks whether the bucket is configured to grant "Storage Object Creator" role to "allUsers". This empowers any user on the internet to create objects. It is recommended to grant limited access to the specific authenticated users.
Recommendation: Login into : https://console.cloud.google.com. Navigate to "Storage" and select "Browser". Click on the bucket reported as a violation. Go to the "Permissions" tab and remove the "Storage Object Creator" access granted to IAM member "allusers"
CQL: StorageBucket should not have policyBindings with [role='roles/storage.objectCreator' and members contain-any ('allUsers')]

Policy: Storage Buckets that allow object CREATOR access to all authenticated users.
Description: Bucket IAM policy governs the access permissions to bucket content. This policy checks whether the bucket is configured to grant "Storage Object Creator" role to "allAuthenticatedUsers". This empowers any user authenticated with a Google account to create objects. It is recommended to grant limited access to the specific authenticated users.
Recommendation: Login into : https://console.cloud.google.com. Navigate to "Storage" and select "Browser". Click on the bucket reported as a violation. Go to the "Permissions" tab and remove the "Storage Object Creator" access granted to IAM member "allAuthenticatedUsers"
CQL: StorageBucket should not have policyBindings with [role='roles/storage.objectCreator' and members contain-any ('allAuthenticatedUsers')]

Policy: Storage Buckets that allow object ADMIN access to public.
Description: Bucket IAM policy governs the access permissions to bucket content. This policy checks whether the bucket is configured to grant "Storage Object Admin" role to "allUsers". This empowers any user on the internet with full control over objects, including listing, creating, viewing, and deleting objects. It is recommended to grant limited access to the specific authenticated users.
Recommendation: Login into : https://console.cloud.google.com. Navigate to "Storage" and select "Browser". Click on the bucket reported as a violation. Go to the "Permissions" tab and remove the "Storage Object Admin" access granted to IAM member "allusers"
CQL: StorageBucket should not have policyBindings with [role='roles/storage.objectAdmin' and members contain-any ('allUsers')]

Policy: Storage Buckets that allow object ADMIN access to all authenticated users.
Description: Bucket IAM policy governs the access permissions to bucket content. This policy checks whether the bucket is configured to grant "Storage Object Admin" role to "allAuthenticatedUsers". This empowers any user authenticated with a Google account with full control over objects, including listing, creating, viewing, and deleting objects. It is recommended to grant limited access to the specific authenticated users.
Recommendation: Login into : https://console.cloud.google.com. Navigate to "Storage" and select "Browser". Click on the bucket reported as a violation. Go to the "Permissions" tab and remove the "Storage Object Admin" access granted to IAM member "allAuthenticatedUsers"
CQL: StorageBucket should not have policyBindings with [role='roles/storage.objectAdmin' and members contain-any ('allAuthenticatedUsers')]

Policy: Storage Buckets that allow storage ADMIN access to public.
Description: Bucket IAM policy governs the access permissions to bucket content. This policy checks whether the bucket is configured to grant "Storage Admin" role to "allUsers". This empowers any user on the internet with full control of buckets and objects. It is recommended to grant limited access to the specific authenticated users.
Recommendation: Login into : https://console.cloud.google.com. Navigate to "Storage" and select "Browser". Click on the bucket reported as a violation. Go to the "Permissions" tab and remove the "Storage Admin" access granted to IAM member "allusers"
CQL: StorageBucket should not have policyBindings with [role='roles/storage.admin' and members contain-any ('allUsers')]

Policy: Storage Buckets that allow storage ADMIN access to all authenticated users.
Description: Bucket IAM policy governs the access permissions to bucket content. This policy checks whether the bucket is configured to grant "Storage Admin" role to "allAuthenticatedUsers". This empowers any user authenticated with a Google account with full control of buckets and objects. It is recommended to grant limited access to the specific authenticated users.
Recommendation: Login into : https://console.cloud.google.com. Navigate to "Storage" and select "Browser". Click on the bucket reported as a violation. Go to the "Permissions" tab and remove the "Storage Admin" access granted to IAM member "allAuthenticatedUsers"
CQL: StorageBucket should not have policyBindings with [role='roles/storage.admin' and members contain-any ('allAuthenticatedUsers')]

Policy: Storage Buckets that allow object VIEWER access to public.
Description: Bucket IAM policy governs the access permissions to bucket content. This policy checks whether the bucket is configured to grant "Storage Object Viewer" role to "allUsers". This empowers any user on the internet with access to view objects and their metadata, and also list the objects in a bucket. It is recommended to grant limited access to the specific authenticated users.
Recommendation: Login into : https://console.cloud.google.com. Navigate to "Storage" and select "Browser". Click on the bucket reported as a violation. Go to the "Permissions" tab and remove the "Storage Object Viewer" access granted to IAM member "allusers"
CQL: StorageBucket should not have policyBindings with [role='roles/storage.objectViewer' and members contain-any ('allUsers')]

Policy: Storage Buckets that allow object VIEWER access to all authenticated users.
Description: Bucket IAM policy governs the access permissions to bucket content. This policy checks whether the bucket is configured to grant "Storage Object Viewer" role to "allAuthenticatedUsers". This empowers any user authenticated with a Google account with access to view objects and their metadata, and also list the objects in a bucket. It is recommended to grant limited access to the specific authenticated users.
Recommendation: Login into : https://console.cloud.google.com. Navigate to "Storage" and select "Browser". Click on the bucket reported as a violation. Go to the "Permissions" tab and remove the "Storage Object Viewer" access granted to IAM member "allAuthenticatedUsers"
CQL: StorageBucket should not have policyBindings with [role='roles/storage.objectViewer' and members contain-any ('allAuthenticatedUsers')]

Policy: IAM users assigned with Owner role at the project level
Description: Primitive role "Owner" grants an identity to create, delete and manage roles and permissions for all resources within the project, plus manage roles for a project. It is advised to grant predefined-roles or custom roles with least-privilege access to users.
Recommendation: Login into GCP Console: https://console.cloud.google.com. Navigate to "IAM & admin" and choose "IAM". Ensure no user is assigned with role "Owner" at the project level
CQL: IAMUser should not have roles contain-any ('roles/owner')

Policy: IAM users assigned with Editor role at the project level
Description: Primitive role "Editor" grants an identity to create and delete most of the GCP resources. It is advised to grant predefined-roles or custom roles with least-privilege access to users.
Recommendation: Login into GCP Console: https://console.cloud.google.com. Navigate to "IAM & admin" and choose "IAM". Ensure no user is assigned with role "Editor" at the project level
CQL: IAMUser should not have roles contain-any ('roles/editor')

Policy: IAM groups assigned with Owner role at the project level
Description: Primitive role "Owner" grants an identity to create, delete and manage roles and permissions for all resources within the project, plus manage roles for a project. It is advised to grant predefined-roles or custom roles with least-privilege access to groups.
Recommendation: Login into GCP Console: https://console.cloud.google.com. Navigate to "IAM & admin" and choose "IAM". Ensure no group is assigned with role "Owner" at the project level
CQL: IAMGroup should not have roles contain-any ('roles/owner')

Policy: IAM groups assigned with Editor role at the project level
Description: Primitive role "Editor" grant an identity to create and delete most of the GCP resources. It is advised to grant predefined-roles or custom roles with least-privilege access to groups.
Recommendation: Login into GCP Console: https://console.cloud.google.com. Navigate to "IAM & admin" and choose "IAM". Ensure no group is assigned with role "Editor" at the project level
CQL: IAMGroup should not have roles contain-any ('roles/editor')

Policy: Ensure Kubernetes Clusters enable Stackdriver Logging
Description: The Stackdriver logging is enabled by default on Kubernetes clusters unless it is explicitly disabled. The logging helps you store, search, analyze, and alert on log data. As a best practice, ensure they are not disabled.
Recommendation: Login into GCP Console - https://console.cloud.google.com/. Navigate to "Kubernetes Engine" and select "Clusters". Select the cluster, click "EDIT", and ensure "Kubernetes Engine Monitoring" is not "Disabled"
CQL: KBECluster should not have loggingService = 'none'

Policy: Ensure Kubernetes Clusters enable Stackdriver Monitoring
Description: The Stackdriver logging is enabled by default on Kubernetes clusters unless it is explicitly disabled. The monitoring helps you get metrics on CPU utilization, disk traffic, network traffic, and uptime information about the Kubernetes Cluster. As a best practice, ensure they are not disabled.
Recommendation: Login into GCP Console - https://console.cloud.google.com/. Navigate to "Kubernetes Engine" and select "Clusters". Select the cluster, click "EDIT", and ensure "Kubernetes Engine Monitoring" is not "Disabled"
CQL: KBECluster should not have monitoringService = 'none'

Policy: Ensure Legacy Authorization is set to Disabled on Kubernetes Engine Clusters
Description: Legacy authorization grants identities in the Kubernetes cluster, including service accounts,  nodes, and controllers, will have permissions beyond those provided by the RBAC configuration or IAM.  It is a best practice to disable legacy authorization on Kubernetes cluster.
Recommendation: Login into GCP Console - https://console.cloud.google.com/. Navigate to "Kubernetes Engine" and select "Clusters". Select the cluster, click on "EDIT" and set "Legacy Authorization" to disabled
CQL: KBECluster should have legacyAbacEnabled isEmpty()

Policy: Ensure Master authorized networks is set to Enabled on Kubernetes Engine Clusters
Description: Enabling authorized networks blocks unauthorized IP addresses from Public network and Internal  GCP IP address from accessing Kubernetes master endpoint.
Recommendation: Login into GCP Console - https://console.cloud.google.com/. Navigate to "Kubernetes Engine" and select "Clusters". Select the cluster, click on "EDIT" and set "Master authorized networks" to enabled
CQL: KBECluster should have masterAuthorizedNetworksConfigEnabled=true

Policy: Ensure Kubernetes Clusters are configured with Labels
Description: Labeling helps in managing Kubernetes Clusters efficiently. It helps in filtering the specific cluster by their attached label, and also in billing.
Recommendation: Login into GCP Console - https://console.cloud.google.com/. Navigate to "Kubernetes Engine" and select "Clusters". Ensure, key and value pair is set for each cluster, in the Cluster "labels" section
CQL: KBECluster should not have labels isEmpty()

Policy: Ensure Kubernetes web UI / Dashboard is disabled
Description: The Kubernetes Web UI (Dashboard) is supported by a highly privileged Kubernetes Service Account. It is a best practice to disable the Kubernetes Web UI (Dashboard) when running on Kubernetes Engine.
Recommendation: Login into GCP Console - https://console.cloud.google.com/. Navigate to "Kubernetes Engine" and select "Clusters". Select the cluster, click on EDIT and go to "Add-ons". Ensure "Kubernetes dashboard" is Disabled
CQL: KBECluster should have kubernetesDashboardDisabled isNotEmpty()

Policy: Kubernetes Clusters without Automatic node repair
Description: Enabling "Automatic node repair" helps the kubernetes engine to make periodic health checks on the Cluster nodes and when any cluster fails consecutive health checks over a specified threshold, kubernetes engine initiates a repair process for that node.
Recommendation: Login into GCP Console - https://console.cloud.google.com/. Navigate to "Kubernetes Engine" and select "Clusters". Select the cluster, click on EDIT and go to "Node Pools". For each node pool  set "Automatic node repair" to enabled
CQL: KBECluster should have autoRepairOnAllNodePools=true

Policy: Kubernetes Engine Clusters nodes without automatic node upgrade
Description: Enabling "Automatic node upgrades"  helps your cluster nodes to be up to date with the latest version of Kubernetes. This ensures lower management overhead, better security and ease of use.
Recommendation: Login into GCP Console - https://console.cloud.google.com/. Navigate to "Kubernetes Engine" and select "Clusters". Select the cluster, click on EDIT and go to "Node Pools". For each node pool  set "Automatic node upgrades" to enabled
CQL: KBECluster should have autoUpgradeOnAllNodePools=true

Policy: Kubernetes Engine Clusters Node images without Container-Optimized OS (cos)
Description: Container-Optimized OS is an operating system image for your Compute Engine VMs that is optimized for running Docker containers. They help in bringing up the Docker containers quickly, efficiently and securely. Note: Container-Optimized OS requires Kubernetes version 1.4.0 or higher
Recommendation: Login into GCP Console - https://console.cloud.google.com/. Navigate to "Kubernetes Engine" and select "Clusters". Select the cluster, click on EDIT and go to "Node Pools". For each node pool set "Node image" to "Container-Optimized OS (cos)
CQL: KBECluster should have cosImageOnAllNodePools=true

Policy: Ensure Network policy is enabled on Kubernetes Engine Clusters
Description: A network policy is a specification of how groups of pods are allowed to communicate with each other and other network endpoints. Network policy API allows the cluster administrator to specify what pods are allowed to communicate with each other
Recommendation: Login into GCP Console - https://console.cloud.google.com/. Navigate to "Kubernetes Engine" and select "Clusters". Select the cluster, click on "EDIT" and set "Network policy for master" and "Network policy for nodes" to enabled
CQL: KBECluster should have networkPolicyEnabled=true

Policy: Ensure Kubernetes Cluster is created with Alias IP ranges enabled
Description: Enabling Alias IP ranges allows you to assign ranges of internal IP addresses as aliases to a virtual machines network interfaces. This feature is useful when a VM is running with multiple services and you want to assign each service with a different IP address.
Recommendation: It is not possible to enable the alias IP ranges for existing Kubernetes cluster. You can enable alias IP ranges only at the time of the creation of the cluster. Follow the above link to create a cluster with alias IP ranges enabled.
CQL: KBECluster should have aliasIPRangesEnabled=true

Policy: Ensure PodSecurityPolicy controller is enabled on the Kubernetes Engine Clusters
Description: A PodSecurityPolicy is a cluster-level configuration that enforces the security restrictions and requirements for Pods. They define a set of conditions that Pods must meet to be accepted by the cluster; any request to create or update a Pod is verified against the conditions in the PodSecurityPolicy and allowed or rejected based on their compliance. It is a security best practice to enable PodSecurityPolicy to ensure pods always adhered to defined security policy.
Recommendation: Note: Before enabling podSecurityPolicy for an existing cluster you must define and authorize policies. Run the following command to enable podSecurityPolicy for existing Kubernetes cluster . gcloud beta container clusters update [CLUSTER_NAME] --zone [COMPUTE_ZONE] -- enable-pod-security-policy
CQL: KBECluster should have podSecurityPolicyEnabled=true

Policy: Ensure Kubernetes Cluster is created with Private cluster enabled
Description: A private cluster is a cluster that makes your master inaccessible from the public internet. In a private cluster, nodes do not have public IP addresses, so your workloads run in an environment that is isolated from the internet. It is a security best practice to enable a private cluster.
Recommendation: It is not possible to enable the private cluster on existing Kubernetes cluster. You can enable private cluster only at the time of the creation of Kubernetes cluster. Follow the above link to create a Kubernetes cluster with private cluster enabled. . Note: When you create a private cluster, you must specify a /28 CIDR range for the VMs that run the Kubernetes master components. You also need to enable Alias IPs. The range you specify for the masters must not overlap with any subnet in your cluster's VPC
CQL: KBECluster should have privateCluster=true

Policy: Compute Instances with 'IP Forwarding' on network interfaces disabled.
Description: Forwarding of data packets should be disabled to prevent data loss or information disclosure.
Recommendation: Login into GCP console - https://console.cloud.google.com. Navigate to "Compute Engine" and choose "VM Instances". Select the VM instance reported under violation, and ensure "IP forwarding" is set to Off under "Network interfaces" section
CQL: VMInstance should not have canIpForward=true

Policy: Compute Instances using default service account with scope 'Allow full access to all Cloud APIs'
Description: Compute Engine default service account with Scope "Allow full access to all Cloud APIs" can allow an IAM user with certain roles accessing an instance to perform cloud operations/API calls that user is not supposed to perform. It is a best practice to avoid granting the default service account with scope "Allow full access to all Cloud APIs" to block any privilege escalation.
Recommendation: Login into GCP console - https://console.cloud.google.com. Navigate to "Compute Engine" and choose "VM Instances". Select the VM instance reported under violation and stop the instance. Click on the EDIT button. Under "Service Account" section. Set the appropriate "Access scope" as per business needs and Save the configuration. Start the VM Instance
CQL: VMInstance should not have serviceAccounts with [email like '%compute@developer.gserviceaccount.com' and scopes contain-any ('https://www.googleapis.com/auth/cloud-platform')]

Policy: Cloud SQL instances not enforcing SSL
Description: It is a best practice, to enforce SSL encryption on all incoming connections to the SQL database to protect from threats like Man in the middle (MITM) attacks.Note: Client needs to have Client-certificate to establish communication with SQL instance after enabling SSL.
Recommendation: To enable SSL encryption for an instance run the following CLI command. gcloud sql instances patch [INSTANCE_NAME] --require-ssl
CQL: SQLInstance should have requireSsl=true

Policy: Cloud SQL instances that are exposed to the internet
Description: It is a best practice to accept connections at SQL instance only from specific trusted IP addresses and block the access to the external world.
Recommendation: Login into GCP Console - https://console.cloud.google.com/. Navigate to "SQL". Select the instance reported as a violation, and click on Edit. Click on section "Set Connectivity". Go to selected "Public IP" section. Under "Authorized networks" remove the entry allowing traffic from CIDR 0.0.0.0/0
CQL: SQLInstance should not have authorizedNetworks with [value like '0.0.0.0%']

Policy: Project should have atleast one sink for all logs
Description: Log entries are retained in Stackdriver Logging for a limited time. And post that time, all log entries are deleted. To keep log entries for a longer period, it is recommended to create a sink without any filter and store all log entries in any one of the supported destinations - Cloud Storage, BigQuery, or Cloud Pub/Sub.
Recommendation: Login into GCP Console - https://console.cloud.google.com/. Navigate to "logging" and select "logs". In the Filter bar, click on the down arrow and select "Convert to advanced filter". Clear all text in the Filter bar, and click "Submit Filter". Now click on "CREATE EXPORT", provide Sink Name, select Sink service and sink destination. Click "Create Sink"
CQL: Project should have sinks with [filter='*' or filter isEmpty()]

Policy: Logmetric alert on project ownership changes
Description: A project owner has all privileges to manage a project and its resources. It is a security best practice to monitor the assignment of the primitive role "roles/Owner" to any IAM member (user/ServiceAccounts) to avoid unnecessary project ownership or any misuses of projects and resources.
Recommendation: Login into GCP Console - https://console.cloud.google.com/. Navigate to "logging" and select "logs". In the Filter bar, click the down arrow and select "Convert to advanced filter". Clear all text in the Filter bar, and add the below text:. (protoPayload.serviceName="cloudresourcemanager.googleapis.com") AND (ProjectOwnership OR projectOwnerInvitee) OR (protoPayload.serviceData.policyDelta.bindingDeltas.action="REMOVE" AND protoPayload.serviceData.policyDelta.bindingDeltas.role="roles/owner") OR (protoPayload.serviceData.policyDelta.bindingDeltas.action="ADD" AND protoPayload.serviceData.policyDelta.bindingDeltas.role="roles/owner"). Click Submit Filter. Click "CREATE METRIC", provide Name, Description, set Unit as "1", Type as "Counter". Click "Create Metric". Follow the instructions documented at the below link to "Create alert from metric", and ensure you enable the notification. https://cloud.google.com/logging/docs/logs-based-metrics/charts-and-alerts#creating_a_simple_alerting_policy_on_a_counter_metric
CQL: Project should have logMetrics with [filter='(protoPayload.serviceName="cloudresourcemanager.googleapis.com") AND (ProjectOwnership OR projectOwnerInvitee) OR (protoPayload.serviceData.policyDelta.bindingDeltas.action="REMOVE" AND protoPayload.serviceData.policyDelta.bindingDeltas.role="roles/owner") OR (protoPayload.serviceData.policyDelta.bindingDeltas.action="ADD" AND protoPayload.serviceData.policyDelta.bindingDeltas.role="roles/owner")' and alertWithNotification=true]

Policy: Logmetric alert on  audit configuration changes
Description: Cloud Audit logging provides a history of AWS API calls for an account, including API calls made via the Console, SDKs, command line tools, and other GCP services. It is a security best practice to monitor audit configuration changes to ensure recommended state of audit logs are maintained always.
Recommendation: Login into GCP Console - https://console.cloud.google.com/. Navigate to "logging" and select "logs". In the Filter bar, click the down arrow and select "Convert to advanced filter". Clear all text in the Filter bar, and add the below text:. protoPayload.methodName="SetIamPolicy" AND protoPayload.serviceData.policyDelta.auditConfigDeltas:*. Click Submit Filter. Click "CREATE METRIC", provide Name, Description, set Unit as "1", Type as "Counter". Click "Create Metric". Follow the instructions documented at the below link to "Create alert from metric", and ensure you enable the notification. https://cloud.google.com/logging/docs/logs-based-metrics/charts-and-alerts#creating_a_simple_alerting_policy_on_a_counter_metric
CQL: Project should have logMetrics with [filter='protoPayload.methodName="SetIamPolicy" AND protoPayload.serviceData.policyDelta.auditConfigDeltas:*' and alertWithNotification=true]

Policy: Logmetric alert on custom role changes
Description: Google cloud platform provides options to create custom roles along with existing predefined IAM roles for Organization specific needs. It is a security best practice to monitor any changes to custom roles to ensure any unwanted permissions are not granted.
Recommendation: Login into GCP Console - https://console.cloud.google.com/. Navigate to "logging" and select "logs". In the Filter bar, click the down arrow and select "Convert to advanced filter". Clear all text in the Filter bar, and add the below text:. resource.type="iam_role" AND protoPayload.methodName = "google.iam.admin.v1.CreateRole" OR protoPayload.methodName="google.iam.admin.v1.DeleteRole" OR protoPayload.methodName="google.iam.admin.v1.UpdateRole". Click Submit Filter. Click "CREATE METRIC", provide Name, Description, set Unit as "1", Type as "Counter". Click "Create Metric". Follow the instructions documented at the below link to "Create alert from metric", and ensure you enable the notification. https://cloud.google.com/logging/docs/logs-based-metrics/charts-and-alerts#creating_a_simple_alerting_policy_on_a_counter_metric
CQL: Project should have logMetrics with [filter='resource.type="iam_role" AND protoPayload.methodName="google.iam.admin.v1.CreateRole" OR protoPayload.methodName="google.iam.admin.v1.DeleteRole" OR protoPayload.methodName="google.iam.admin.v1.UpdateRole"' and alertWithNotification=true]

Policy: Logmetric alert on VPC network firewall rule changes
Description: Firewall rules govern access to/from the cloud environment. It is a security best practice to monitor any changes to network firewall rules to ensure recommended state of network access is maintained always.
Recommendation: Login into GCP Console - https://console.cloud.google.com/. Navigate to "logging" and select "logs". In the Filter bar, click the down arrow and select "Convert to advanced filter". Clear all text in the Filter bar, and add the below text:. resource.type="gce_firewall_rule" AND jsonPayload.event_subtype="compute.firewalls.patch" OR jsonPayload.event_subtype="compute.firewalls.insert". Click Submit Filter. Click "CREATE METRIC", provide Name, Description, set Unit as "1", Type as "Counter". Click "Create Metric". Follow the instructions documented at the below link to "Create alert from metric", and ensure you enable the notification. https://cloud.google.com/logging/docs/logs-based-metrics/charts-and-alerts#creating_a_simple_alerting_policy_on_a_counter_metric
CQL: Project should have logMetrics with [filter='resource.type="gce_firewall_rule" AND jsonPayload.event_subtype="compute.firewalls.patch" OR jsonPayload.event_subtype="compute.firewalls.insert"' and alertWithNotification=true]

Policy: Logmetric alert on VPC network route changes
Description: Google Cloud Platform (GCP) routes define the paths network traffic takes from a VM instance to other destinations. It is a security best practice to monitor any changes to VPC network route tables to ensure all VPC traffic flows through an expected path.
Recommendation: Login into GCP Console - https://console.cloud.google.com/. Navigate to "logging" and select "logs". In the Filter bar, click the down arrow and select "Convert to advanced filter". Clear all text in the Filter bar, and add the below text:. resource.type="gce_route" AND jsonPayload.event_subtype="compute.routes.delete" OR jsonPayload.event_subtype="compute.routes.insert". Click Submit Filter. Click "CREATE METRIC", provide Name, Description, set Unit as "1", Type as "Counter". Click "Create Metric". Follow the instructions documented at the below link to "Create alert from metric", and ensure you enable the notification. https://cloud.google.com/logging/docs/logs-based-metrics/charts-and-alerts#creating_a_simple_alerting_policy_on_a_counter_metric
CQL: Project should have logMetrics with [filter='resource.type="gce_route" AND jsonPayload.event_subtype="compute.routes.delete" OR jsonPayload.event_subtype="compute.routes.insert"' and alertWithNotification=true]

Policy: Logmetric alert on VPC network changes
Description: Google Cloud Platform (GCP) Virtual Private Cloud provides global, scalable, flexible networking for your cloud-based resources and services. It is a security best practice to monitor any changes to the VPC network to ensure VPC traffic flow is not impacted.
Recommendation: Login into GCP Console - https://console.cloud.google.com/. Navigate to "logging" and select "logs". In the Filter bar, click the down arrow and select "Convert to advanced filter". Clear all text in the Filter bar, and add the below text:. resource.type=gce_network AND jsonPayload.event_subtype="compute.networks.insert" OR jsonPayload.event_subtype="compute.networks.patch" OR jsonPayload.event_subtype="compute.networks.delete" OR jsonPayload.event_subtype="compute.networks.removePeering" OR jsonPayload.event_subtype="compute.networks.addPeering". Click Submit Filter. Click "CREATE METRIC", provide Name, Description, set Unit as "1", Type as "Counter". Click "Create Metric". Follow the instructions documented at the below link to "Create alert from metric", and ensure you enable the notification. https://cloud.google.com/logging/docs/logs-based-metrics/charts-and-alerts#creating_a_simple_alerting_policy_on_a_counter_metric
CQL: Project should have logMetrics with [filter='resource.type=gce_network AND jsonPayload.event_subtype="compute.networks.insert" OR jsonPayload.event_subtype="compute.networks.patch" OR jsonPayload.event_subtype="compute.networks.delete" OR jsonPayload.event_subtype="compute.networks.removePeering" OR jsonPayload.event_subtype="compute.networks.addPeering"' and alertWithNotification=true]

Policy: Logmetric alert on Cloud Storage IAM permission changes
Description: Google Cloud Platform (GCP) Cloud Storage allows world-wide storage and retrieval of any amount of data at any time. It is a security best practice to monitor any changes to the Cloud Storage IAM permissions to detect and correct permissions on sensitive Cloud Storage bucket and objects inside the bucket.
Recommendation: Login into GCP Console - https://console.cloud.google.com/. Navigate to "logging" and select "logs". In the Filter bar, click the down arrow and select "Convert to advanced filter". Clear all text in the Filter bar, and add the below text:. resource.type=gcs_bucket AND protoPayload.methodName="storage.setIamPermissions". Click Submit Filter. Click "CREATE METRIC", provide Name, Description, set Unit as "1", Type as "Counter". Click "Create Metric". Follow the instructions documented at the below link to "Create alert from metric", and ensure you enable the notification. https://cloud.google.com/logging/docs/logs-based-metrics/charts-and-alerts#creating_a_simple_alerting_policy_on_a_counter_metric
CQL: Project should have logMetrics with [filter='resource.type=gcs_bucket AND protoPayload.methodName="storage.setIamPermissions"' and alertWithNotification=true]

Policy: Logmetric alert on SQL instance configuration changes
Description: Google Cloud Platform (GCP) Cloud SQL is a fully managed database service that makes it easy to set up, maintain, manage, and administer your relational databases. It is a security best practice to monitor any changes to the SQL instances to reduce time to detect and correct misconfigurations on SQL servers.
Recommendation: Login into GCP Console - https://console.cloud.google.com/. Navigate to "logging" and select "logs". In the Filter bar, click the down arrow and select "Convert to advanced filter". Clear all text in the Filter bar, and add the below text:. protoPayload.methodName="cloudsql.instances.update". Click Submit Filter. Click "CREATE METRIC", provide Name, Description, set Unit as "1", Type as "Counter". Click "Create Metric". Follow the instructions documented at the below link to "Create alert from metric", and ensure you enable the notification. https://cloud.google.com/logging/docs/logs-based-metrics/charts-and-alerts#creating_a_simple_alerting_policy_on_a_counter_metric
CQL: Project should have logMetrics with [filter='protoPayload.methodName="cloudsql.instances.update"' and alertWithNotification=true]

Policy: Firewall rule should not allow ingress traffic from 0.0.0.0/0
Description: Allowing Inbound traffic from any IPv4 address - 0.0.0.0/0 can lead to attacks like DoS, Brute Force, Smurf, and reconnaissance. It is a best practice to restrict inbound traffic from required source IP address(es).
Recommendation: Login into GCP Console - https://console.cloud.google.com/. Navigate to "VPC Network"  and select "Firewall rules". Select the firewall reported as a violation, and click EDIT. Go to "Source IP ranges" and remove the IP address "0.0.0.0" or "0.0.0.0/0", and Save the configuration
CQL: Firewall where disabled = false and direction = 'INGRESS' and allowedRules isNotEmpty() should not have sourceRanges contain ('0.0.0.0%')

Policy: Firewall rule should not allow egress traffic to 0.0.0.0/0
Description: Allowing Outbound traffic to any IPv4 addresses - 0.0.0.0/0 can lead to internal resources accessing unwanted and untrusted resources. If a system is compromised, an attacker can use it to exfiltrate data or conduct spam or phishing campaigns. It is a best practice to restrict outbound traffic to only required IP address(es).
Recommendation: Login into GCP Console - https://console.cloud.google.com/. Navigate to "VPC Network" and select "Firewall rules". Select the firewall reported as a violation, and click EDIT. Go to "Destination IP ranges" and remove the IP address "0.0.0.0" or "0.0.0.0/0", and Save the configuration
CQL: Firewall where disabled = false and direction = 'EGRESS' and allowedRules isNotEmpty() should not have destinationRanges contain ('0.0.0.0%')

Policy: Firewall rule should not allow ingress traffic on ALL protocols and ports
Description: This policy checks whether firewall rule is configured with "Allow all" configuration under "Protocols and ports". Allowing Inbound traffic on all protocols and ports increases the attack surface of your environment. It is a best practice to follow the principle of least privilege, and grant access on only required protocols and ports.
Recommendation: Login into GCP Console - https://console.cloud.google.com/. Navigate to "VPC Network"  and select "Firewall rules". Select the firewall reported as a violation, and click EDIT. Under "Protocols and ports", select "Specified protocols and ports" and provide access to specific protocol(s) and port(s), and save the configuration
CQL: Firewall where disabled = false and direction = 'INGRESS' should not have allowedRules with [ protocol in ('all') ]

Policy: Firewall rule should not allow egress traffic to ALL protocols and ports
Description: This policy checks whether firewall rule is configured with "Allow all" configuration under "Protocols and ports". Allowing Outbound traffic to all protocols and ports can lead to internal resources accessing unwanted and untrusted resources. It is a best practice to follow the principle of least privilege, and grant access to only required protocols and ports.
Recommendation: Login into GCP Console - https://console.cloud.google.com/. Navigate to "VPC Network"  and select "Firewall rules". Select the firewall reported as a violation, and click EDIT. Under "Protocols and ports", select "Specified protocols and ports" and provide access to specific protocol(s) and port(s), and save the configuration
CQL: Firewall where disabled = false and direction = 'EGRESS' should not have allowedRules with [ protocol in ('all') ]

Policy: Firewall rule should not allow ingress traffic on any protocol to all ports
Description: This policy checks whether firewall rule is configured with port range 0-65535 on any protocol. Allowing Inbound traffic on all ports increases the attack surface of your environment. It is a best practice to follow the principle of least privilege, and grant access on only required ports.
Recommendation: Login into GCP Console - https://console.cloud.google.com/. Navigate to "VPC Network"  and select "Firewall rules". Select the firewall reported as a violation, and click EDIT. Under "Protocols and ports", remove the access granted to ports "0-65535", and provide access to the specific required port(s), and save the configuration
CQL: Firewall where disabled = false and direction = 'INGRESS' should not have allowedRules with [ ports contain ('0-65535') ]

Policy: Firewall rule should not allow egress traffic to any protocol on all ports
Description: This policy checks whether firewall rule is configured with port range 0-65535 on any protocol. Allowing Outbound traffic to all ports can lead to internal resources accessing unwanted and untrusted resources. It is a best practice to follow the principle of least privilege, and grant access to only required ports.
Recommendation: Login into GCP Console - https://console.cloud.google.com/. Navigate to "VPC Network"  and select "Firewall rules". Select the firewall reported as a violation, and click EDIT. Under "Protocols and ports", remove the access granted to ports "0-65535", and provide access to the specific required port(s), and save the configuration
CQL: Firewall where disabled = false and direction = 'EGRESS' should not have allowedRules with [ ports contain ('0-65535') ]

Policy: Global Firewall rule should not allow ingress traffic from 0.0.0.0/0
Description: Global firewall rules are applied to all instances in the network. It is a security risk to have a global firewall rule that allows traffic from any IPv4 address. It is a best practice to restrict inbound traffic from required source IP address(es).
Recommendation: Login into GCP Console - https://console.cloud.google.com/. Navigate to "VPC Network" and select "Firewall rules". Select the firewall reported as a violation, and click EDIT. Go to "Source IP ranges" and remove the IP address "0.0.0.0" or "0.0.0.0/0", and Save the configuration
CQL: Firewall where disabled = false and direction = 'INGRESS' and targetTags isEmpty() and allowedRules isNotEmpty() should not have sourceRanges contain ('0.0.0.0%')

Policy: ServiceAccounts with no associated roles
Description: Service Account acts as identity for the applications to authenticate to Google cloud platform. It is a security best practice to always have roles associated with the user managed Service Accounts.
Recommendation: Login into GCP Console - https://console.cloud.google.com/. Navigate to "IAM & admin" and choose "Service accounts". Select the Service Account reported as a violation, and click more options under Actions column. Click Delete and confirm the deletion
CQL: ServiceAccount where type = 'user_managed' should not have roles isEmpty()

Policy: Storage Buckets without versioning
Description: It is a best practice to enable versioning on cloud storage buckets to protect objects in storage bucket from being overwritten or accidentally deleted.
Recommendation: It is not possible to enable versioning on buckets using the Google console. Use the following gsutil command to set versioning on cloud storage buckets. gsutil versioning set on gs://[BUCKET_NAME]
CQL: StorageBucket should not have versioningEnabled isEmpty()

Policy: Storage Buckets without lifecycle
