Description: The Storage bucket lifecycle management feature helps in setting Time to Live (TTL) for objects, archiving older versions of objects, or downgrading storage classes of objects. It is a best practice to set life cycle policies for effective management of storage buckets.
Recommendation: Login into GCP Console - https://console.cloud.google.com/. Navigate to "Storage" and select "Browser". In the bucket list, find the bucket you want to enable, and click "None" in the Lifecycle column. Under object lifecycle rules page, Click "Add rule". Specify and save the appropriate configuration
CQL: StorageBucket should not have lifecycle isEmpty()

Policy: Storage Buckets should have Labels
Description: Labels are a lightweight way to group resources together that are related to or associated with each other. It is a best practice to label cloud resources to better organize and gain visibility into their usage.
Recommendation: Login into GCP Console - https://console.cloud.google.com/. Navigate to "Storage" and select "Browser". In the bucket list, for each bucket reported as a violation, click its more options button (three vertical dots). In the side panel that appears, click the "+ Add label" button. Specify appropriate key and value and save the configuration
CQL: StorageBucket should not have labels isEmpty()

Policy: VMInstance should have Labels
Description: Labels are a lightweight way to group resources together that are related to or associated with each other. It is a best practice to label cloud resources to better organize and gain visibility into their usage.
Recommendation: Login into GCP Console - https://console.cloud.google.com/. Navigate to "Compute Engine" and choose "VM Instances". In the VM Instance list, for each VM instance reported as violation, select the checkboxes next to the VM Instance. Click "Show info panel" to expand the labels column. In the side panel that appears, click the "+ Add label" button. Specify appropriate key and value and save the configuration
CQL: VMInstance should not have labels isEmpty()

Policy: MYSQL Instance should not grant root user connection from anywhere
Description: It is a security best practice to allow root access to a MySql Database Instance only through a specific white-listed trusted IPs.
Recommendation: Login into GCP Console - https://console.cloud.google.com/. Navigate to "SQL". Select the instance reported as a violation, and open its Overview page. Select the "Users" tab. For username "root" with host name as "%" or "0.0.0.0" or "0.0.0.0/0", click on more options (three vertical dots). Select "Delete" and confirm the delete operation
CQL: SQLInstance where databaseVersion like 'MYSQL%' should not have users with [ username = 'root' and host in ('%', '0.0.0.0', '0.0.0.0/0') ]

Policy: Project should not have default network
Description: The default network is an auto mode network with pre-populated firewall rules. The firewall rules in default network allow traffic from internet on protocols like RDP and SSH. It is best practice to delete default networks (mainly in a production environment) to avoid any accidental grant on ingress traffic.
Recommendation: Ensure no subnets, routes or firewall rules under default network is used. Login into GCP console: https://console.cloud.google.com. Navigate to "VPC network" and select "VPC networks". In the network list, click the "default" network under the "Name" column. Click "DELETE VPC NETWORK" and confirm the deletion
CQL: VPCNetwork should not have name = 'default'

Policy: Project should not have legacy network
Description: The legacy network is global in scope and spans across all cloud regions. They come with a single network IPv4 prefix range and a single gateway IP address for the whole network, and you cannot create regional subnets in legacy networks. As a best practice, it is recommended to avoid using the legacy network.
Recommendation: Login into GCP console: https://console.cloud.google.com. Navigate to "VPC network" and select "VPC networks". In the network list, click the legacy network under the "Name" column. Click "DELETE VPC NETWORK" and confirm the deletion
CQL: VPCNetwork should not have subnetworks isEmpty()

Policy: Subnets should enable private google access
Description: Private Google Access enables the virtual machines in a subnet to access Google APIs and services using an internal IP address rather than an external IP address. It is a best practice to enable Private Google Access on a subnet.
Recommendation: Login into GCP console: https://console.cloud.google.com. Navigate to "VPC network" and select "VPC networks". In the network list, click the subnet under "Subnets" column. Under subnet details page, click EDIT. Set "Private Google access" to "On" and save the configuration
CQL: Subnet should have privateIpGoogleAccess = true

Policy: Subnets should enable flow logs
Description: VPC Flow Logs record a sample of network flows sent from and received by VM instances. These logs can be used for network monitoring, forensics, real-time security analysis, and expense optimization. It is a security best practice to enable VPC flow logs.
Recommendation: Login into GCP console: https://console.cloud.google.com. Navigate to "VPC network" and select "VPC networks". In the network list, click the subnet under "Subnets" column. Under subnet details page, click EDIT. Set "Flow Logs" to "On" and save the configuration
CQL: Subnet should have enableFlowLogs = true

Policy: VM Instances in the default network
Description: Every GCP project comes with a default network with pre-populated firewall rules. A default network is suitable for getting started quickly, and for launching public instances for simple websites. But, if you need to host a complex multi-tier application or add more layers of security to your infrastructure it is a best practice to create non-default network with public, private subnets & demilitarized (DMZ) zones. This segregates the network based on their functionality, services, and security.
Recommendation: Login into GCP console: https://console.cloud.google.com. Navigate to "Compute Engine" and choose "VM Instances". Select the VM Instance reported under violation, and Stop the VM Instance. Navigate to "Compute Engine" and choose "Snapshots". Create a snapshot by selecting "Source disk" as that of the VM Instance. Launch new VM instance using the snapshot, and place it in the non-default network. Follow the instructions documented at https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/instances/create-start-instance#createsnapshot. Delete the VM Instance in the default network and also the snapshot
CQL: VMInstance should not have networkInterfaces with [network like '%default']

Policy: Cryptographic key should have rotation age less than 90 days
Description: Cloud KMS provides a facility to encrypt cloud services using symmetric or asymmetric keys. It is a best practice to rotate keys to limit the amount of data protected by a single key, and also to meet the compliance standards.
Recommendation: Login into GCP console: https://console.cloud.google.com. Navigate to "IAM & admin" and choose "Cryptographic Keys". Click on Name of the Keyring where the key is present. For a specific key, click more options button (three vertical dots) and select "Edit rotation period". On Edit rotation period pop-up, set the period to 90 days and save the configuration
CQL: CryptographicKey should have keyAge <= 90

Policy: BigTable should have Labels
Description: Labels are a lightweight way to group resources together that are related to or associated with each other. It is a best practice to label cloud resources to better organize and gain visibility into their usage.
Recommendation: Login into GCP console: https://console.cloud.google.com. Choose "Bigtable". Select the Bigtable instance reported as violation and click on "Labels". Add appropriate label by clicking the "Add Label" button
CQL: BigTableInstance should not have labels isEmpty()

Policy: Cloud Function should only be allowed access to VPC resources
Description: Cloud Functions may require to connect directly to Compute Engine VM instances, Memorystore instances, Cloud SQL instances, and any other resources. It is a best practice to send requests to these resources using an internal IP address by connecting to VPC network using "Serverless VPC Access" configuration.
Recommendation: Login into GCP console: https://console.cloud.google.com. Choose "Cloud Functions". Click the Cloud Function reported as violation and click on "Edit". Click on "Environment variables, networking, timeouts and more". Select the VPC connector from the drop down or create a new one and then use it
CQL: CloudFunction should not have (network isEmpty() and vpcConnector isEmpty())

Policy: Cloud Function should not be triggered by HTTP triggers
Description: Cloud Functions configured to execute by HTTP triggers allow unauthenticated invocation by default. And it demands additional security controls for authentication like using authorization tokens or API gateways etc. As a best practice, wherever possible, avoid configuring cloud functions with HTTP triggers, instead set them with background triggers.
Recommendation: It is not possible to change the trigger type of a function once it is created. The only option is to create a new function with background trigger and delete the old function. Login into GCP console: https://console.cloud.google.com. Choose "Cloud Functions". Create a new Cloud Function triggered by a background trigger and executes the same source as the Cloud Function reported as violation. Click the Cloud Function reported as violation and click on "Delete"
CQL: CloudFunction should not have httpsTrigger isNotEmpty()

Policy: Cloud Function should have Labels
Description: Labels are a lightweight way to group resources together that are related to or associated with each other. It is a best practice to label cloud resources to better organize and gain visibility into their usage.
Recommendation: Login into GCP console: https://console.cloud.google.com. Choose "Cloud Functions". Select the Cloud Function reported as violation and click on "Labels". Add appropriate label by clicking the "Add Label" button
CQL: CloudFunction should have labels isNotEmpty()

Policy: BigQuery Dataset that is not encrypted with CMK
Description: BigQuery automatically encrypts all data before it is written to disk.  By default, Google manages the encryption keys used to protect the data. As a best practice for more sensitive and confidential data, use Customer Managed Key to encrpt the data.
Recommendation: Login into GCP console: https://console.cloud.google.com. Choose "BigQuery". Create a new BigQuery dataset with encryption type as "Customer-managed key", and select the appropriate key managed in Cloud Key Management service. Copy the dataset reported as a violation to the newly created dataset using the steps documented under the following link https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/copying-datasets. Delete the dataset reported as a violation
CQL: BigQueryDataset should not have cmkEncryptionConfiguration isEmpty()

Policy: IAM users that are assigned 'BigQuery Admin' or 'BigQuery Data Owner' or 'BigQuery Data Editor' roles
Description: The pre-defined roles like "BigQuery Admin" or "BigQuery Data Owner" or "BigQuery Data Editor" grant full permissions over Big Query resources and its data. As a best practice, avoid granting access to these roles at the project level; instead, grant specific Big Query related permissions to IAM members.
Recommendation: Login into GCP console: https://console.cloud.google.com. Navigate to "IAM & admin" and choose "IAM". Click on filter table text bar, set the filter as "Type: User". Ensure no user is assigned with "BigQuery Admin" or "BigQuery Data Owner" or "BigQuery Data Editor" role at the project level
CQL: IAMUser should not have roles contain-any ('roles/bigquery.admin', 'roles/bigquery.dataOwner', 'roles/bigquery.dataEditor')

Policy: IAM groups that are assigned 'BigQuery Admin' or 'BigQuery Data Owner' or 'BigQuery Data Editor' roles
Description: The pre-defined roles like "BigQuery Admin" or "BigQuery Data Owner" or "BigQuery Data Editor" grant full permissions over Big Query resources and its data. As a best practice, avoid granting access to these roles at the project level; instead, grant specific Big Query related permissions to IAM members.
Recommendation: Login into GCP console: https://console.cloud.google.com. Navigate to "IAM & admin" and choose "IAM". Click on filter table text bar, set the filter as "Type: Group". Ensure no group is assigned with "BigQuery Admin" or "BigQuery Data Owner" or "BigQuery Data Editor" role at the project level
CQL: IAMGroup should not have roles contain-any ('roles/bigquery.admin', 'roles/bigquery.dataOwner', 'roles/bigquery.dataEditor')

Policy: BigQuery Dataset that are accessible to all authenticated users
Description: Dataset is the lowest level resource that supports access controls in BigQuery. Dataset-level permissions determine the users, groups, and service accounts allowed to access the tables, views, and table data in a specific dataset. It is recommended to grant limited access to BigQuery datasets for specific authenticated users.
Recommendation: Login into GCP console: https://console.cloud.google.com. Choose "BigQuery". Select the BigQuery dataset reported as violation under the Project name and click "Share Dataset". Click on each role to view list of associated members. Delete member with name "allAuthenticatedUsers"
CQL: BigQueryDataset should not have accessingEntities with [specialGroup='allAuthenticatedUsers']

Policy: BigQuery Dataset that are accessible to all users
Description: Dataset is the lowest level resource that supports access controls in BigQuery. Dataset-level permissions determine the users, groups, and service accounts allowed to access the tables, views, and table data in a specific dataset. It is recommended to grant limited access to BigQuery datasets for specific authenticated users.
Recommendation: Login into GCP console: https://console.cloud.google.com. Choose "BigQuery". Select the BigQuery dataset reported as violation under the Project name and click "Share Dataset". Click on each role to view list of associated members. Delete member with name "allUsers"
CQL: BigQueryDataset should not have accessingEntities with [iamMember='allUsers']

Policy: BigQuery Dataset should have Labels
Description: Labels are a lightweight way to group resources together that are related to or associated with each other. It is a best practice to label cloud resources to better organize and gain visibility into their usage.
Recommendation: Login into GCP console: https://console.cloud.google.com. Choose "BigQuery". Select the BigQuery dataset reported as violation under the Project name. Add appropriate label by clicking the edit icon associated with "Labels"
CQL: BigQueryDataset should not have labels isEmpty()

Policy: IAM users that are assigned 'BigTable Administrator' role
Description: The pre-defined role "Bigtable Administrator" grants full permission over Bigtable resources and its data. As a best practice, avoid giving access to this role at the project level; instead, grant specific Bigtable related permissions to IAM members.
Recommendation: Login into GCP console: https://console.cloud.google.com. Navigate to "IAM & admin" and choose "IAM". Click on filter table text bar, set the filter as "Type: User". Ensure no user is assigned with "Bigtable Administrator" role at the project level
CQL: IAMUser should not have roles contain-any ('roles/bigtable.admin')

Policy: IAM groups that are assigned 'BigTable Administrator' role
Description: The pre-defined role "Bigtable Administrator" grants full permission over Bigtable resources and its data. As a best practice, avoid giving access to this role at the project level; instead, grant specific Bigtable related permissions to IAM members.
Recommendation: Login into GCP console: https://console.cloud.google.com. Navigate to "IAM & admin" and choose "IAM". Click on filter table text bar, set the filter as "Type: Group". Ensure no group is assigned with "Bigtable Administrator" role at the project level
CQL: IAMGroup should not have roles contain-any ('roles/bigtable.admin')

Policy: Cloud Function that are accessible to all users
Description: Using Cloud Identity and Access Management(IAM), you can control access to the cloud functions. As a security best practice, ensure the access is not allowed to "allUsers" to avoid data leaks and other security risks.
Recommendation: Login into GCP console: https://console.cloud.google.com. Choose "Cloud Functions".". Select the checkbox next to cloud function, and click "Show Info Panel" to show the Permissions tab. Remove the access granted to "allUsers", and grant access to specific IAM Members
CQL: CloudFunction should not have policyBindings with [members contain-any ('allUsers')]

Policy: Cloud Function that are accessible to all authenticated users
Description: Using Cloud Identity and Access Management(IAM), you can control access to the cloud functions. As a security best practice, ensure the access is not allowed to "allAuthenticatedUsers" to avoid data leaks and other security risks.
Recommendation: Login into GCP console: https://console.cloud.google.com. Choose "Cloud Functions".". Select the checkbox next to cloud function, and click "Show Info Panel" to show the Permissions tab. Remove the access granted to "allAuthenticatedUsers", and grant access to specific IAM Members
CQL: CloudFunction should not have policyBindings with [members contain-any ('allAuthenticatedUsers')]

Policy: VM instances exposed to public
Description: Allowing access from all IP addresses on the Internet to Virtual Machines is risky as it can lead to Brute Force or DoS attacks. It is a best practice to follow the principle of least privilege, and allow access to only required ports.
Recommendation: Login into GCP console - https://console.cloud.google.com. Navigate to "Compute Engine" and choose "VM Instances". Select the VM instance reported under violation and click "View details" under Network interfaces. Identify the Firewall rule allowing traffic from 0.0.0.0/0 and replace the rule with specific source IP address or delete the rule
CQL: VMInstance should not have isPublic=true

Policy: Pub/Sub Topic not encrypted using CMEK
Description: Pub/Sub is a fully-managed real-time messaging service that allows you to send and receive messages between independent applications. A publisher application pushes the message to a topic, and the subscriber application subscribes to the topic to receive the messages. As a security best practice, protect the highly sensitive messages by enabling encryption for the topic using CMEK (Customer-Managed Encryption Keys).
Recommendation: You can enable encryption for topics only at the time of its creation. Login into GCP console - https://console.cloud.google.com. Navigate to "Pub/Sub" and choose "Topics". Create the new Pub/Sub Topic by selecting the option "Customer-managed key" under the Encryption section. Update the Subscriptions of the old topic to use the newly created topic
CQL: PubSubTopic should not have encryptionKmsKeyName isEmpty()

Policy: Pub/Sub Topic that allow anonymous/public access from external sources.
Description: Cloud IAM policy governs the access permissions to pub/sub topics. Granting anonymous or public access to pub/sub topics is risky if you are storing any sensitive messages. As a best practice, limit the access to specific authenticated users or groups or service accounts.
Recommendation: Login into GCP console - https://console.cloud.google.com. Navigate to "Pub/Sub" and choose "Topics". Select the topic reported as a violation. Under right side panel, select the tab "Permissions". Remove the access granted to IAM members "allUsers" or "allAuthenticatedUsers" for any role
CQL: PubSubTopic should not have policyBindings with [members contain-any ('allUsers', 'allAuthenticatedUsers')]

Policy: Pub/Sub Topic should have labels
Description: Labels are a lightweight way to group resources together that are related to or associated with each other. It is a best practice to label cloud resources to better organize and gain visibility into their usage.
Recommendation: Login into GCP console - https://console.cloud.google.com. Navigate to "Pub/Sub" and choose "Topics". Select the topic reported as a violation. Under right side panel, select the tab "Labels". Click the "+Add Label" button, type the appropriate key and value and save the configuration
CQL: PubSubTopic should not have labels isEmpty()

Policy: IAM users that are assigned 'Pub/Sub Admin' role
Description: The pre-defined role "Pub/Sub Admin" grants full access over Pub/Sub topics, subscriptions, and snapshots. As a best practice, avoid granting access to Pub/Sub Admin roles at the project level; instead, grant specific Pub/Sub permissions to IAM members.
Recommendation: Login into GCP console - https://console.cloud.google.com. Navigate to "IAM & admin" and choose "IAM". Click on filter table text bar, set the filter as "Type: User". Ensure no user is assigned with "Pub/Sub Admin" role at the project level
CQL: IAMUser should not have roles contain-any ('roles/pubsub.admin')

Policy: IAM groups that are assigned 'Pub/Sub Admin' role
Description: The pre-defined role "Pub/Sub Admin" grants full access over Pub/Sub topics, subscriptions, and snapshots. As a best practice, avoid granting access to Pub/Sub Admin roles at the project level; instead, grant specific Pub/Sub permissions to IAM members.
Recommendation: Login into GCP console - https://console.cloud.google.com. Navigate to "IAM & admin" and choose "IAM". Click on filter table text bar, set the filter as "Type: Group". Ensure no group is assigned with "Pub/Sub Admin" role at the project level
CQL: IAMGroup should not have roles contain-any ('roles/pubsub.admin')

Policy: Storage Buckets not encrypted using CMEK
Description: As an additional layer on top of Google-managed encryption keys, you can choose to use keys generated by Key Management Service. Such keys are known as customer-managed encryption keys. If you use a customer-managed encryption key, your encryption keys are stored within KMS. It is a security best practice to encrypt storage buckets using customer-managed encryption keys.
Recommendation: Login into GCP console - https://console.cloud.google.com. Choose "Storage". Select the bucket reported as a violation, click edit icon. Select a customer-managed key and save the configuration
CQL: StorageBucket should have kmsEncryptionName isNotEmpty()

Policy: Ensure VM Instances enable Shielded Boot
Description: Secure Boot ensures the virtual machines run only authentic software by verifying the signature of all boot components, and halting the boot process if signature verification fails. This helps to prevent any kernel malware from persisting across VM reboots. As a best practice enable secure boot for desired virtual machines.
Recommendation: Login into GCP console - https://console.cloud.google.com. Navigate to "Compute Engine" and choose "VM Instances". Select the VM instance reported under violation and click "EDIT". Under section "Shielded VM", select "Turn on Secure Boot", "Turn on vTPM", "Turn on Integrity Monitoring" and Save the configuration
CQL: VMInstance should have integrityMonitoringEnabled=true and secureBootEnabled=true and vtpmEnabled=true

Policy: Ensure no static IPs are unused
Description: Any external static IP address that is not associated with a VM instance is a potential threat for misuse, and also can incur unnecessary cost. As a best practice, ensure you remove the un-associated static IP addresses.
Recommendation: Login into GCP console - https://console.cloud.google.com. Navigate to "VPC Network" and choose "External IP Address". Any static IP address that has "In use by" column as "None", associate to a VM instance or Load Balancer, or release the IP address
CQL: StaticIP should have status = 'IN_USE'

Policy: Ensure Compute Images are not publicly accessible
Description: Granting public access to the compute image allows unrestricted access from any user or machine on the internet. It can result in data leaks and malicious attacks. As a security best practice, and to meet the compliance guidelines, ensure the compute images are not publicly accessible.
Recommendation: Login into GCP console - https://console.cloud.google.com. Navigate to "Compute Engine" and choose "Images". Select the desired image and click "Show Info Panel" to see the "Permissions" tab. Under the "Permissions" tab, remove the access granted to "allUsers" and "allAuthenticatedUsers", instead grant access to specific IAM members
CQL: ComputeImage should not have iamPolicyBindings with [members contain-any ('allUsers', 'allAuthenticatedUsers')]

Policy: Ensure Compute Images are Encrypted using Customer Managed or Supplied Keys
Description: Compute Images are encrypted by default using the Google-managed encryption keys. However, for highly sensitive images and more control over the encryption and decryption process, use either customer-managed keys or customer-supplied keys for encryption.
Recommendation: There is no option to change the encryption of the existing compute image. You can create a new custom image by selecting either customer-managed or customer-supplied keys. Create a new custom image by choosing the "Source" as "image", and "Source image" as the image reported as a violation. Under Encryption section, select either "Customer-managed key" or "Customer-supplied key", and input the appropriate key details. Click "Create"
CQL: ComputeImage should not have imageEncryptionKey isEmpty()

Policy: Ensure Compute Images have Labels
Description: Labels are a lightweight way to group resources together that are related to or associated with each other. It is a best practice to label cloud resources to better organize and gain visibility into their usage.
Recommendation: Login into GCP console: https://console.cloud.google.com. Navigate to "Compute Engine" and choose "Images". Select the desired image and click "Show Info Panel", and select the tab "Labels". Add appropriate label by clicking the "Add Label" button
CQL: ComputeImage should not have labels isEmpty()

Policy: Ensure SQL Instances have Labels
Description: Labels are a lightweight way to group resources together that are related to or associated with each other. It is a best practice to label cloud resources to better organize and gain visibility into their usage.
Recommendation: Login into GCP console: https://console.cloud.google.com. Navigate to "SQL". Select the desired instance and click "Show Info Panel", and select the tab "Labels". Add appropriate label by clicking the "Add Label" button
CQL: SQLInstance should not have labels isEmpty()

Policy: Ensure Kubernetes Clusters enable Application-layer Secrets Encryption using Customer-managed key
Description: By default, the Google Kubernetes Engine encrypts sensitive data stored in the etcd. However, for more sensitive data and granular control over the encryption and decryption process, enable Application-layer Secrets Encryption and protect the data at rest using customer-managed keys.
Recommendation: Login into GCP Console - https://console.cloud.google.com/. Navigate to "Kubernetes Engine" and select "Clusters". Select the cluster, click "EDIT", set "Application-layer Secrets Encryption" to "Enabled" state, and select the desired customer-managed key. Click "Save"
CQL: KBECluster should have databaseEncryptionState = 'ENCRYPTED' and databaseEncryptionKeyName isNotEmpty()

Policy: Ensure Dataproc clusters are encrypted using Customer-managed keys
Description: Dataproc clusters are encrypted by default using the Google-managed encryption keys. However, for more sensitive data and granular control over the encryption and decryption process, use customer-managed keys for encryption.
Recommendation: There is no option to change the encryption of the existing Dataproc cluster. You can create a new Dataproc cluster by selecting Customer-managed keys for encryption
CQL: DataprocCluster should not have encryptionKmsKeyName isEmpty()

Policy: Ensure Dataproc Clusters have Labels
Description: Labels are a lightweight way to group resources together that are related to or associated with each other. It is a best practice to label cloud resources to better organize and gain visibility into their usage.
Recommendation: Login into GCP console: https://console.cloud.google.com. Navigate to "DataProc" and choose "Clusters". Select the desired cluster and click "Show Info Panel", and select the tab "Labels". Add appropriate label by clicking the "Add Label" button
CQL: DataprocCluster should not have labels isEmpty()
